• The model explicitly incorporates the cost of debt capital. 
• The model can be used when cash flows are unpredictable. 
• There is less of an impact arising from the uncertainty in forecasting terminal 
value.

questions.question_text: Based on the information in Exhibit 1, Armishaw’s estimate of VFG’s residual income per share for 2015 is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Residual income for 2015 = Net income (2015) – Equity charge at the beginning of 2015 (all amounts in millions except per share data) 2014 2015 Growth rate 14% 12.50% 2015 growth: 14% – (14% – 5%)/6 years Net income: $422 in 2013 $481.08 $541.22 Prior year × (1 + g): g = 14% and 12.5%, respectively Less Dividends 192.43 216.49 40% × Net income Increase in retained earnings $288.65 $324.73 Starting equity $2,153.00 $2,441.65 Start of 2014: $1,075 + $1,078 Increase in retained earnings 288.65 324.73 Ending equity $2,441.65 $2,766.38 Net income $541.22 From above Less equity charge 366.24 Starting equity (2,441.65) × 15% Residual income $174.98 Residual income per share $1.17 Divide by 150 (million shares)

questions.question_text: Using the information in Exhibit 2, comparing Armishaw’s approach to terminal value to Stack’s approach, Stack’s assumption leads to a 2024 value that is approximately:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Armishaw’s assumption VT = 5.32/0.15 = $35.47 Perpetuity of $5.32 per year starting in 2024 Stack’s assumption Estimated 2024 growth rate 0.20 × 0.60 = 0.12 ROE2023 × Retention ratio Residual income in 2024 5.32 × 1.12 = 5.96 RI2024 = RI2023 × (1 + 0.12) Terminal value 2024 5.96/(1 + 0.15 – 0.50) = $9.17 VT = RI2024/(1 + r – ω) Difference in VT 9.17 – 35.47 = –26.30 Stack’s vs. Armishaw’s assumptions Difference in PV(VT) –26.30/(1.1510) = –$6.50 Stack’s estimate will be $6.50 lower Where r = required return on equity; ω = persistence factor for residual income; VT = terminal value at forecast horizon.

questions.question_text: The least appropriate justification that Stack makes in support of the use of the residual income model is Statement:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The residual income model uses accounting income estimates and assumes that the cost of debt capital is properly reflected by interest expense, but because of changing market conditions interest expense may not be a good proxy for the company’s cost of debt capital.

vignettes.topic: Residual Income Valuation - Vignette Questions 39-42
vignettes.case_study_text:
Woojae Park and Shelly Sonberg, junior analysts at Felix & Danick are valuing Sparrow 
Telecom Company (Sparrow) a regional telecom provider. Their supervisor instructed 
them to use residual income models for the valuation.  
 
Sonberg starts the meeting by saying she wants to be sure Park understands the 
difference between the residual income approach and the dividend discount approach 
and makes the following statements: 
**Statement 1:**
If there is clean surplus accounting, the residual income model and 
the dividend discount model will yield the same results. 
**Statement 2:**
The residual income model has more of its value determined in the 
current time period, whereas the dividend discount model has more 
of its value determined in later periods. 
**Statement 3:**
The residual income model makes assumptions about future earnings 
and dividend growth, whereas the dividend discount model makes 
assumptions only about dividend growth. 
 
To start their analysis, the analysts gather the information in Exhibit 1 for Sparrow and 
calculate the forecasted residual income for the current fiscal year ending 31 August.  
 
**Exhibit 1:**
Forecasted Residual Income 
  Per share 
| Year | Forecasted Residual Income Per Share | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Book value per share 31 August previous** | year | $47.50 |
| **Forecasted earnings for year-end 31 August current** | year | $7.58 |
| **Forecasted dividends for current fiscal** | year | $2.10 |
| **Book value per share 31 August current fiscal** | year | $52.98 |
| **Required rate of return =** | re | 13% |
| **Long-term growth** | rate | 6% |
| **Return on equity** | (ROE) | 15% |

They continue by selecting the single -stage residual income model to calculate the intrinsic value of the Sparrow. Next, the analysts decide to calculate the economic value added (EVA) for Sparrow and gather some additional data (Exhibit 2):
 
**Exhibit 2:**
Sparrow Telecom Company Additional Information (US$, thousands) 
Total debt, 31 August previous fiscal year $4,391,600 
Total equity, 31 August previous fiscal year $2,151,750 
Earnings before interest and taxes $616,000 
| Item | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Tax rate** | 20% |
| **Weighted average cost of capital (WACC)** | 7.0% |
| **Cost of equity re** | 13% |

questions.question_text: In Sonberg’s comparison of the residual income and dividend discount models, which of her statements is least accurate?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Statement 3 is the least accurate. The residual income model does not make assumptions about future earnings and dividend growth.

questions.question_text: Using Exhibit 1, the forecasted residual income per share for current fiscal year is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Using Exhibit 1, residual income per share is calculated as follows: Step 1: Book value per share beginning of period (31 August previous year): $47.50 Step 2: Multiply beginning book value per share by cost of equity 13% Per share equity charge $6.18 Step 3: Earnings per share forecast $7.58 Less per share equity charge –$6,18 Per share residual income $1.41

questions.question_text: Using Exhibit 1 and the single -stage residual income model, the intrinsic value of Sparrow is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The single-stage residual income model is: 𝐵0 + 𝑅𝑂𝐸−𝑟 𝑟−𝑔 × 𝐵0 Where: B0 = book value per share at the beginning of the period, see calculation below ROE = return on equity: 15% g = long-term growth rate: 6% re = cost of equity Book value per share (31 August previous fiscal year) = $47.50 per share 𝑃 = 47.50 + .15−.13 .13−.06 × 47.50 = 61.07

questions.question_text: Using Exhibit 2, the EVA for Sparrow is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. EVA = NOPAT – (C% × TC) Where: NOPAT = Net operating profit after tax: $616,000 × (1 – 0.20) = $492,800 C% = Cost of capital (WACC): 7.0% TC = Total capital: $4,391,600 + $2,151,750 = $6,543,350 EVA = 492,800 – (0.070 × 6,543,350) = 492,800 – 458,035 = $34,765

vignettes.topic: Residual Income Valuation - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following data for a company: Tax rate 25% Cost of debt before tax 6% Cost of equity 10% Debt financing $4,000,000 Equity financing $6,000,000 Net operating profit after tax $750,000 The company's residual income is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because in this second approach to calculating residual income, which takes the perspective of all providers of capital (both debt and equity), a capital charge (the company’s total cost of capital in money terms) is subtracted from the company’s after-tax operating profit. Since after-tax operating profit is given, the formula for the total capital charge is: equity charge + debt charge. In this example the equity charge = cost of equity × equity financing = 0.10 × $6,000,000 = $600,000. The debt charge is = cost of debt (1 – tax rate) × debt financing = 0.06 × (1 – 0.25) × $400,000 = $180,000. The total capital charge = $600,000 + $180,000 = $780,000. Therefore the residual income is equal to $750,000 – $780,000 = – $30,000.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following data for a company: Book value per share € 50 Long-term growth rate 10% Long-term ROE 16% Cost of equity 12% Using the single -stage residual income model, the intrinsic value of the company shares is:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the formula for the single-stage residual income model is: V0 = B0 + (ROE – r)/(r-g) ×B0. In this example V 0 = 50 + (.016 – 0.12)/(0.12 – 0.10) × 50 = 50 + 100 = €150.

questions.question_text: The least likely use of a residual income model is to determine:
options: A. the value of a stock index.
B. if a company is creating value.
C. the opportunity cost of a company's equity capital.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a residual income model does not determine the opportunity cost of a company's equity capital. The model requires knowing the opportunity cost in order to calculate the residual income. Conceptually, residual income is net income less a charge (de duction) for common shareholders’ opportunity cost in generating net income.

questions.question_text: An analyst believes a company with variable cash flows has been smoothing out earnings and its reported interest expense understates its marginal cost of capital. Which of the following factors best supports using a residual income model for valuation?
options: B. The variability of cash flows
C. The low marginal cost of capital
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the residual income (RI) models rely on accounting data and can be readily applied to companies that do not have positive expected near -term free cash flows. The models can be used when cash flows are unpredictable. The cash flows for the company in this scenario are variable (unpredictable), which would favor a RI model over a FCFE model.

questions.question_text: In a single-stage residual income model, the estimated value of a company is the:
options: A. replacement cost of the company's total assets.
B. present value of all the company's future dividends.
C. company's book value plus the present value of its expected economic profits.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because under the single -stage residual income model, the estimated value of a share is the book value per share (B 0) plus the present value [(ROE − r)B0/(r − g)] of the expected stream of residual income. The second term is the present value of the company’s expected economic profits.

questions.question_text: Compared to the dividend discount model, the residual income model:
options: A. is based on discounting future cash flows.
B. tends to be more sensitive to terminal value estimation.
C. attributes most of a stock's value to earnings from earlier periods.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because in the residual income (RI) model, most of the stock’s total value is attributed to the earnings from the earlier periods. In contrast, in the dividend discount model (DDM), value is derived from the receipt of dividends, and typically, a smaller proportion of value is attributed to the earlier periods.

questions.question_text: If a company aggressively capitalizes an expenditure in the current year rather than expense it, it most likely leads to:
options: A. an increase in its future earnings.
B. a decrease in its current return on equity.
C. an overstatement of the company's value in the residual income model.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because aggressive accounting choices will lead to lower reported future earnings, consider an example in which a company chooses to capitalize an expenditure in the current year rather than expense it. Doing so overstates current-year earnings as well as current book value. If an analyst uses current earnings (or ROE) naively in predicting future residual earnings, the RI model will overestimate the company’s value.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Year 1 Year 2 Cost of equity 8.50% --- --- Book value per share at beginning of Year 1 € 25.50 --- --- P/B ratio at end of Year 2 1.5× --- --- EPS --- € 5.10 € 5.87 Dividend per share --- € 1.25 € 1.50 Using a multi-stage residual income model, the per share value of the company's stock is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the share value = B0 + Present value (PV) of the Residual income (RI) + Present value of the Residual terminal value. Step 1: Calculate the PV of the Residual income (RI) Residual income = Net income per share – (cost of equity × beginning BV) Year 1 Year 2 Beginning book value € 25.50 € 29.35 plus net income 5.10 5.87 less dividends -1.25 -1.5 Ending book value € 29.35 € 33.72 Residual income (RI) calculation 5.10 – (0.085 × 25.50) 5.87 – (0.085 × 29.35) Residual income € 2.93 € 3.38 Present value of RI at cost of equity 2.93/(1 + 0.085)^1 3.38/(1 + 0.085)^2 € 2.70 € 2.87 Total present value of the RI = €2.70 + €2.87 = €5.57 Step 2: Calculate the PV of the residual terminal value (PT – BT) Terminal market price of share (PT) at end of year 2: 1.5 × €33.72 = €50.58 Terminal residual value = PT – BT = €50.58 – €33.72 = €16.86 PV of (PT – BT) for 2 years at cost of equity: 16.86/(1+0.85)^2 = €14.32 Step 3: Calculate the value per share: Current book value + PV of 2 years RI + PV Residual terminal value €25.50 + €5.57 + €14.32 = €45.39 ≈ €45.00

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about three companies: ROE Cost of Equity WACC Company 1 9% 10% 8% Company 2 11% 11% 9% Company 3 12% 10% 10% Based on the residual income model and the above information only, the intrinsic value will be greater than the current book value per share for:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because intrinsic value will be greater than book value when the cost of equity is less than the return on equity. If ROE was lower than the cost of equity, the company would have negative residual income and be valued at less than book value. Therefore, if ROE exceeds the cost of equity, residual income will be positive and intrinsic value will be greater than book value.

questions.question_text: Under the residual income valuation model, the premium over book value at the end of the forecast period will most likely be close to zero when the forecast period is:
options: A. short and competition is low.
B. short and competition is high.
C. long and competition is high.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because, as ROE has been found to revert to mean levels over time and may decline to the cost of equity in a competitive environment, residual income approaches often model ROE fading toward the cost of equity. As ROE approaches the cost of equity, residual income approaches zero. Analysts make a variety of assumptions concerning continuing residual income. Frequently, one of the following assumptions is made: • residual income continues indefinitely at a positive level; • residual income is zero from the terminal year forward; • residual income declines to zero as ROE reverts to the cost of equity through time; or • residual income reflects the reversion of ROE to some mean level. One finite-horizon model of residual income valuation assumes that, at the end of time horizon T, a certain premium over book value ( PT − BT) exists for the company, in which case current value equals the following (Bauman, 1999): V0 = B0 + Σt [(Et − rBt−1)/(1 + r)t] + (PT − BT)/(1 + r)T, t = 1, ..., T Alternatively, V0 = B0 + Σt [(ROEt − r)Bt−1/(1 + r)t] + (PT − BT)/(1 + r)T, t = 1, ..., T The last component in both specifications represents the premium over book value at the end of the forecast horizon. The longer the forecast period, the greater the chance that the company’s residual income will converge to zero. For long forecast periods, this last term may be treated as zero. For shorter forecast periods, a forecast of the premium should be calculated.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following per-share data for a company with a required rate of return on equity of 10%: Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Beginning book value (Bt–1) € 10.00 € 11.00 € 13.00 Net income (EPSt) € 2.00 € 3.50 € 4.00 Dividends paid (Dt) € 1.00 € 1.50 € 2.00 Using the residual income model and assuming residual income beyond Year 3 is equal to zero, the intrinsic value of the stock is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the intrinsic value of the company's shares is equal to the sum of the current book value and the present value of future residual income: V0 = B0 + Σt [RIt]/(1 + r)t = B0 + Σt [Et − rBt−1]/(1 + r)t, t = 1, ..., ∞. In this case, the residual income for Year 1 is the net income per share of €2.00 less the per-share equity charge; €2.00 – (€10.00 × 10%) = €1.00. Residual income for Year 2 = €3.50 – (€11.00 × 10%) = €2.40. Residual income for Year 3 = €4.00 – (€13.00 × 10%) = €2.70. Therefore, V0 = €10.00 + €1.00/(1.1) + €2.40/(1.1)2 + €2.70/(1.1)3 ≈ €14.92.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Current stock price $24.50 Current book value per share $10.50 Cost of equity 7.20% Long-term ROE 11.00% Using a single-stage residual income model, the implied long-term growth rate in the company's residual income is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the implied long -term growth rate in the residual income is calculated using: P0 = B0 + [(ROE – r)/(r – g)] × B0, where B0 is the current book value per share, ROE is the return on equity, r is the cost of equity, and g is the implied growth rate of residual income. This implies: g = r – (ROE – r)/[(P0 – B0)/B0] = 0.072 – (0.11 – 0.072)/[($24.50 – $10.50)/$10.50] = 4.35%

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 1-5
vignettes.case_study_text:
Ulrich Schwalke has been recently hired as an analyst at a private equity firm that 
specializes in buying and restructuring private companies to be taken public within five 
years. Given his background with valuing public firms, this role will provide him h is first 
experiences in valuing private companies. 
 
Before starting his new position, Schwalke meets with a former classmate who works as 
an associate focused on private company valuations in order to resolve legal disputes. 
During the meeting, Schwalke’s classmate mentions that private business valuation often 
requires normalizing certain expense items on a company’s income statement before 
taking next steps. 
 
On his first assignment, Schwalke is asked to estimate a WACC for a potential private 
target company. The partner has commented that the private target has a far lower debt 
ratio than would be considered optimal. 
 
Schwalke’s firm recently announced plans to buy one of the private companies that 
Schwalke has valued. Schwalke spent considerable time assessing the validity of 
different control premiums in analyzing a possible offer price.

questions.question_text: Which valuation feature will Schwalke find different in valuing private companies versus public companies?
options: A. Using FCFF to value companies
B. Using market multiples to value companies
C. Assessing discounts to account for illiquidity
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. An issue with private versus public company valuations is the need to adjust the valuation downward to account for a lack of liquidity. A and B are both incorrect because FCFF and multiples are used in both private and public company valuations.

questions.question_text: During Schwalke’s meeting with his former classmate, they discuss how their approaches to private company valuation vary given the different uses of their analysis. Which of the following best characterizes how Schwalke’s approach differs from that of his former classmate?
options: A. Schwalke usually incorporates a DLOM.
B. Schwalke usually adjusts the investment value as a minority interest.
C. Schwalke’s approach usually considers a synergistic control premium.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. As Schwalke’s firm specializes in buying and restructuring private companies to be taken public, as a strategic buyer it will consider a control premium. Choices A and B are inconsistent with his firm’s strategy of controlling and restructuring companies over a five-year period.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best describes the meaning of “normalizing earnings” in the context of private business valuation?
options: A. Adjustments to revenues and/or costs necessary to allow comparison of private
B. Adjustments to offset the cyclicality of revenues and/or costs for private
C. Adjustments that allow comparisons due to the lack of marketability for private
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Private companies, especially when a controlling owner also serves as a senior manager, may engage in economic transactions such as non -market-based market compensation that distorts earnings versus comparable public companies. Cyclicality is a factor that may need to be adjusted in public companies as well, while lack of marketability should not affect earnings.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes a possible bias in the WACC of the private target with a suboptimal debt ratio?
options: A. Private companies are likely to have WACC estimates below their optimal WACC
B. Private companies are likely to have WACC estimates above their optimal WACC
C. Private companies are likely to have WACC estimates above their optimal WACC
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Recall the formula for WACC: rWACC = wdrd + were First, a higher equity weight implies a lower debt weight, as these proportions combined must equal one. Also, re > rd, as equity is riskier than debt. Therefore, as wd falls, the WACC increases, approaching re as the debt ratio approaches zero. A suboptimal debt ratio translates to a higher than optimal WACC. A is incorrect because a lower debt ratio does not reduce WACC. C is incorrect because a higher debt weight likely lowers the WACC.

questions.question_text: Schwalke learns that his firm intends to combine the new target company with an existing portfolio company prior to taking it public. Should Schwalke apply a financial or synergistic control premium, and how does this level of control premium compare to the other?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. As Schwalke’s firm seeks to realize synergies from the business combination of the target and existing portfolio company, it is likely to consider a synergistic premium which exceeds that of a financial buyer.

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 6-10
vignettes.case_study_text:
Ulrich Schwalke continues his work in valuing private companies, taking specific interest 
in transactions involving public companies buying private company targets. As he has seen 
in his work, private company discount rates are often biased because private  firms 
typically have less access to debt capital. 
 
While Schwalke has experience using CAPM for public companies, he has rarely used it 
for private firms, instead relying on the expanded CAPM or a build -up approach to 
estimate required return on equity. When using the expanded CAPM for a private 
company, J NK Corporation, Schwalke gathered beta estimates from publicly traded 
comparable companies. On a recent engagement, he found the average beta from public 
comparables of 1.20. The average debt ratio of the public comparables exceeded that of 
JNK, while tax rates were equal between the public comparables and JNK. 
 
Continuing in his role, Schwalke completed many private company valuations for entire 
businesses. As a result, certain methods of calculating terminal values seemed to be more 
useful for his work than other methods. 
 
Schwalke had initially struggled with applying discounts in private company valuation but 
became more comfortable with different estimation methods. In particular, he finds an 
option-based approach to quantifying the lack of marketability quite useful. In his recent 
work on valuing JNK, he estimated the value of three put options with three months until 
expiration on the most similar public comparable company to JNK. The public comparable 
was trading at a stock price of EUR 29.70. The three -month risk-free rate is 4%. The 
put option valuation results are summarized as follows: 
JNK Put Option Exercise Prices and Values 
Exercise price Put option value 
EUR 25 EUR 1.25 
EUR 30 EUR 3.75 
EUR 35 EUR 6.95

questions.question_text: Which statement best reflects how discount rate biases may affect offer prices in transactions involving public company buyers and private company targets?
options: A. Public company buyers pay offer prices for private firms that reflect
B. Public company buyers pay offer prices for private firms that reflect the higher
C. Public company buyers pay offer prices for private companies that do not reflect
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Acquisition offer prices often reflect the improvements that a public company buyer will make to the private firm, such as reducing expenses. B is incorrect as this statement contradicts the statement in A. C is incorrect as the public company buyer is likely to pay a premium to successfully gain control of the private company.

questions.question_text: Which statement best explains why the CAPM may be inappropriate for estimating required return on equity for private firms?
options: A. The CAPM was only designed for publicly traded stocks.
B. The CAPM does not utilize a company-specific risk premium.
C. The CAPM assumes investors are well diversified.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Private company owners are rarely well diversified, as much of their wealth is tied up in their company. CAPM assumes that investors are only exposed to market risk, not the total risk of a company. B is incorrect, as the CAPM includes a company-specific risk component measured by beta multiplied by the equity risk premium. A is incorrect, as the CAPM can measure the expected return of any financial asset, not just traded stocks.

questions.question_text: Which statement is most correct regarding Schwalke’s estimation of JNK’s beta?
options: A. Schwalke estimates JNK’s beta to be less than 1.20.
B. Schwalke estimates JNK’s beta to be 1.20.
C. Schwalke estimates JNK’s beta to be greater than 1.20.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Observed beta estimates from public companies are levered betas. The levered beta must be adjusted to remove the effects of the debt on firm risk by applying the following unlevered beta equation. 𝛽unlevered = 𝛽levered [1 + (1 − 𝑡) × ( Debt Equity)] The larger the public company debt ratio, the lower the unlevered beta result. The unlevered beta is re-levered using JNK’s debt ratio using the following levered beta equation. 𝛽levered ∗ = 𝛽unlevered [1 + (1 − 𝑡∗) × ( Debt Equity) ∗ ] As JNK’s debt ratio is below that of the public companies, the resulting levered beta will not be as high as the levered beta for the public companies.

questions.question_text: Which terminal value estimation method is least useful for Schwalke?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Because Schwalke’s work involves valuing entire businesses, the EEM is likely to be the least useful due to its reliance on multiple discount rates. The EEM is more commonly used to value a company’s intangible assets, while the CCF and market multiple methods are more useful in valuing entire businesses.

questions.question_text: Which amount most closely estimates the DLOM for JNK?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The put option approach involves an at -the-money option based on the prevailing forward price. Given the current price of EUR 29.70, the three -month forward price using a 4% risk-free rate is EUR 30 (=29.70e(0.25×0.04)), so the put option with exercise price of EUR 30 should be used. Dividing the 3.75 option value by the EUR 29.70 stock price equals 12.6%.

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 11-15
vignettes.case_study_text:
Schwalke is currently valuing LPE, a private furniture manufacturing company based in 
France. The company is owned entirely by the Lapiere family, with several family 
members employed as senior company managers. Jean Lapiere is the current CEO and 
owns 25% of LPE stock. LPE’s most recent income statement as part of its reviewed 
financial statements is as follows: 
As of 31 December (in EUR) As Reported 
Revenues 3,00,00,000 
Cost of goods sold 1,80,00,000 
Gross profit 1,20,00,000 
SG&A expenses 80,00,000 
EBITDA 40,00,000 
Depreciation and amortization 24,00,000 
Earnings before interest and taxes 16,00,000 
Pro forma taxes (at 25%) 4,00,000 
Operating income after taxes 12,00,000 
 
As Schwalke  reviews compensation expenses, he learns that Jean Lapiere’s annual 
compensation is EUR 300,000 and that CEOs of similarly sized consumer durable goods 
companies earn EUR 600,000 on average. 
 
To estimate LPE’s required return on equity, Schwalke gathers betas from public 
furniture manufacturing companies, and after making appropriate adjustments, 
estimates LPE’s beta at 0.80. He uses an equity risk premium of 6%, a small -cap stock 
premium of 2%, a company-specific stock premium of 1.5%, and an industry risk premium 
of 1%. 
 
After making other normalizing assumptions to LPE’s income statement and deducting 
the change in long -term assets of EUR 600,000 (equal to EUR 3,000,000 in capital 
expenditures less EUR 2,400,000 in depreciation), Schwalke estimates FCFF for the 
base year to be EUR 600,000. He decides to use the CCM in his income approach to 
valuing LPE with a WACC of 8% and perpetual growth of 4%. 
 
Given the availability of similar publicly traded furniture manufacturing companies, 
Schwalke also uses a market approach to value LPE. He finds an average EV/Sales multiple 
of 0.60 from these public comparable companies. Schwalke notes that LPE’s debt is 
currently EUR 6 million. 
 
Jean Lapiere is seeking an estimate of the value of his LPE ownership stake. In the course 
of discussing the ownership structure, Schwalke concludes that none of the family 
members, including Jean, has a controlling interest in the company. Schwalke estima tes 
discounts for lack of control and lack of marketability as 20% and 15%, respectively.

questions.question_text: Which amount is closest to LPE’s normalized EBITDA after considering Jean Lapiere’s compensation?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Jean Lapiere receives EUR 0.3 million less as LPE’s CEO than what he should expect in an arms-length contract. LPE’s normalized EBITDA should therefore be EUR 0.3 million below its reported EUR 4.0 million.

questions.question_text: Which amount most accurately reflects the difference between Schwalke’s estimates of LPE’s required return on equity using the build -up approach versus the expanded CAPM?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The build-up method is the sum of the equity risk premium (6%), small- cap stock premium (2%), company-specific premium (1.5%), and industry risk premium (1%), or 10.5%. The expanded CAPM reflects the sum of the beta -adjusted equity risk premium (0.8×6%), the small -cap stock premium (2%), and the company -specific premium (1.5%), or 8.3%.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is closest to the proper calculation of LPE’s EV using the CCM?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The CCM uses the following formula: Firm Value𝑡 = FCFF𝑡+1 WACC − 𝑔 Recall that the FCFF at time t+1 must equal the base year FCFF multiplied by one plus the growth rate. Firm Value𝑡 = FCFF𝑡+1 WACC − 𝑔 = 600,000 × 1.04 0.08 − 0.04 = 15,600,000

questions.question_text: Which of the following is closest to the proper calculation of LPE’s equity value using the EV/Sales multiple?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Applying the EV/Sales multiple of 0.60 to LPE’s base year sales of EUR 30 million results in an EV of EUR 18 million. To calculate equity value from EV, we deduct the debt of EUR 6 million to arrive at an equity value of EUR 12 million.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is closest to the size of the total discount taken in calculating the value of Jean Lapiere’s equity stake?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The total discount equals 1 – (1 – 0.20)×(1 – 0.15) or 32.0%.

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 16-19
vignettes.case_study_text:
Ian Aaron owns a small consulting firm that specializes in private company valuation.  He 
is asked to make a presentation at a local university about his company's role in the 
investment industry, which he agrees to do. 
 
Aaron opens by explaining  how valuation approaches for private companies are 
conceptually similar to those used for public companies. He talks about how his firm uses 
the various methodologies available but that the method most frequently employed 
provides a direct comparisons to public companies. This method values an asset based on 
pricing multiples from sales of assets that are viewed as similar and relies on  data 
generated in actual transactions. He stresses  that a  stable, mature company,  where 
the sales and cash flows are more predictable, might be best valued utilizing this 
method.  
 
Aaron describes  how private company earnings  can potentially require significant 
adjustments in order to estimate the company’s normalized earnings. It may be necessary 
to adjust for non-recurring, non-economic, or other unusual items to eliminate anomalies 
and facilitate comparisons. A member of the audience asks  about a new business his 
family recently started and comments: 
Comment 1: Revenues are limited, family members working for the business have 
deferred their compensation.  
Comment 2: The business operates in a building that is currently owned by a family 
member.  
Comment 3: The few employees the business does have are paid similar to other 
start-ups in our area.    
 
Aaron next discusses how standard ways of determining an appropriate discount rate are 
not applicable to private firm valuation. He points out that several adjustments may be 
necessary to incorporate the real risk of  the investment. He states the following  may 
affect the selection of an appropriate discount rate:  
**Statement 1:**
Due to its ease of access and liquidity, private companies tend to 
rely on bank financing. 
**Statement 2:**
In evaluating an acquisition the buyer’s cost of capital is irrelevant. 
**Statement 3:**
An adjustment for managerial experience is rarely necessary.   
 
He concludes on his discussion regarding the discount rate by talking about the various 
methods available to  estimate a required rate of return for valuing a private 
company. Aaron explains that his firm typically uses the method that was used to 
determine the discount rate as a set of premia added to the risk-free rate. He explains 
that the premia are typically based on factors such as size and company risk. This model 
is often used when guideline public companies are unavailable and excludes the application 
of beta to the equity risk premium. He also indicates an   industry risk adjustment may 
need to be made.

questions.question_text: Which private company valuation approach does Aaron's firm most frequently employ?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Aaron's comments indicate his firm most frequently employs the market approach. The market approach values an asset based on pricing multiples from sales of assets viewed as similar to the subject asset. (Pricing multiples may refer to multiples b ased on share price or multiples based on a measure of total company value.) Furthermore, the market approach uses direct comparisons to public companies and acquired enterprises to estimate the fair value of an equity interest in a private company. Additi onally, the market approach relies on data generated in actual market transactions. Finally, a stable, mature company might be best valued on the basis of the market approach.

questions.question_text: Which of the audience member's comments would least likely require an adjustment to normalize earnings?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because Comment 3: The few employees the business does have are paid similar to other start -ups in our area, would least likely require an adjustment to normalize earnings. Their compensation is commensurate with market levels required by employees performing similar activities for other firms and would not require an adjustment to normalize earnings.

questions.question_text: Which of Aaron's statements regarding the selection of an appropriate discount rate is accurate?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Statement 2: In evaluating an acquisition the buyer’s cost of capital is irrelevant is accurate. In evaluating an acquisition, finance theory indicates that the cost of capital used should be based on the target company’s capital structure and the riskiness of the target company’s cash flows—the buyer’s cost of capital is irrelevant.

questions.question_text: When valuing small private companies, which method does Aaron's firm most frequently use to estimate the required rate of return?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Aaron's comments indicate his firm most frequently uses the build-up approach. The build -up approach, introduced earlier as part of return concept topics, relies on building up the required rate of return as a set of premia added to the risk-free rate. The added premia are typically based on factors such as size and company risk. When guideline public companies (public-company comparables for the company being valued) are unavailable or of questionable comparability, appraisers may rely on a build-up method rather than the CAPM or other models. The build-up method, unlike the expanded CAPM, excludes the application of beta to the equity risk premium. In the build -up model, in which beta is implicitly assumed equal to 1, an argument exists to include an industry risk adjustment (premium or discount), although there are challenges in measuring industry risk adjustments. For the baseline implementation of the build -up model, we take the model with an industry risk adjustment.

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 20-23
vignettes.case_study_text:
Dan Brady is a partner at a firm that facilitates investments and sales of interests in 
privately-held firms. He is reviewing several firms that are seeking investors or 
purchasers. 
 
HILLSIDE COMPANY 
Ted Samant founded Hillside Company 23 years ago and wants to retire. Knowing the 
importance of sound financial practices, Samant has audited financial statements for 
Hillside for each of the past 10 years. Hillside operates in a declining growth segment of 
its industry. The firm's plant is located in a busy and growing industrial area and may be 
attractive to nearby businesses seeking to expand. Many of its fixed assets, including 
machinery and equipment, can be used in other businesses. There are two larg e 
multinational publicly-held firms that are in the same industry as Hillside and private 
market transactions of firms in the industry occur once every 1-2 years.  After collecting 
information from Samant, Brady considers what method he should use for valuing Hillside 
and the type of transaction he would need to identify to obtain maximum value for 
Hillside. 
 
The following month, Samant contacts Brady to tell him that his oldest daughter is willing 
to leave her position with a public firm and become CEO of Hillside. As part of the 
arrangement, the daughter wants Brady to find minority investors to provide capital for 
various plant upgrades and for developing new markets.   Brady estimates investors in a 
private firm such as Hillside will require a 15% discount for lack of control and a 20% 
discount for lack of marketability.  
 
AJACKS COMPANY 
Ajacks is a family-owned firm that the family wishes to sell in order to set up a charitable 
foundation. The father, mother, and son all participate in the business. Brady reviews 
their financial statements and comes to these conclusions: 
 
• Compensation, which is part of SG&A, was $800,000 higher per year for family 
members than is typical for firms of comparable size and sales revenue. 
• The top officers of the firm had annual “strategic planning retreats” at various resort 
locations with annual expenses (part of SG&A) averaging $50,000. Brady thought this 
was unnecessary and excessive for this type of firm. 
• The firm owns a private plane, book value of $180,000 with annual operating expenses 
of $14,000  and annual depreciation expense of $30,000. Given their business, owning 
such an asset is unusual as annual business air travel would cost approximately $4,000. 
• The parents own a 500,000 square foot warehouse which they rent to Ajacks at a 
cost of $8 a square foot annually.   Market-based leases for this type of facility 
average $6 a square foot.  This would affect reported SG&A expense. 
 
Exhibit 1 shows selected financial data from Ajacks. Brady computes the firm's 
normalized operating income after taxes. 
 
| normalized operating | income after taxes. |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Revenues** | $40,000,000 |
| **Costs of goods sold** | $25,500,000 |
| **Gross profit** | 1,45,00,000 |
| **SG&A expense** | $6,800,000 |
| **EBITDA** | $7,700,000 |
| **Depreciation** | $1,750,000 |
| **EBIT** | $5,950,000 |
| **Pro forma taxes (35%)** | $2,082,500 |
| **Operating income after taxes** | $3,867,500 |

Exhibit 1 As reported Brady also collects the following estimates to compute a discount rate and estimated firm value for Ajacks:
 
• Nominal risk-free rate: 3% 
• Expected inflation rate: 2% 
• Equity risk premium: 5.5% 
• Beta for similar firms, adjusted for capital structure differences: 1.2 
• Small stock premium: 3.5% 
• Industry risk premium: 0% 
• Company-specific risk adjustment: 1%

questions.question_text: Based on the information initially collected from Samant about his firm, the appropriate method for valuing Hillside is:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. as this is likely to be the most appropriate valuation approach. Hillside's assets can be sold and repurposed by other businesses and its location will make the plant site attractive to other businesses.

questions.question_text: If Samant's daughter becomes Hillside's CEO, the valuation discount for additional equity investors will be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. as the correct calculation is: 1 – (1 – 0.15)(1 – 0.20) = 1 – 0.68 = 0.32 or 32% For an equity interest in which a 10% lack of control discount and a 20% lack of marketability discount are believed to be appropriate, the total discount is 28% [1 – (1 – 10%)(1 – 20%)] rather than 30% (10% + 20%).

questions.question_text: The normalized operating income after taxes for Ajacks is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the candidate removes excess SG&A expenses ($800,000 compensation, $50,000 retreats, $10,000 excess air travel expenses ($14,000 plane expenses minus estimated $4000 cost of regular air travel) and $1 million excess warehouse rent (excess charge of $2/sq. ft x 500,000 sq ft). In addition, the candidate correctly removes $30,000 of excess depreciation due to the company airplane. As reported Adjustment Normalized Revenues $40,000,000 $40,000,000 COGS $25,500,000 $25,500,000 Gross profit $14,500,000 $14,500,000 SG&A Expense $6,800,000 $4,940,000 –$800,000 excess compensation –$50,000 retreats –$10,000 excess air travel ($14k – $4k) –$1,000,000 excess warehouse cost EBITDA $7,700,000 $9,560,000 Depreciation $1,750,000 $1,720,000 $30,000 airplane depreciation Operating income $5,950,000 $7,840,000 Pro forma taxes (35%) $2,082,500 $2,744,000 Operating income after taxes $3,867,500 $5,096,000

questions.question_text: Brady's estimate for the required return on equity for Ajacks using the expanded CAPM is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. as the candidate correctly applied the expanded CAPM: Expanded CAPM = RFR + beta*equity risk premium + small stock premium + company- specific risk premium. Expanded CAPM = 3% + (1.20)(5.5%) + 3.5% + 1% = 14.1% The expanded CAPM is an adaptation of the CAPM that adds to the CAPM premiums for small size and company-specific risk.

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 24-25
vignettes.case_study_text:
Daniel Pegham is a mergers and acquisitions analyst currently advising Horzak Digital, a 
privately owned, rapidly growing satellite dish manufacturer that reports in USD. 
 
Pegham wants to use FCFE to value the company and recalls that some peers 
have used EBITDA as a proxy for FCFE. He concludes that EBITDA is not a good proxy 
for Horzak’s FCFE for two reasons. First, EBITDA fails to account for cash flows from 
new borrowings. Second, EBITDA understates cash flows for growth companies such as 
Horzak. 
 
Instead, Pegham uses the indirect method to calculate FCFE, starting with Horzak’s 
previous year’s net income. Horzak had two unusual noncash items in the previous year: a 
USD 5 million after-tax reversal of noncash restructuring charges taken in the previous 
year, and a USD 2 million after-tax gain. 
 
Horzak is entirely family-owned, and the president is the largest shareholder. Horzak is 
subject to a 30% tax rate. Pegham determines that, in the event of a takeover, his 
valuation should reflect two additional adjustments to normalize net income. 
 
• Board compensation was USD 1,500,000 in the previous year, compared to USD 
800,000 at comparable companies. 
• Horzak has had free use of storage facilities owned by the company’s president for 
warehousing inventory. This benefit would otherwise have cost USD 150,000 in the 
previous year. 
 
Lastly, Pegham evaluates Horzak using the capitalized cash flow method under the 
following assumptions. 
 
• Normalized FCFE in the current period (FCFE0) is USD 4.0 million. 
• The long-term growth rate of FCFE is 5%. 
• The market value of debt is USD 6.6 million. 
• The cost of equity is 17.5%.

questions.question_text: The total additional adjustment (in USD) needed to normalize Horzak’s previous year net income in the event of a takeover is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the excessive Board remuneration is an above -market expense whose excess over the normal rate of remuneration needs to be added to reported net income, while the free use of storage facilities is a benefit that requires a deduction from net income to reflect its normal market cost. Earnings normalization in private companies seeks to adjust for anomalies such as under- or over- reporting of earnings arising from noneconomic or non-recurring factors, in order to establish a baseline for forecasting future earnings under new ownership. These anomalies can include executive remuneration that deviates significantly from market practice, private use by executives of the companies’ assets, or free use of personal assets by the company. New ownership of Horzak Digital would likely cut executive remuneration to the market rate, bringing about an increase in net income of USD 700,000 (USD 1.5 million less USD 0.8 million). Likewise, under new ownership, the CEO is unlikely to want to continue to provide free use of storage facilities in property he personally owns, resulting in a loss of USD 150,000. Combining these two adjustments results in a pre-tax gain of USD 550,000. Accounting for a 30% tax rate results in a total adjustment to net income of USD 385,000 [=550,000 × (1-30%)].

questions.question_text: The estimated value (in USD millions) of Horzak’s equity under the capitalized cash flow method is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because, according to the FCFE variation of the capitalized cash flow method, the estimated value of equity equals the estimate of the normalized free cash flow to equity for the next period divided by the capitalization rate for equity. The capitalization rate is the required rate of return (i.e., cost of equity) for equity less the long-term growth rate in free cash flow to equity. The formula for the value of equity, V, is: 𝑉 = 𝐹𝐶𝐹𝐸1 (𝑟−𝑔) where FCFE1 is the next period's FCFE, equivalent to the current period's FCFE increased by the long -term growth rate, g, and is r is the cost of equity. 𝑉 = 4.0 × 1.05 0.175 − 0.05 = 4.2 0.125 = USD 33.6 million

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 26-28
vignettes.case_study_text:
Minneapolis Viking Arbitrageurs, LLC (MVA), is a fledgling US -based hedge fund having 
slightly more than $50 million in assets under management. MVA specializes in owning 
and managing small-sized properties in agriculture, forestry, and mining.  
 
Jim Hester, MVA’s merger and acquisition (M&A) analyst is evaluating Fargo Durum 
Farms, Inc. (FDF) for a potential acquisition. FDF owns 1,500 acres of fertile land, farm 
buildings, machinery, residential quarters, livestock, cattle feed, seeds, grain, significant 
amounts of intangible assets, and so on. Selected data from FDF’s income statement for 
the previous year ended and additional estimates compiled by Hester are presented in 
Exhibits 1 and 2. 
 
**Exhibit 1:**
FDF’s Select Financial Data for the Previous Year Ended  
Gross revenues from crops, livestock, feed, and so on $2,500,000 
Cost of goods sold 10,00,000 
Selling, general, and administrative expenses (SG&A) 9,00,000 
Depreciation and amortization 2,00,000 
Tax rate 30% 
Notes:  
1) FDF carries debt in the amount of $750,000 at an interest rate of 8%, and it 
comprises 30% of total assets on a book value basis. Debt will be a part of the 
acquisition transaction. 
2) FDF holds $200,000 in cash and short -term investments, but it will not be a 
part of the assets under acquisition transaction. 
 
**Exhibit 2:**
Additional Data and Hester’s Estimates for Normalization 
1 The cost of goods sold ratio should be higher at 45%. 
2 
SG&A includes $400,000 in owners’ compensation. According to Hester’s 
research, owners’ compensation expense for similar sized farms is 
$200,000. 
3 
A ranch and living quarters are not required for the farm’s core operations. 
The reported SG&A expenses include $100,000 for operating expenses 
relating to those properties. The ranch and living quarters will be kept by 
the current owners and are not a par t of FDF’s farming operations being 
considered for purchase by the hedge fund. 
4.  
For pro forma estimations, Hester will consider depreciation and 
amortization at 10% of gross revenues. He thinks the current tax rate of 
30% is reasonable. 
 
First, Hester assesses FDF’s normalized operating income after tax. Next, Hester values 
FDF’s equity starting with the free cash flow to the firm (FCFF) with the data and 
assumptions in Exhibit 3. 
 
**Exhibit 3:**
| Item | Value 1 | Value 2 | Value 3 | Value 4 | Value 5 | Value 6 | Value 7 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| FDF’s Data and Estimates for Valuing Its Equity FCFF for | Current Year | $336,250 FCFF annual growth rate for the foreseeable future | 5.00% FDF’s debt holding at an interest rate of | 8.0% per year | $750,000 FDF’s weighted average cost of capital (WACC) | 11.50% FDF’s cost of equity | 14.00% Hester presents his initial assessment and valuation of FDF to MVA’s investment committee. The following are comments and suggestions from some members on the |
committee.  
 
Xavier Moreno, commodities analyst, suggests the use of the excess earnings method 
(EEM) for valuing FDF and makes the following three statements in support of his 
preference: 
 
1. EEM involves estimating the earnings remaining before deducting amounts that 
reflect the required returns to the tangible assets. 
2. EEM is a widely-used method for valuing entire private businesses similar to FDF. 
3. EEM is especially useful for valuing FDF because it allows for valuing working capital, 
fixed assets, and intangibles using different discount rates. 
 
Jamal Bahrami, the external consultant on the committee, differs from Hester and 
prefers the use of the free cash flow to equity (FCFE) model. Furthermore, he develops 
his own estimates for valuing FDF's equity, shown in Exhibit 4.  
 
**Exhibit 4:**
Bahrami’s Estimates for Valuing FDF’s Equity 
1 In current year, FDF will: 
  • realize $1,000,000 in cash flow from operations. 
 
  • require $400,000 in new capital investment.  
  • need additional borrowing in the amount of $250,000 at an interest cost 
of 8%.  
2 
Because of the continued strength in the global demand for wheat, FDF will 
experience a higher annual growth rate of 10% for the following year; thereafter, 
it will grow at a constant rate of 6% per year. 
 
3 Because of illiquidity and small -firm risk premiums, the appropriate WACC and 
required return on equity will be higher at 12.9% and 16%, respectively.

questions.question_text: Using the company’s data and Hester’s assessments and estimates in Exhibits 1 and 2, FDF’s normalized operating income after taxes for the previous year is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. FDF’s Financial Performance for the Previous Year Ended As Reported ($) Normalized ($) Gross revenues from crops, livestock, feed, and so on 25,00,000 25,00,000 Cost of goods sold (normalized to 45% of revenues) –1,000,000 –1,125,000 Gross profit 15,00,000 13,75,000 SG&A (reflects $200,000 reduction in owner’s compensation and $100,000 reduction in operating expenses related to the ranch) –900,000 –600,000 EBITDA 6,00,000 7,75,000 Depreciation and amortization (normalized to 10% of revenues) –200,000 –250,000 Earnings before interest and taxes 4,00,000 5,25,000 Pro forma taxes (at 30%) –120,000 –157,500 Operating income after taxes 2,80,000 3,67,500

questions.question_text: Using the data and assumptions in Exhibit 3 compiled by Hester, FDF’s value of equity as at the end of the previous year is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Vprev yr = FCFFcurr yr/(WACC – g) 336.250/(0.115 – 0.05) $5,173,077 Less debt -7,50,000 Value of equity (Previous Year end) $4,423,077

questions.question_text: In regard to Moreno’s three statements, he is most accurate with respect to the statement concerning:
options: B. required returns to tangible assets.
C. the use of different discount rates for valuation.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The EEM allows for valuing working capital, fixed assets, and intangible assets using different discount rates.

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Vignette Questions 29-32
vignettes.case_study_text:
Rila Rakia & Beer Ltd. (RRBL), a small privately owned company, produces high quality 
rakia (a high-potency hard liquor), vino (wine), and bira (beer) in Bulgaria. After Bulgaria 
joined the European Union in 2007, international demand for the country’s liq uor, wine, 
and beer increased substantially. Most firms in the industry, including RRBL, have been 
reporting double-digit sales growth on a year-over-year basis. 
 
Metiu Metev, a portfolio strategist at a major German investment consulting firm, 
inherited RRBL from his grandparents. Frankfurter Destillerie & LiqueurFabrik (FDLF), 
a German distillery interested in entering the Bulgarian market, has made a cash offer 
of BGN900 million for the company’s equity (BGN = Bulgarian Lev; EUR1 = BGN1.95586, 
pegged rate). FDLF will assume RRBL’s entire outstanding debt, including both current 
liabilities and long -term debt. If Metev does not want to sell a controlling interest, 
FDLF’s minimum equity stake will be 40%, with an appropriate discount for lack of control. 
 
Metev starts by evaluating the company himself using the capitalized cash flow method 
(CCM) and taking the following steps: 
• Using the build-up method to estimate the required rate of return on equity 
• Computing the firm’s weighted average cost of capital (WACC) using the book values 
of debt and considering the current weight of total debt in capital structure to be 
optimal 
 
Exhibits 1A, 1B, 2, and 3 contain RRBL’s financial data and other inputs that Metev uses. 
 
Before responding to FDLF’s offer, however, Metev meets with Vasil Nenkov, his 
colleague and a senior equity analyst covering the wine and beer industry in the Balkan 
region. After reviewing the data and Metev’s valuation analysis, Nenkov suggests that 
11% would be a more reasonable estimate of RRBL’s WACC based on an analysis of 
industry peers. Metev decides to use this rate to calculate the value of RRBL’s equity. 
 
Nenkov also makes the following comments: 
 
1. CCM is most often used for the valuation of large public companies, and it is less valid 
for valuing private companies such as RRBL. 
2. The excess earnings method is preferable because it provides an estimate of the 
value of intangible assets by capitalizing future earnings in excess of the estimated 
return requirements associated with working capital and fixed assets. 
 
Nenkov also suggests that the enterprise value (EV) multiple approach should work well 
for valuing RRBL. After searching his database, Nenkov finds that Rhodopi Wineries PLC, 
a publicly traded company and a close competitor of RRBL, is currently valued at an 
EV/EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) multiple 
of 7.2. Nenkov further suggests two adjustments: 
 
• RRBL should command an upward adjustment of 25% in the EV/EBITDA multiple 
reflecting its lower risk and higher growth relative to Rhodopi Wineries. 
• Forward-looking EBITDA should be used to determine RRBL’s value. 
 
On a cautionary note, Nenkov makes two statements regarding the use of EV/EBITDA 
approach to valuation. 
 
1. It is more appropriate than the P/E for comparing companies with different financial 
leverage. 
2. EBITDA underestimates cash flow from operations if working capital is growing. 
 
Finally, Metev recalls FDLF’s willingness to purchase a non-controlling ownership interest 
at a discount for lack of control. Nenkov responds by saying that a control premium of 
30% is typically applied for purchase transactions of small privately owned firms similar 
to RRBL and that proper adjustment for lack of control should be made if the transaction 
involves a non-controlling interest. Metev thanks Nenkov for his help and goes back to 
his desk to revise his valuations. 
Exhibit 1A: RRBL EBITDA and Other Data (BGN millions)

questions.question_text: According to the method used by Metev for computing the cost of equity and data in Exhibits 1B and 3, RRBL’s WACC is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Using data from Exhibit 3, the cost of equity (build-up method) = Risk- free rate + Equity risk premium + Small stock risk premium + Industry risk premium + Company-specific risk adjustment. Cost of equity = 3.9 + 6.0 + 2.5 + 0.0 + 1.5 = 13.9% WACC = Pre -tax cost of debt × (1 – T) × (Debt weight) + Cost of equity × (Equity weight) WACC = 10.0 × (1 – 0.25) × (0.3) + 13.9 × (0.7) = 2.25 + 9.03 = 11.98%

questions.question_text: According to the revised method used by Metev , the WACC suggested by Nenkov, and the data provided in Exhibit 3, RRBL’s capitalization rate is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Capitalization rate = (WACC – Long-term growth rate) = 11.0% – 5.0% = 6.0%.

questions.question_text: Regarding the two comments that Nenkov made after reviewing Metev’s valuation analysis, he is most accurate with respect to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Nenkov is incorrect with respect to his first comment because the capitalized cash flow method is rarely used for the valuation of public companies and is more appropriate for valuing a private company such as RRBL. Nenkov’s second comment is correct because the excess earnings method involves estimating the earnings remaining after deducting the amounts that reflect the required returns to working capital and tangible assets. The residual amount of earnings (i.e., the excess earnings) is capitalized to obtain an estimate of the value of intangible assets. Therefore, Nenkov’s second comment is correct.

questions.question_text: The discount for lack of control, given the typical control premium indicated by Nenkov, is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Discount for lack of control = 1 – [1/(1+ Control premium)] = 1 – [1/(1.30 = 23.1%

vignettes.topic: Private Company Valuation - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: Which of the following approaches to private company valuation corresponds to discounted cash flow models?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the income approach corresponds to what public equity analysts call discounted cash flow models or present value models.

questions.question_text: Using a market approach, an analyst seeks to estimate the enterprise value of a privately held company from the perspective of a minority shareholder, and gathers the following data: Lines of Business Percentage of Revenue Public Comparable EV/Sales Multiple Toys 60% 8.3 Apparel 30% 5.2 Grocery 10% 1.5 If the total discount for lack of marketability and control is 25% and the company has total revenues of $65,000,000, its enterprise value is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because to arrive at an initial estimate of the private company’s EV, the analyst must multiply the respec tive peer industry multiples by the private firm’s fundamental variables associated with each multiple: (0.60) × (8.3) + (0.30) × (5.2) + (0.10) × (1.5) ≈ 6.69. The composite EV/Sales ratio is multiplied by the private firm’s revenues to give a preliminary EV estimate of 6.69 × 65,000,000 ≈ $434,850,000. This value needs to be adjusted to reflect the discount for lack of marketability and control, resulting in a final value of: $434,850,000 × (1 – 0.25) ≈ $326,137,500.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about private company discount rates is most accurate?
options: A. Limited access to debt leads a private company to rely more on equity financing,
B. Size premium estimates from financially distressed small capitalization public
C. Lack of information about a private company's operations introduces greater
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a relative lack of information concerning a private company’s operations or business model compared with that of a similar public company introduces greater uncertainty into projections that may lead to a higher required rate of return.

questions.question_text: An analyst values a private company using the income approach from the perspective of a non-controlling shareholder and calculates the following information: FCFF (base year) (Year 0) $25,000,000 WACC 18% Growth rate for first 2 years 8% Perpetual growth rate 2% Market value of outstanding debt $12,000,000 If the total discount for lack of marketability and control is 25%, the non-marketable equity value of the firm is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the enterprise value of the firm is computed as: EV = summation from 1 to i of FCFFt+ i/(1+WACCi) + FCFF t+i+n/(WACC – g)/(1 + WACC n). We discount these annual cash flows at the WACC of 18%. Year Growth Rate Calculation FCFF Base Year (Year 0) --- --- $25million Year 1 8% $25million × 1.08 $27million Year 2 8% $27million × 1.08 $29.16million Terminal Value 2% ($29.16million × 1.02)/(0.18 – 0.02) = $185,895,000 The EV is the present value of the FCFF from years 1 and 2 plus the present value of the terminal value: = $27,000,000/1.181 + $29,160,000/1.182 + $185,895,000/1.182 = $22,881,355.93 + $20,942,257.97 + $133,506,894.60 = $177,330,508.50 ≈ $177,330,509 The enterprise value less outstanding debt, gives the equity value: = $177,330,509 – $12,000,000 = $165,330,509 Finally, applying the total discount for lack of marketability and control, the non - marketable equity value is: = $165,330,509 × (1 – 0.25) = $123,997,881.80 ≈ $123,998,000

questions.question_text: Compared to public company shareholders, private company shareholders are more likely to experience:
options: C. a greater level of transparency.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because private company ownership stakes also differ significantly from common shares in publicly traded companies due to their lack of liquidity, concentration of ownership control which may impact some shareholders differently than others, and share sale restrictions, all of which affect company valuation.

questions.question_text: A private company valuation conducted for tax reporting purposes is best classified as which type of valuation?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because compliance-related valuations support actions required by law or regulation and include financial reporting and tax reporting. Tax-related reasons for private company valuations include corporate and individual tax reporting. For example, activities such as corporate restructurings, transfer pricing, and property tax matters may require valuations.

questions.question_text: A fund manager plans to take a majority stake in a private company with the goal of exiting at a higher valuation after improving the efficiency of the company's business practices. The fund is most likely a:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because leveraged buy -out firms acquire majority control and seek to create value through more efficient business practices and optimizing the balance sheet.

questions.question_text: An analyst prepares the following information (all € figures in thousands) to estimate the value of a private company: FCFF base year (Year 0) € 10,500 Growth rate years 1 through 4 6% Present value at Year 0 of the year 4 terminal value € 75,389 WACC 13% Debt value € 18,500 Discount for lack of marketability (DLOM) 15% The equity valuation (in € thousands) of the company, from the perspective of a non- controlling shareholder, is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because as shown below this calculates the present value of 4 years of FCFF plus the terminal value to determine enterprise value (EV), then adjusts the EV for the debt value and DLOM. Year 0 Year 1 Year 2 Year 3 Year 4 FCFF g= 6% 11,130 11,798 12,506 13,256 present value at 13% 9,850 9,239 8,667 8,130 Sum of FCFF years 1 - 4 35,886 Present value terminal value 75,389 Enterprise value 1,11,275 less debt value -18,500 Equity value 92,775 less DLOM 15% -13,916 Equity value less DMOL 78,859

questions.question_text: An analyst seeks to value a private company using a market -based approach. Which of the following methods is based on pricing multiples derived from acquisitions of entire public or private companies?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because there are three major variations of the market approach. The guideline transactions method (GTM) establishes a value estimate based on pricing multiples derived from the acquisition of control of entire public or private companies. Unlike the GPCM, the GTM uses pricing multiples derived from acquisitions of public or private companies.

questions.question_text: An analyst identifies the following characteristics that make a private company different from public companies: • The financial statements have limited disclosures • The senior management owns 60% of the shares • The shareholder agreement restricts the ability to sell shares Which characteristic would most likely have a positive effect on the private company valuation?
options: B. Senior management owning a controlling interest in the company
C. A shareholder agreement that restricts the shareholders' ability to sell shares
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because when senior management has a controlling interest it reduces the principal -agent problem and gives them more direct control over strategic decisions. Both factors allow managers to take a longer-term perspective in decision- making which would be a positive factor in valuing the company. The senior management of many private firms often has a controlling ownership interest in the company. This feature of private companies greatly reduces the principal -agent problem which may arise when owners and managers are separate allows more direct control over strategic decisions than for public companies. Private company managers can take a longer -term perspective in strategic decision making without pressure from external investors seeking short-term gains on publicly traded shares.

questions.question_text: Which of the following private company valuation approaches corresponds to the discounted cash flow method?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the income approach corresponds to the discounted cash flow approach to valuation introduced earlier for public companies.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a private company: Working capital at fair value $300,000 Fixed assets at fair value $500,000 Required return on working capital 4% Required return on fixed assets 10% Normalized earnings for the most recent year $80,000 Perpetuity growth rate of residual income 2% Discount rate for intangible assets 10% Based on the excess earnings method, the company’s value is:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the excess earning method (EEM) is the fair market value of tangible assets + present value of excess earnings (residual value). First, calculate residual income (RIt) = normalized earnings – (required return on working capital × working capital amount) – (required return on fixed assets × fixed assets amount) = $80,000 – (4% × $300,000) – (10% × $500,000) = $18,000 Second, value intangible assets using the growing perpetuity RVt = (RIt × (1 + g)) / (rRI – g) = ($18,000 × (1 + 2%)) / (10% – 2%) = $229,500 Third, firm value = working capital + fixed assets + present value of intangible = $300,000 + $500,000 + $229,500 = $1,029,500.

questions.question_text: An analyst is assessing the value of a private company from the perspective of a non- controlling, minority -interest shareholder. The analyst gathers the following information: • The company's normalized EBITDA is $35,000,000 • The comparable peer multiple for EV/EBITDA is 8.0 • The discount for lack of control is 12% • The discount for lack of marketability is 15% Based on the guideline public company method, the value of the company is:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the guideline public company method (GPCM) establishes a value estimate based on observed multiples from trading activity in the shares of public companies viewed as comparable to the subject private company. The preliminary value estimate for the private company = normalized EBITDA × peer multiple of EV/EBITDA = $35,000,000 × 8.0 = $280,000,000. Adjusting the company’s value to reflect a non -controlling and non -marketable shareholder’s perspective. Total Discount = [1 – (1 – DLOC)×(1 – DLOM)] = [1 – (1 – 12%) × (1 – 15%)] = 0.252. Adjusting the valuation estimates for this discount = EVEV/EBITDA = $280,000,000 × (1 – 25.2%) = $209,440,000.

questions.question_text: An analyst collects the following data for a privately held company with no debt outstanding and two lines of business: Engineering Homebuilding EBITDA per business line $1,500,000 $1,000,000 EV/EBITDA multiples for comparable public companies 13.1× 4.1× Using a market approach to estimate the EV of the company, the analyst determines the discount for lack of control to be 10% and the discount for lack of marketability to be 12.5%. The EV for the company from a non -controlling, minority interest shareholder perspective is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because it calculates the EV based on the weighted average multiples of the two lines of business and adjusts it for the discount for lack of control (DLOC) and discount for lack of marketability (DLOM). EBITDA $ Weights Multiple Engineering 15,00,000 60.00% 13.1 Homebuilding 10,00,000 40.00% 4.4 Total 25,00,000 100% Weighted average multiple to use: (13.1 × 0.60) + (4.1 × 0.40) = 9.5 EV = $2,000,000 × 9.5 = $23,750,000 The adjustment for the DLOC and DLOM is calculated as follows: [1–((1–DLOC) × (1–DLOM))] = [1 – ((1 – 0.10) × (1 – 0.125))] = 21.25% $23,750,000 × (1–0.2125) = $18,703,125

questions.question_text: An analyst reviews the following annual income statement (as of 31 December) for a private company with no debt: Gross profit $8,000,000 SG&A expenses $3,000,000 EBITDA $5,000,000 Depreciation and amortization $1,000,000 EBIT $4,000,000 Income taxes (at 21%) $840,000 Operating income after taxes $3,160,000 The analyst determines that the salary and benefits package of the sole owner is $500,000 above market rates and that certain assets are being depreciated at a reduced rate, causing depreciation expense to be understated by $200,000. To normalize the company's earnings, after-tax operating income should be:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because, in order to determine the company's normalized earnings, the analyst must adjust for the higher salary and benefits and the lower depreciation expense. In this case, SG&A expense is overstated since replacing the current owner will reduce SG&A expense by $500,000. Therefore, SG&A expense should be decreased by $500,000. Depreciation expense is lower than what the analyst believes is warranted and therefore should be increased by $200,000. Gross profit $8,000,000 SG&A expenses $2,500,000 EBITDA $5,500,000 Depreciation and amortization $1,200,000 EBIT $4,300,000 Income taxes (at 21%) $903,000 Operating income after taxes $3,397,000 Therefore, the net change = $3,397,000 – $3,160,000 = $237,000. This can also be calculated as ($500,000 – $200,000) × (1 – 21%) = $237,000.

questions.question_text: An investor considers purchasing a non -controlling minority interest in a private company whose shares have not traded recently. The private company's closest comparable public company is trading at €60.00 per share and the appropriate at - the-money put option premium on shares of the public company is €7.50. Assuming a control premium of 20%, the estimated total discount due to lack of control and lack of marketability is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because a discount for lack of control (DLOC) involves a deduction from the pro rata share of 100% of the value of an equity interest to reflect the absence of some or all powers of control. A discount for lack of marketability (DLOM) is a deduction from an ownership interest’s value to reflect the relative absence (compared with publicly traded companies) of a liquid market for a company’s shares. Lack of marketability discounts are frequently applied in the valuation of non - controlling equity interests in private companies. Although a DLOM differs from a DLOC, they are often linked; that is, if a valuation is on a non -controlling interest basis, a lack of marketability discount is typically appropriate. Option-based approaches seek to quantify DLOMs using the right to sell shares as captured by a put option premium. This premium is used to quantify the ability to sell at a given price. As a first step, an at-the-money put option is priced. The put option premium as a percentage of the stock value provides an estimate of the DLOM. The DLOC is 1 – [1/(1 + Control premium)] = 1 – [1/(1 + 0.2)] = 1 – [1/1.2] ≈ 1 – 0.833 = 0.167 = 16.7%. The DLOM is €7.50/€60.00 = 0.125 = 12.5%. The total discount for lack of control and lack of marketability is therefore 1 – [(1 – 0.167) × (1 – 0.125)] ≈ 0.271 = 27.1%.

questions.question_text: When compared to publicly traded peers, a private company's WACC should be adjusted:
options: A. upward when the company has reduced access to debt financing.
B. downward when the company is smaller in size relative to its peers.
C. downward when the company's management is not experienced in forecasting
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because reduced debt access may lead a private company to rely more on equity financing, which would tend to increase its WACC.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information with respect to a private company: Beta 0.5 Risk-free rate 5.1% Equity risk premium 4.2% Industry risk premium 0.0% Small-cap risk premium 2.2% Company-specific risk premium 1.5% Using the build -up approach, the required rate of return on the company's equity is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because, according to the build -up approach, the required return on equity is calculated as rf + Equity risk premium + Small -cap risk premium + Industry risk premium + Company -specific risk premium. Substituting, required return on equity = 5.1% + 4.2% + 2.2% + 0.0% + 1.5% = 13.0%.

vignettes.module: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct
vignettes.topic: Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements best describes an aspect of the Professional Conduct Program process?
options: A. Inquiries are not initiated in response to information provided by the media.
B. Investigations result in Disciplinary Review Committee panels for each case.
C. Investigations may include requesting a written explanation from the member or
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. When an inquiry is initiated, the Professional Conduct staff conducts an investigation that may include requesting a written explanation from the member or candidate. A is incorrect because Professional Conduct inquiries can be initiated in response to information provided by the media. CFA Institute staff may become aware of questionable conduct by a member or candidate through the media, regulatory notices, or other public sources. B is incorrect because although the Disciplinary Review Committee (DRC) is responsible for enforcement of the Code and Standards in conjunction with the Professional Conduct Program (PCP), only in the event that a member or candidate does not accept the charges and proposed sanction is the matter referred to a panel composed of DRC members.

questions.question_text: A current Code of Ethics principle reads in full, “Promote the integrity:
options: A. and viability of the global capital markets.”
B. of and uphold the rules governing capital markets.”
C. and viability of the global capital markets for the ultimate benefit of society.”
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. One of the principles in the Code of Ethics reflects the role that the capital markets have in society as a whole. A is incorrect because it is incomplete, missing the additional language to reflect the role that the capital markets have in society as a whole. B is incorrect because the principle as written in the Code of Ethics reflects the role of the capital markets in society as a whole.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is correct with regard to the Standards of Professional Conduct?
options: A. They require supervisors to focus only on the detection and prevention of
B. They set ethical conduct requirements for members and candidates that have
C. They address the risks and limitations of recommendations being made to clients.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct.
