yield curve will be influenced by investor expectations for declining policy rates, 
increasing inflation premiums for longer dated bonds, and bond risk premiums that are 
negatively related to consumption hedging benefits. 
 
Given the ongoing recession in  Australia, Redfield is reviewing  Australian corporate 
bonds with  the same five -year maturity  held in the  foreign bond strategy. The three 
bonds being reviewed are listed in Exhibit 2. Redfield agrees with Simmons’ assessment 
of the Australian economy. 
 
**Exhibit 2:**
| Item | Value 1 | Value 2 | Value 3 | Value 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A A Consumer Staples** | 0.50% | 40.00% | 200 bps |
| **B BBB– Consumer Discretionary** | 2.00% | 35.00% | 350 bps |
| C BB Industrial | 3.50% | 45.00% | 650 bps |  |
 
Based on this information, Redfield makes the following comments to Simmons regarding 
the Australian bonds: 
Comment 1: You can calculate the expected loss by multiplying the probability of 
default by the recovery rate. 
Comment 2: Relative to bond B and bond C, bond A has likely experienced the worst 
price return over the last 9 months. 
Comment 3: 
Based on your economic forecasts, in 6 –12 months overall credit 
spreads should narrow and the rate of improvement will likely be 
greatest for bond C.

questions.question_text: Using only the data in Exhibit 1, which country will Simmons most likely expect to have the highest average real short-term risk-free interest rates over the forecasted horizon?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Germany has the highest combination of expected GDP growth and expected GDP volatility of the countries presented in Exhibit 1. Thus, all else held constant, it would have the highest expected real short-term risk-free rates.

questions.question_text: Which of Simmons’ statements concerning inflation-linked bonds is most likely correct?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Since Simmons makes her statements while looking at the yield difference between nominal and inflation-linked bonds, Statement 2 (The difference in yields reflects the uncertainty about the quantity of goods and services investors will be able to consume in the future.) is correct. The difference in yields incorporates uncertainty about future inflation and, therefore, the uncertainty about the quantity of goods and services investors will be able to consume in the future.

questions.question_text: Which one of Simmons’ factors is most likely accurate with regard to investors influencing the future shape of the yield curve?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Simmons’ forecast is supported by increasing inflation premiums for longer dated bonds. Many factors can influence an upward sloping yield curve, including higher risk premiums as maturities increase.

questions.question_text: Which of Redfield’s comments regarding the Australian corporate bonds in Exhibit 2 is most likely correct?
options: B. GERA's expectations are that Country A and Country B will have similar levels of
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Comment 3 is correct. When credit spreads are generally narrowing, the rate of improvement will tend to be greater for those bonds issued by entities with a relatively weaker ability to pay. Bond C has the lowest credit rating, highest probability of defau lt, and widest spread over government bonds and should experience the greatest rate of improvement as the Australian economy improves and spreads narrow.

vignettes.topic: Economics and Investment Markets - Vignette Questions 37-40
vignettes.case_study_text:
Jose Ramires and Antonio Gomes are analysts at Global Economic Research Affiliates 
(GERA), an economic research firm. GERA focuses on providing research and investment 
recommendations to the global asset management community. Ramires and Gomes are 
reviewing Countries A, B, and C for possible investment. 
They discuss the role that economic news has in adjusting market valuations. Ramires 
provides the example of economic data that were recently released on Country B's 
economy. The most recent report showed that quarterly GDP declined at a 2.5% 
annualized rate. This was relative to market expectations of a 3.2% decline. Ramires 
discusses the immediate impact of the release of Country B's GDP report on the credit 
spreads of Country B's government bonds. 
 
GERA forecasts that Country A and Country C will experience the same long -term real 
GDP growth rate, which is significantly higher than the growth rate estimate for Country 
B. GERA's expectations are that Country A and Country B will have similar levels of  
volatility of real GDP growth, with volatility expected to be significantly higher than that 
of Country C. 
 
Maria Silva is an equity strategist at GERA. Silva believes it is important to study the 
relationships between economic cycles and corporate earnings, and she has made the 
following observations: 
Observation 1: 
Corporate profitability tends to be a lagging indicator of the business 
cycle and improvements are not typically evident until a recovery is 
well underway. 
Observation 2: An understanding of the business cycle is more important for making 
earnings projections over the long term than the short term. 
Observation 3: 
Durable goods manufacturers have much more volatile earnings 
profiles than non -durable goods manufacturers because of the 
dramatic shifts in economic cycle-related demand. 
 
GERA also offers a corporate bond research service designed to identify attractive 
industries for fixed -income investment based on economic cycles. GERA is currently 
forecasting a period of above-consensus growth.

questions.question_text: Following the GDP release, the credit spreads of Country B's government bonds are most likely to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because market news is only really considered relevant for impacting valuations when it reflects a change to existing consensus expectations as the consensus is already priced into valuations. In this case, Country B posted a quarterly GDP number that was significantly better than expectations and since this was an unexpected positive surprise, spreads should narrow, all else being equal.

questions.question_text: Based on GERA's GDP growth forecasts, which country will most likely have the highest short-term interest rates, all else being equal?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because Country A is expected to have the highest level of real GDP growth rates (tied with Country C) but also high levels of volatility of real GDP growth, both of which are positively correlated with the level of real default -free interest rates.

questions.question_text: Which of Silva's observations regarding economic cycles and corporate earnings is most accurate?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because durable goods manufacturers do have more volatile earnings profiles than non -durable goods manufacturers because of the dramatic shifts in economic cycle-related demand.

questions.question_text: Based on GERA's global economic forecast, which of the following industries is most appropriate for GERA's corporate bond research service to recommend?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because in times of narrowing spreads, lower -rated more economically sensitive corporate bonds will tend to outperform higher-rated bonds in more stable industries. Airline bonds are generally more economically sensitive than consumer staples industries such as beverages and household products and would be likely to outperform in this environment.

vignettes.topic: Economics and Investment Markets - Vignette Questions 41-44
vignettes.case_study_text:
Evelyn Gosser is an analyst tasked with estimating changes in interest rates and  risk 
premiums to help make investment decisions in various countries.  
 
For the country of Jurica, Gosser analyzes data in order to forecast changes in central 
bank policy rates and the government bond rate. She gathers the information in Exhibit 
1. Based on this data, she concludes the current policy rate is too low. 
 
Exhibit 1 
| **Actual GDP (in $ billions)** | 465 |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Potential GDP (in $ billions)** | 455 |
| **Target inflation rate** | 2.50% |
| **Inflation rate** | 2.00% |
| **Real rate that balances long-term savings and borrowing** | 0.50% |
| **Current policy rate** | 2.25% |
 
In addition to the information in Exhibit 1, Gosser gathers information on zero -coupon 
government bond rates as an input into her forecasts of the break -even inflation rate. 
The yield on a 5 -year default-free real bond is 1.8%, and the yield on a default -free 
nominal bond with the same maturity is 4.3%. 
 
Finally, Gosser considers the risk premiums and valuation of equities in the country of 
Paleotopia. The country has experienced a long period of economic growth. She believes 
that a recession is likely to occur in the near future  and recommends investing i n 
companies that are better at hedging lower consumption. 
 
Gosser notes that current P/E ratios are high by historical standards, but she  expects 
them to decrease. She justifies her expectations on future P/E ratios by linking them to 
her forecasts of an increase in inflation expectations, a decrease in equity risk premiums, 
and a decline in the real rate of interest.

questions.question_text: The break-even inflation rate in Jurica is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the difference between the yield on, for example, a zero-coupon default-free nominal bond and on a zero -coupon default-free real bond of the same maturity is known as the break-even inflation (BEI) rate. The BEI rate for Jurica is 4.30% – 1.80% = 2.50%.

questions.question_text: Based on Gosser's forecast for the Paleotopian economy, the most appropriate companies to invest in produce:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because non-cyclical companies have earnings that are relatively less sensitive to economic conditions. Non-cyclical companies include those that produce staple (or less discretionary) consumer goods. In a recession, these companies will have earnings that will not fall (on average) as other sectors, including companies in durables and cyclical sectors.

questions.question_text: Gosser's conclusion about the level of the policy rate is best supported by the difference between:
options: B. the target and actual inflation rates.
C. the current policy rate and the target inflation rate.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the actual GDP is greater than the potential GDP, creating a positive output gap, but the inflation rate is below its target rate. Using the Taylor rule, the policy rate = I t + t t + 0.5(t t – t*t) + 0.5(Y t – Y*t) and the neutral rate = It + tt, where It = real rate that balances savings and borrowing tt = inflation rate t*t = target inflation rate Yt = actual GDP output (in log scale) Y*t = potential GDP output (in log scale), and (Yt – Y*t) = output gap. Given the information in the exhibit, the neutral rate = 0.5% + 2.0% = 2.5%. However, the current policy rate is 2.25% (= 0.5% + 2.0% + 0.5 × (2.0% – 2.5%) + 0.5 × output gap = 2.25% + 0.5 × output gap). The current policy rate would be appropriate if the output gap was 0. However, the output gap is positive (Actual GDP > Potential GDP), so the current policy rate is too low.

questions.question_text: Which of Gosser's forecasts supports her expectation about changes in P/E ratios?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because high P/E ratios are likely associated with a decrease in inflation expectations, a decrease in real interest rates, and a decrease in the equity risk premium. Gosser is expecting an increase in inflation expectations, which will have a negative (decreasing) effect on P/E ratios.

vignettes.topic: Economics and Investment Markets - Vignette Questions 45-48
vignettes.case_study_text:
committee, she evaluates three bonds from different sectors (shown in Exhibit 1) for a potential new short position in the company’s hedge fund. All three bonds mature in five years. 
 
### Exhibit 1: Credit Market Observations

| Bond | Economic Sector | Debt/Capital | Enterprise Value/EBITDA | Spread to Treasuries (bps) | Credit Rating |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Bond 1** | Pharmaceutical | 52.20% | 7.2 | 255 | Baa2 |
| **Bond 2** | Consumer Discretionary | 48.30% | 7.8 | 220 | Baa1 |
| **Bond 3** | Soft Drinks | 32.30% | 8.5 | 210 | A3 |
 
Quantum is looking to enhance its equity offerings. It has recently hired David Wu to help construct a quantitative equity rotation strategy that will use economic input from the fixed-income committee. Wu has a background in quantitative modeling of equity markets and is tasked with developing an aggregate earnings forecasts. He is also working on incorporating a target equity risk premium into an equity rotation model. Wu makes the following observations based on his prior experiences: 
 
• Observation 1: The equity premium should be larger than, and positively correlated with, the corporate bond premium. 
• Observation 2: Corporate profitability is a leading economic indicator. 
• Observation 3: Equities provide superior consumption-hedging properties to high-quality bonds.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most likely impact on short -term bond prices if Quantum’s expectations regarding the payroll report are correct?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Although Quantum is forecasting a fairly low non-farm payroll number, their expectation is in line with market consensus forecasts. Although this data may be considered weak, it provides information that is anticipated and thus already reflected in asset prices. Prices would be more likely to rise or fall if the news is a surprise relative to fully anticipated information.

questions.question_text: Is Rutherford most likely correct with regard to the impact on short -term TIPS rates?
options: B. No, with regard to the impact of growth
C. No, with regard to the impact of volatility
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Short-term TIPS are a proxy for real default-free interest rates in the United States. Real default-free interest rates should be positively related to GDP growth and positively related to the expected volatility of GDP growth. Expected increases in GDP volatility would put upward pressure on short -term TIPS rates, all else being held equal.

questions.question_text: Which implied market expectation most likely accounts for the discrepancy in bond pricing that Rutherford notes?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The breakeven inflation rate incorporates both premiums for expectations about inflation and for the uncertainty of the future inflation environment.

questions.question_text: Based on Quantum’s economic forecast and the data in Exhibit 1, which bond is Coombs most likely to recommend as the short position for the hedge fund?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Bond 1 is in a non -cyclical industry, unlike Bond 2, that is in a cyclical industry. Bond 1 has a slightly higher debt -to-capital ratio than Bond 2 but not material. Bond 2 has a relatively tight spread compared with Bond 1. These factors suggest that Bond 2 is a better candidate for a short position. During an environment in which GDP is forecast to surprise to the downside, higher-rated issues are likely to outperform such as Bond 3. Given Quantum ’s expectation for declining GDP and its relatively tight spread, Bond 2 is the best candidate for a short position.

vignettes.topic: Economics and Investment Markets - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: All else equal, which of the following would most likely explain the fall in price of a particular company’s shares?
options: A. The expected inflation rate falls.
B. The company’s future cash flows are expected to increase.
C. The yield to maturity on real default-free investments rises.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. According to the fundamental pricing equation, the market value of an asset is affected by economic factors that influence the asset’s expected future cash flows, default-free interest rates, expected inflation rates, or the asset’s risk premium. From Equation 1, expected cash flows are in the numerator, while expected inflation and the real risk -free rate are in the denominator. Consequently, a rise in the real risk-free rate (the yield to maturity on a default-free instrument) will lead to a fall in the price of a risky asset, such as stock, by increasing the rate at which its cash flows are discounted.

questions.question_text: The covariance between a risk-averse investor’s inter-temporal rate of substitution and the expected future price of a risky asset is typically:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. For risk -averse investors, when the expected future price of the investment is high (low), the marginal utility of future consumption relative to that of current consumption is low (high). Hence, the covariance of the inter-temporal rate of substitution with asset price is expected to be negative for risk-averse investors.

questions.question_text: The prices of one -period, real default -free government bonds are likely to be most sensitive to changes in:
options: B. the expected volatility of economic growth.
C. the covariance between investors’ inter -temporal rates of substitution and the
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Only changes in default-free real interest rates will affect the price of real, default-free bonds. The average level of default -free real interest rates is positively related to the volatility of economic growth in the economy; thus, changes in the expected volatility of economic growth would likely lead to changes in default- free real interest rates, which in turn would affect the prices of real, default -free government bonds.

questions.question_text: Default-free real interest rates tend to be relatively high in countries with high expected economic growth because investors:
options: B. have high inter-temporal rates of substitution.
C. have high uncertainty about levels of future consumption.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The average level of default -free real interest rates is positively related to the expected rate of growth of the underlying economy and also to the volatility of economic growth in the economy. During periods of high expected economic growth, investors are less worried about the future and their consumption abilities in the future; that is, their inter -temporal rate of substitution is low, so they borrow more today and save less. Other things being equal, this means that the average level of default -free real interest rates (the reciprocal of the rate of substitution, see Equation 4) should be higher in an economy with high growth and lower in an economy with lower, more stable growth.

questions.question_text: Positive output gaps are usually associated with:
options: C. economic growth beyond sustainable capacity.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. An economy operating with a positive output gap—that is, where the level of actual GDP exceeds potential GDP —is producing beyond its sustainable capacity. Positive output gaps are usually associated with high and/or rising inflation, while high levels of unemployment usually accompany negative output gaps.

questions.question_text: All else equal, an investor expects future inflation to increase, but the uncertainty of future inflation to fall. For such an investor the break-even inflation rate:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The break-even inflation rate is the difference between the yield on a zero-coupon, default-free nominal bond and on a zero-coupon, default-free real bond of the same maturity. The rate incorporates changing expectations about inflation and changing perceptions about the uncertainty of the future inflation environment. Consequently, if inflation is expected to rise while the uncertainty about future inflation falls (in Equation 10, θ t,s rises but πt,s falls), it is unclear in which direction break-even inflation rates will move.

questions.question_text: The difference between the yield on a zero -coupon, default-free nominal bond and the yield on a zero-coupon, default-free real bond of the same maturity reflects:
options: A. investors’ expectations about future inflation only.
B. a premium for the uncertainty of future inflation only.
C. both, investors’ expectations about future inflation and a premium for the
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The difference between the yield on a zero-coupon, default-free nominal bond and the yield on a zero-coupon, default-free real bond of the same maturity is known as the break-even inflation rate. This break-even inflation rate will incorporate the inflation expectations of investors over the investment horizon of the two bonds, plus a risk premium to compensate investors for uncertainty about future inflation. Break-even inflation rates are not simply the market’s best estimate of future inflation over the relevant investment horizon, because break -even inflation rates also include a risk premium to compensate investors for their uncertainty about future inflation

questions.question_text: During a recession, the slope of the yield curve for default -free government bonds is most likely to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. During a recession, short rates are often lower because central banks tend to lower their policy rate in these times because the output gap is likely to be negative. However, the impact of such monetary policy on longer -term rates will not be as strong, so long rates may not fall by as much as short rates. The central bank will usually be expected to bring short -term rates back to normal as the recession recedes, and the risk-free rates will increase as economic growth recovers. Thus, the slope of the yield curve will typically steepen during a recession.

questions.question_text: One interpretation of an upward sloping yield curve is that the returns to short-dated bonds are:
options: B. more positively correlated with bad times than are returns to long-dated bonds.
C. more negatively correlated with bad times than are returns to long-dated bonds.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. One interpretation of an upward -sloping yield curve is that returns to short-dated bonds are more negatively correlated with bad times than are returns to long-dated bonds. This interpretation is based on the notion that investors are willing to pay a premium and accept a lower return for short -dated bonds if they believe that long-dated bonds are not a good hedge against economic “bad times.”

questions.question_text: An analyst, who measures yield as a combination of interest rates and premiums, observes an upward-sloping, default-free government bond nominal yield curve. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: A. Interest rates must be expected to rise in the future.
B. Bond risk premiums must be expected to rise in the future.
C. Expectations relating to the future direction of interest rates are indeterminate.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. An upward -sloping yield curve may be caused by a combination of expected rate increases and positive bond risk premiums. It may also be a combination of expectations that interest rates will be unchanged in the future coupled with positive bond risk premiums. Lastly, an upward -sloping yield curve may actually be a reflection of expected rate cuts that are more than offset by the existence of positive bo nd risk premiums. So, expectations relating to the future direction of interest rates are indeterminate.

questions.question_text: A corporate bond has a remaining maturity of 1 year, has a face value of EUR100, and is currently priced at EUR90.90. The real risk -free rate is 3.25%. Inflation is expected to be 2.0% next year, and the premium required by investors for inflation uncertainty is 0.25%. The implied credit risk premium embedded in the bond’s price is best described as:
options: B. 10% reduced by the real risk-free rate and expected inflation.
C. 10% reduced by the real risk-free rate, expected inflation, and the premium for
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The implied credit risk premium embedded in the bond’s price is the yield (10%) less the default-risk-free nominal interest rate, which includes a premium for inflation uncertainty. See Example 15. The credit risk premium can be calculated as 4.51% in this case: 𝛾𝑡,𝑠 𝑖 = 100 90.90 − (1 + 0.0325 + 0.02 + 0.0025) 𝛾𝑡,𝑠 𝑖 = 4.51%

questions.question_text: A decrease in the prices of AAA -rated corporate bonds during a recession would most likely be the result of:
options: B. increases in credit risk premiums.
C. increases in short-term, default-free interest rates.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. During recessions, the risk premium that investors demand on financial assets, particularly those that are not default -free, such as corporate bonds, may rise because investors in general may be less willing and able to take on heightened default risk during such periods. Specifically, the credit risk premium demanded by investors tends to rise in times of economic weakness, when the probability of a corporate default and bankruptcy is highest.

questions.question_text: During an economic period when spreads between corporate and government bonds are narrowing, and spreads between higher - and lower -rated corporate bond categories are also narrowing, it can be expected that:
options: A. government bonds will outperform corporate bonds.
B. lower-rated corporate bonds will outperform higher-rated corporate bonds.
C. higher-rated corporate bonds will outperform lower-rated corporate bonds.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. When spreads are narrowing, investors seem to be less discerning between issues with weak versus strong credit, and the rate of improvement will tend to be greater for those bonds issued by entities with a relatively weaker ability to pay. Th us, during times when corporate bond spreads are narrowing relative to government bonds and the spreads between higher- and lower-rated bond categories are also narrowing, corporate bonds will generally outperform government bonds and lower-rated corporate bonds will tend to outperform higher-rated corporate bonds.

questions.question_text: The sensitivity of a corporate bond’s spread to changes in the business cycle is most likely to be:
options: A. uncorrelated with the level of cyclicality in the company’s business.
B. positively correlated with the level of cyclicality in the company’s business.
C. negatively correlated with the level of cyclicality of the company’s business.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The sensitivity of a corporate bond’s spread to changes in the business cycle and the level of cyclicality tend to be positively correlated. The greater the level of cyclicality, the greater the sensitivity of the bond’s spread to changes in the business cycle.

questions.question_text: The category of bonds whose spreads can be expected to widen the most during an economic downturn are bonds from the:
options: A. cyclical sector with low credit ratings.
B. cyclical sector with high credit ratings.
C. non-cyclical sector with low credit ratings.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. During an economic downturn, the spreads of corporate bonds can be expected to widen, because the risk premium that investors demand on risky financial assets will increase. When spreads widen, the spreads on bonds issued by corporations with a low credit rating and that are part of the cyclical sector will tend to widen the most.

questions.question_text: With regard to the credit risk of the sovereign debt issued by country governments, which of the following statements is correct? The credit risk premium on such debt is:
options: A. zero because governments can print money to settle their debt.
B. negligibly small because no country has defaulted on sovereign debt.
C. a non -zero and positive quantity that varies depending on a country’s
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Credit premiums have been an important component of the expected return on bonds issued by countries (sovereign debt). The credit premium varies from country to country depending on how creditworthy investors consider it to be. The fact that countries have both printed money to pay back debt and defaulted on it gives rise to a non-zero credit risk premium.

questions.question_text: Risk-averse investors demanding a large equity risk premium are most likely expecting their future consumption outcomes and equity returns to be:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. If investors demand high equity risk premiums, they are likely expecting their future consumption and equity returns to be positively correlated. The positive correlation indicates that equities will exhibit poor hedging properties, because equity returns will be high (i.e., pay off) during “good times” and will be low (i.e., not pay off) during “bad times.” In other words, the covariance between risk -averse investors’ inter-temporal rates of substitution and the expected future prices of equities is highly negative, resulting in a positive and large equity risk premium. This is the case because in good times, when equity returns are high, the marginal value of consumption is low. Similarly, in bad times, when equity returns are low, the marginal value of consumption is high. Holding all else constant, the larger the magnitude of the negative covariance term, the larger the risk premium.

questions.question_text: Which of the following financial assets is likely to offer the most effective hedge against bad consumption outcomes?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The relative certainty about the real payoff from short-dated, default- free government bonds and, therefore, the relative certainty about the amount of consumption that the investor will be able to undertake with the payoff indicate that an investment in such bonds would be a good hedge against bad consumption outcomes.

questions.question_text: When assessing investment opportunities in equities, investors should:
options: A. assign higher equity risk premiums to non -cyclical companies, relative to cyclical
B. forecast lower volatility in the growth rate of earnings for cyclical companies,
C. forecast higher growth rates in earnings for cyclical companies coming out of a
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. During recessions, cyclical companies are likely to experience sharp declines in earnings, more so than non -cyclical companies. In contrast, while coming out of a recession, cyclical companies are likely to generate higher earnings growth relative to non-cyclical companies.

questions.question_text: Other things equal, equilibrium price -to-earnings ratios (P/Es) will most likely decrease if:
options: B. inflation is expected to increase.
C. there is less uncertainty about future inflation.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Other things being equal, an increase in inflation expectations would result in lower equity prices relative to current earnings. This would result in lower equilibrium P/Es.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements relating to commercial real estate is correct?
options: A. Rental income from commercial real estate is generally unstable across business
B. Commercial real estate investments generally offer a good hedge against bad
C. The key difference in the discount rates applied to the cash flows of equity
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. To arrive at an appropriate discount rate to be used to discount the cash flows from a commercial real estate investment, a liquidity premium is added to the discount rate applicable to equity investments. The added liquidity premium provides additional compensation for the risk that the real estate investment may be very illiquid in bad economic times.

questions.question_text: All else being equal, which of the following will most likely result in a rise of a company's stock price?
options: A. A fall in the stock's risk premium only
B. A rise in the expected inflation rate only
C. Both a fall in the stock's risk premium and a rise in the expected inflation rate
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the stock's risk premium represents the additional return that investors expect for investing in financial assets because of uncertainty about the asset's future cash flows. Hence, a fall in the stock's risk premium will result in a rise of the stock price.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following economic data about three countries: Country X Country Y Country Z Expected GDP growth rate 4% 6% 6% Expected volatility of the GDP growth rate 15% 10% 15% Based on this information, which country most likely has the highest real short-term interest rates?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because, when the GDP growth rate is uncertain, the short-term default- free interest rates will still be positively related to the expected growth rate of GDP, but additionally they will be positively related to the expected volatility of GDP growth. Country Z has the highest expected GDP growth rate and also the highest expected volatility of the GDP growth rate and, hence, it is likely to have the highest real short-term interest rates.

questions.question_text: The break-even inflation rate:
options: A. is the markets' best guess of future inflation.
B. includes a risk premium that is lower for longer maturities.
C. is equal to the yield spread between a real bond and a nominal bond of the same
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the difference between the yield on, for example, a zero-coupon default-free nominal bond and on a zero -coupon default-free real bond of the same maturity is known as the break-even inflation (BEI) rate.

questions.question_text: A downward-sloping yield curve most likely implies that:
options: A. investors expect inflation to rise.
B. investors expect interest rates to fall.
C. bond risk premiums are negatively correlated with time to maturity.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because a downward-sloping yield curve is probably largely the result of investor expectations of future declines in interest rates.

questions.question_text: In which of the following situations would a central bank most likely raise interest rates?
options: A. When both inflation and economic activity are too low
B. When both inflation and economic activity are too high
C. When inflation is too low and economic activity is too high
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the central banks and monetary authorities responsible for setting interest rates in an economy do so in response to the economy's position in the business cycle —cutting their policy rates when activity and/or inflation are judged to be 'too low' and raising rates when activity and/or inflation are judged to be 'too high.'

questions.question_text: Recovery rates on corporate bonds tend to be higher when the economy is expanding because:
options: C. any secured assets are likely to be sold at a higher price.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because assets that can be sold in order to recover value for bond holders are likely to fetch a higher price in a buoyant economic environment than in a stagnant one.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about three corporate bonds: Industrial Sector Credit Rating Bond X Consumer cyclical AAA Bond Y Consumer cyclical BBB Bond Z Consumer non-cyclical BBB All else being equal, which bond is most likely to have the worst performance as the economy enters a recession?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because lower-rated (BBB) bonds will underperform higher-rated (AAA) bonds and bonds of issuers in the consumer cyclical sector will underperform bonds of issuers in the consumer non -cyclical sector. Hence, Bond Y is likely to underperform Bond X and Bond Z in a recession. When spreads are narrowing relative to government bonds, the spreads between higher- and lower-rated bond categories also narrow. In these times, although corporate bonds will generally outperform government bonds, lower-rated corporate bonds will tend to outperform higher-rated bonds. The converse is true as spreads widen. Additionally, in past recessions, the spread on the consumer cyclical sector rose more dramatically than it did for corporate bonds in the consumer non-cyclical sector.

questions.question_text: Which of the following companies will be most impacted by an improving business cycle? A company that produces:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because cyclical sectors in this case are represented by companies that produce discretionary consumer goods and there is greater sensitivity to business conditions for the cyclical sector.

questions.question_text: The consumption hedging properties of equities are considered:
options: A. poor as the potential for loss is high.
B. poor as equities tend not to pay off in bad times.
C. good as equities have outperformed bonds historically.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because, when the economy turns down, so (normally) do corporate profits and it is in these bad times that investors need their investments to offset these worsening earnings. Because of the pro-cyclicality of economies and corporate profits (in aggregate ), equities are not a good hedge against bad consumption outcomes. Hence, the consumption hedging properties of equities are considered poor.

questions.question_text: If investors expect a company's earnings to grow, the company's historical P/E ratio will most likely be:
options: A. less than its forward P/E ratio.
B. equal to its forward P/E ratio.
C. greater than its forward P/E ratio.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because, when the EPS used to estimate the ratio refers to last year's earnings, the P/E is referred to as being a historical or a trailing P/E. However, when the EPS is based on an estimate of future earnings, it is referred to as the leading or forward P/E. If a company's EPS is expected to grow, then its historical P/E will be greater than its forward P/E.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes a similarity between commercial real estate and equity investments?
options: C. Returns that are correlated with the state of the economy
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the terminal value of the commercial real estate when the lease expires depends largely on the economic activity in the area, which is an equity -like feature. When general economic activity is high and thus there is strong demand for property, then the sale or redevelopment option might be worth pursuing. However, if the location is deemed to be less desirable or the economy is weak, then redevelopment may not be an option. The potential for profit or loss and the uncertainty related to this profit from redevelopment add an equity -like dimension to investment in commercial real estate.

questions.question_text: The real default-free interest rate in an economy is most likely positively related to:
options: A. the expected growth rate of GDP only.
B. the expected volatility of GDP growth only.
C. both the expected growth rate of GDP and the expected volatility of GDP growth.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because GDP growth from one period to the next cannot be perfectly anticipated. Under these uncertain circumstances, interest rates will still be positively related to the expected growth rate of GDP, but additionally they will be positively related to the expected volatility of GDP growth.

questions.question_text: The break-even inflation rate is the difference between the yield on a zero -coupon default-free nominal bond and:
options: A. nominal expected inflation for the same period.
B. the yield on a zero-coupon default-free real bond of the same maturity.
C. the premium required by investors to compensate them for uncertainty about
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the difference between the yield on, for example, a zero-coupon default-free nominal bond and on a zero -coupon default-free real bond of the same maturity is known as the break-even inflation (BEI) rate.

questions.question_text: In an environment of widening credit spreads, the credit spreads on bonds issued by corporations with a low credit rating tend to widen:
options: A. less than those of corporations with a high credit rating.
B. by the same amount as those of corporations with a high credit rating.
C. more than those of corporations with a high credit rating.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because corporate bond spreads will be driven over time by the business cycle, but the impact of the economic environment on spreads will depend on issuers' industrial sector and rating. When spreads widen, the spreads on bonds issued by corporations with a low credit rating and/or that are part of a cyclical sector will tend to widen the most.

questions.question_text: With respect to business cycle sensitivity, which of the following companies are most likely categorized as defensive?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because some companies make products or provide services that are relatively insensitive to general economic conditions. Toothpaste might fall into this category. Companies and equity sectors that produce such products are referred to as non-cyclical or defensive investments.

questions.question_text: An increase of a company's P/E multiple is most consistent with an increase in:
options: C. the expectation of future real earnings growth.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because, with regard to the basic pricing relationship, the high P/E could be the result of a number of factors, including an increase in the expectation of future real earnings growth.

questions.question_text: Which of the following aspects of investing in commercial real estate is most similar to investing in equities?
options: A. The existence of a liquid secondary market
B. The rents collected from tenants net of ownership costs
C. The uncertain profit from potential redevelopment of the real estate
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the potential for profit or loss and the uncertainty related to this profit from redevelopment add an equity -like dimension to investment in commercial real estate. In other words, this potential and uncertainty add either a positive increment to cash flow or a negative one.

questions.question_text: In efficient markets, asset values should:
options: A. fully reflect information that has been anticipated.
B. respond to news that confirm expectations of future cash flows.
C. be driven by past cash flows and current expectations of future cash flows.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because, since asset values are dependent on expectations of future cash flows, information that changes expectations affects asset values and realized returns. Information that has been anticipated is already reflected in asset prices, but information that is different from what was expected constitutes real news that requires expectations to adjust.

questions.question_text: A portfolio manager reviews the following year -over-year changes in economic indicators, released just before the stock market opens: Expected Change Reported Change Durable goods orders 4.60% 3.70% Consumer price index (CPI) 3.00% 3.00% Retail sales –1.00% 1.00% All else being equal, asset values will most likely be positively impacted by the reported data for:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the retail sales data would be considered new information since the actual result differed materially from expectations. The actual change in retail sales was better than the expected change, with the likely impact on asset values being positive. Because asset values are dependent on expectations of future cash flows, information that changes expectations affects asset values and realized returns. Information that has been anticipated is already reflected in asset prices, but information that is di fferent from what was expected constitutes real news that requires expectations to adjust. The adjustment generates a holding period return that differs from the expected return. This observation about investors’ anticipations is important to understanding sometimes seemingly counterintuitive market reactions to economic information releases. Investors judge economic data releases relative to their expectations for the data. Prices may fall (rise) despite 'good' ('bad') news if the expectation was for bette r (worse) news. Thus, for valuation, one important distinction is information that is 'news' or new information and information that has been fully anticipated. Therefore, news is a surprise relative to fully anticipated information.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 4-10
vignettes.case_study_text:
James Frazee is chief investment officer at H&F Capital Investors. Frazee hires a third-
party adviser to develop a custom benchmark for three actively managed balanced funds 
he oversees: Fund X, Fund Y, and Fund Z. (Balanced funds are funds invested in equities 
and bonds.) The benchmark needs to be composed of 60% global equities and 40% global 
bonds. The third-party adviser submits the proposed benchmark to Frazee, who rejects 
the benchmark based on the following concerns: 
 
• Concern 1: Many securities he wants to purchase are not included in the benchmark 
portfolio. 
• Concern 2:  One position in the benchmark portfolio will be somewhat costly to 
replicate. 
• Concern 3:  The benchmark portfolio is a float -adjusted, capitalization -weighted 
portfolio. 
 
After the third-party adviser makes adjustments to the benchmark to alleviate Frazee’s 
concerns, Frazee accepts the benchmark portfolio. He then asks his research staff to 
develop risk and expected return forecasts for Funds X, Y, and Z as well as for the 
benchmark. The forecasts are presented in Exhibit 1. 
 
**Exhibit 1:**
Forecasted Portfolio Statistics for Funds X, Y, and Z and the Benchmark 
  Fund X Fund Y Fund Z Benchmark 
Portfolio weights:         
| Forecasted Portfolio Statistics for | Funds X, Y, and | Z and the Benchmark | Fund X Fund Y | Fund Z Benchmark Portfolio weights: |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Global equities (%)** | 60 | 65 | 68 | 60 |
| **Global bonds (%)** | 40 | 35 | 32 | 40 |
| **Expected return (%)** | 10.0 | 11.6 | 13.2 | 9.4 |
| **Expected volatility (%)** | 17.1 | 18.7 | 22.2 | 16.3 |
| **Active risk (%)** | 5.2 | 9.2 | 15.1 | N/A |
| **Sharpe ratio (SR)** | 0.45 | 0.50 | 0.49 | 0.44 |
| **Note: Data are based on a** | risk-free | rate | of | 2.3%. |
| **model: Fund W would now have maximum over - and underweight** | constraints | of | 7% | on |

Frazee decides to add a fourth offering to his group of funds, Fund W, which will use the same benchmark as in Exhibit 1. Frazee estimates Fund W’s information ratio to be 0.35. He is considering adding the following constraint to his portfolio constructio n
single-country positions. 
 
Frazee conducts a search to hire a manager for the global equity portion of Fund W and 
identifies three candidates. He asks the candidates to prepare risk and return forecasts 
relative to Fund W’s benchmark based on their investment strategy, with the only  
constraint being no short selling. Each candidate develops independent annual forecasts 
with active return projections that are uncorrelated and constructs a portfolio made up 
of stocks that are diverse both geographically and across economic sectors. Selected 
data for the three candidates’ portfolios are presented in Exhibit 2. 
 
**Exhibit 2:**
Forecasted Portfolio Data for Equity Portion of Fund W 
  Candidate A Candidate B Candidate C 
Rebalancing Annually Annually Annually 
| Forecasted Portfolio Data for | Equity Portion of Fund | W Candidate A Candidate | B Candidate C Rebalancing Annually Annually Annually |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of securities** | 100 | 64 | 36 |
| **Information ratio (IR)** | 0.582 | 0.746 | 0.723 |
| **Transfer coefficient (TC)** | 0.832 | 0.777 | 0.548 |
| **Information coefficient*** | 0.07 | 0.12 | 0.22 |
* Information coefficient based on previously managed funds. 
 
Frazee asks Candidate C to re-evaluate portfolio data given the following changes: 
• Change 1: Fix the number of securities to 50. 
• Change 2: Rebalance on a semi-annual basis. 
• Change 3: Add maximum over- or underweight constraints on sector weightings.

questions.question_text: Which of Frazee’s concerns best justifies his decision to reject the proposed benchmark?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Because the benchmark does not contain many assets that Frazee wants to invest in, the benchmark may not be representative of his investment approach. Concern 2, as stated, is less important because it does not imply that the cost of replicating the benchmark is a serious concern. Finally, Concern 3 actually states a generally positive feature of the benchmark.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, the expected active return from asset allocation for Fund X is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Active return from asset allocation is derived from differences between the benchmark weight and the portfolio weight across asset classes. For Fund X, the expected active return from asset allocation is calculated as ∑ Δ𝑤𝑗𝑅𝐵,𝑗 𝑀 𝑗=1 = (60 − 60)𝑅𝐵,𝑒 + (40 − 40)𝑅𝐵,𝑏 = 0 , where Δ wj is the difference in the active portfolio and the benchmark asset weights, RB,e is the benchmark’s return from global equities, and RB,b is the benchmark’s return from global bonds. Because Fund X has the same asset weights as the benchmark across the two asset classes (60% global equities, 40% global bonds), the expected active return from asset allocation is zero.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, which fund is expected to produce the greatest consistency of active return?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The IR measures the consistency of active return. The IR is calculated for the three funds as follows: 𝐼𝑅 = 𝑅𝑃 − 𝑅𝐵 σ(𝑅𝑃 − 𝑅𝐵) = 𝑅𝐴 σ𝐴 . IR for Fund X = (10.0 − 9.4)/5.2 = 0.6/5.2 = 0.12. IR for Fund Y = (11.6 − 9.4)/9.2 = 2.2/9.2 = 0.24. IR for Fund Z = (13.2 − 9.4)/15.1 = 3.8/15.1 = 0.25. Fund Z has the largest IR and thus is expected to produce the greatest consistency of active return.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, combining Fund W with a fund that replicates the benchmark would produce a Sharpe ratio closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Given the IR for Fund W of 0.35 and the benchmark’s SR of 0.44, the combination of the benchmark portfolio and Fund W would produce an SR of 0.56, calculated as follows: 𝑆𝑅𝑃 2 = 𝑆𝑅𝐵 2 + 𝐼𝑅2; SRP = (0.442 + 0.352)0.5 = 0.56.

questions.question_text: If Frazee added the assumption he is considering in Fund W’s portfolio construction, it would most likely result in:
options: A. a decrease in the optimal aggressiveness of the active strategy.
B. the information ratio becoming invariant to the level of active risk.
C. an increase in the transfer of active return forecasts into active weights.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The new assumption adds constraints to Fund W. The IR for a constrained portfolio generally decreases with the aggressiveness of the strategy because portfolio constraints reduce the transfer of active return forecasts into active weights. Furthermore, the optimal active risk is given by the following formula: σ𝐴 = 𝑇𝐶 𝐼𝑅 𝑆𝑅𝐵 σ𝐵. The addition of portfolio constraints reduces the TC, thus also reducing the optimal active risk. So, having maximum over - and underweight constraints on single -country positions decreases the optimal aggressiveness of the active management strategy.

questions.question_text: Based on the data presented in Exhibit 2, the candidate with the greatest skill at achieving active returns appears to be:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The IR measures the consistency of active return generation. A higher ratio generally indicates better managerial skill at achieving active returns on a risk- adjusted basis. The IR for Candidate B (0.746) is higher than the IR for Candidate A (0.582) and Candidate C (0.723). Thus, Candidate B appears to have the greatest skill, as indicated by the highest IR of 0.746.

questions.question_text: Which proposed change to Fund W would most likely decrease Candidate C’s information ratio?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The IR is calculated as 𝐼𝑅 = (𝑇𝐶)(𝐼𝐶)√𝐵𝑅, where BR is breadth. Change 3, establishing new constraints of caps on the over - and underweight of sectors, reduces the correlation of optimal active weights with the actual active weights, which results in a decreased TC and thus a decrease in the IR. Change 1 (increasing portfolio size from 36 to 50) and Change 2 (increasing the frequency of rebalancing from annually to semi -annually) would both likely have the effect of increasing the BR of the portfolio, which would increase the IR.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 11-14
vignettes.case_study_text:
John Martinez is assessing the performance of the actively managed diversified asset 
portfolio. The diversified asset portfolio is invested in equities, bonds, and real estate, 
and allocations to these asset classes and to the holdings within them are constrained. 
 
Selected return and financial data for the portfolio for 2019 are presented in Exhibit 1. 
 
**Exhibit 1:**
Diversified Asset Portfolio 2019 Portfolio Performance 
  
Sub-Portfolio 
Return (%) 
Benchmark 
Return (%) 
Portfolio 
Allocation (%) 
Strategic Asset 
Allocation (%) 
| Item | Value 1 | Value 2 | Value 3 | Value 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Equities sub-portfolio** | 36.9 | 31.6 | 63 | 60 |
| **Bond sub-portfolio** | −2.4 | −2.6 | 28 | 35 |
| **Real estate sub-portfolio** | 33.4 | 28.3 | 9 | 5 |

 
**Exhibit 2:**
Diversified Asset Portfolio Data, 2000-2019 
  Transfer 
Coefficient (TC) 
Information 
Coefficient (IC) 
Breadth 
(BR) 
Equities sub-portfolio 0.90 0.091 21 
Bond sub-portfolio 0.79 0.087 23 
Real estate sub-portfolio 0.86 0.093 19 
 
Martinez has recently hired Kenneth Singh to help him evaluate portfolios. Martinez asks 
Singh about the possible effects on the portfolio’s information ratio if cash were added 
to the diversified asset portfolio or if the aggressiveness of the portfolio’s  active 
weights were increased. Singh responds with two statements: 
• **Statement 1:**
Adding cash to the portfolio would change the portfolio’s information 
ratio. 
• **Statement 2:**
Increasing the aggressiveness of active weights would not change the 
portfolio’s information ratio.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, the value added to the diversified asset portfolio attributable to the security selection decision in 2019 was closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Based on the differences in returns for the portfolio and benchmark in Exhibit 1, the value added by each asset class within the portfolio is shown in the following table: Sub-Portfolio Return (%) Benchmark Return (%) Value Added (%) Portfolio Allocation (%) Equities sub-portfolio 36.9 31.6 5.3 63 Bond sub-portfolio −2.4 −2.6 0.2 28 Real estate sub- portfolio 33.4 28.3 5.1 9 The value added from security selection is calculated as the sum of the actual portfolio weights multiplied by each sub -portfolio’s value added measure. Thus, the value added from security selection is calculated as: Value added from security selection = 0.63(5.3%) + 0.28(0.2%) + 0.09(5.1%) = 3.9%. A is incorrect. It represents the value added from asset allocation (2.3%). C is incorrect. It represents the total value added (2.3% + 3.9% = 6.1%, with rounding).

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, the value added of the diversified asset portfolio attributable to the asset allocation decision in 2019 was closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The value added from asset allocation is calculated as the sum of the differences in the weights between the strategic (benchmark) allocation and the actual sub-portfolio allocation multiplied by each sub-portfolio’s benchmark return. Benchmark Return (%) Actual Asset Allocation (%) Strategic Asset Allocation (%) Actual − Strategic Asset Allocation (%) Equities sub- portfolio 31.6 63 60 +3 Bond sub-portfolio −2.6 28 35 −7 Real estate sub- portfolio 28.3 9 5 +4 Thus, the value added by the active asset allocation decision is calculated as Value added from asset allocation decision = 0.03(31.6%) − 0.07(−2.6%) + 0.04(28.3%) = 2.3%. B is incorrect. It is the value added from security selection. C is incorrect. It is the total value added.

questions.question_text: Based on data in Exhibit 2 and using the information ratio as the criterion for evaluating performance, which sub-portfolio had the best performance in the period 2000–2019?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The information ratio for a portfolio can be expressed as follows: 𝐼𝑅 = (𝑇𝐶)(𝐼𝐶)√𝐵𝑅. The information ratios for the three sub-portfolios are calculated as follows: Information Ratio Equities sub-portfolio 0.90 × 0.091 × (21)0.5 = 0.38 Bond sub-portfolio 0.79 × 0.087 × (23)0.5 = 0.33 Real estate sub-portfolio 0.86 × 0.093 × (19)0.5 = 0.35 Based on the information ratio, the equities sub -portfolio outperformed the real estate sub-portfolio. The information ratio for the equities sub-portfolio of 0.38 was higher than the information ratio for the real estate sub -portfolio of 0.35 and the bond sub-portfolio of 0.33.

questions.question_text: Which of Singh’s statements regarding the information ratio is correct?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The information ratio for a portfolio of risky assets will generally shrink if cash is added to the portfolio. Because the diversified asset portfolio is an unconstrained portfolio, its information ratio would be unaffected by an increase in the aggressiveness of active weights.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 18-19
vignettes.case_study_text:
S&P 500 Indigo Fund 
Expected annual return 9.00% 10.50% 
Return standard deviation 18.00% 25.00% 
Sharpe ratio 0.333 0.30 
Active return   1.20% 
Active risk   8.00% 
Information ratio   0.15 
Note: Data are based on a risk-free rate of 2.3%.

questions.question_text: What is the maximum Sharpe ratio that a manager can achieve by combining the S&P 500 benchmark portfolio and the Indigo Fund?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The highest squared Sharpe ratio of an actively managed portfolio is 𝑆𝑅𝑃 2 = 𝑆𝑅𝐵 2 + 𝐼𝑅2 = 0.3332 + 0.152 = 0.1334. The highest Sharpe ratio is SRP = √0.1334 = 0.365.

questions.question_text: Which of the following pairs of weights would be used to achieve the highest Sharpe ratio and optimal amount of active risk through combining the Indigo Fund and benchmark portfolio, respectively?
options: A. 1.014 on Indigo and −0.014 on the benchmark
B. 1.450 on Indigo and −0.450 on the benchmark
C. 1.500 on Indigo and −0.500 on the benchmark
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The optimal amount of active risk is σ𝐴 = 𝐼𝑅 𝑆𝑅𝐵 σ𝐵 = 0.15 0.333 18.0% = 8.11%. The weight on the active portfolio (Indigo) would be 8.11%/8.0% = 1.014, and the weight on the benchmark portfolio would be 1 − 1.014 = −0.014. We can demonstrate that these weights achieve the maximum Sharpe ratio (of 0.365). Note that 8.11% is the optimal level of active risk and that Indigo has an expected active return of 1.014(1.2%) = 1.217% over the benchmark and a total excess return of 6.0% + 1.217% = 7.217%. The portfolio total risk is σP 2 = σB 2 + σA 2 = 18.02 + 8.1112 = 389.788. Taking the square root, σ P = 19.743, and the optimal Sharpe ratio is indeed 7.217/19.743 = 0.365.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 25-26
vignettes.case_study_text:
You are analyzing three investment managers for a new mandate. The following table 
provides the managers’ ex -ante active return expectations and portfolio weights. The 
last two columns include the risk and the  ex post, realized active returns for the four 
stocks. Use the following data for the following two questions: 
  
Manager 1 Manager 2 Manager 3 
Risk Realized RA Δw E(RA) Δw E(RA) Δw E(RA) 
Security 1 −0.125 0.03 0.2 0.04 −0.05 0.025 0.17 0.06 
Security 2 0.025 0.04 0 0.01 0.05 0.015 0.10 0.07 
Security 3 0.075 0.05 −0.1 0 0.05 0.005 0.12 0.04 
Security 4 0.025 0.06 −0.1 0.02 −0.05 0.015 0.25 0.02

questions.question_text: Suppose all three managers claim to be good at forecasting returns. According to the expanded fundamental law of active management, which manager is the best at efficiently building portfolios by anticipating future returns?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The proper statistic to calculate is the information coefficient, and it is defined as follows: 𝐼𝐶 = ρ (𝑅𝐴𝑖 σ𝑖 , μ𝑖 σ𝑖 ). A manager is a good forecaster if his or her ex ante, active return expectations (forecasts) are highly correlated with the realized active returns. The information coefficient requires that these forecasts and realized returns be risk -weighted. When this is done for the three managers, the risk-weighted forecasts and realized returns are: Risk-Weighted Forecasts, μi/σi RAi/σi Manager 1 Manager 2 Manager 3 Realized Security 1 0.176 0.235 0.147 0.353 Security 2 0.400 0.100 0.150 0.700 Security 3 0.417 0.000 0.042 0.333 Security 4 0.240 0.080 0.060 0.080 The ICs are found by calculating the correlations between each manager’s forecasts and the realized risk-weighted returns. The three managers have the following ICs: Manager 1 Manager 2 Manager 3 Information coefficient 0.5335 0.0966 0.6769 Manager 3 has the highest IC.

questions.question_text: Suppose all three managers claim to be efficient in portfolio construction. According to the expanded fundamental law of active management, which manager is the best at building portfolios to make full use of their ability to correctly anticipate returns?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The proper statistic to calculate is the transfer coefficient, and it is defined as follows: TC = ρ(μi/σi,Δwiσi) The TC is the cross -sectional correlation between the forecasted active security returns and the actual active weights, adjusted for risk. Risk-Weighted Forecasts, μi/σi Risk-Adjusted Weights, Δwiσi Manager 1 Manager 2 Manager 3 Manager 1 Manager 2 Manager 3 Security 1 0.1765 0.2353 0.1471 −0.0213 0.0340 −0.0085 Security 2 0.4000 0.1000 0.1500 0.0025 0.0000 0.0050 Security 3 0.4167 0.0000 0.0417 0.0090 −0.0120 0.0060 Security 4 0.2400 0.0800 0.0600 0.0063 −0.0250 −0.0125 The three managers have the following TCs: Manager 1 Manager 2 Manager 3 Transfer coefficient 0.7267 0.8504 −0.0020 Manager 2 has the highest TC.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 30-33
vignettes.case_study_text:
Steven Smith, the CFO at Brickman Industries, is considering replacing  the current 
portfolio manager of the firm's pension assets. He has identified  three alternative 
managers as a possible replacement.  
 
Smith first reviews the current manager's portfolio, which is invested in an equity fund 
and a fixed-income fund. Exhibit 1 shows the portfolio's strategic asset allocation and 
current allocation to these funds as well as the return on the two funds and th eir 
benchmarks. 
 
Exhibit 1 
  Benchmark 
Return 
Strategic Asset 
Allocation 
Fund 
Return 
Manager's 
Allocation 
| Benchmark | Return | Strategic | Asset | Allocation Fund Return Manager's Allocation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Equity fund** | 14% | 60% | 17% | 50% |
| **Fixed-income fund** | 6% | 40% | 4% | 50% |

Smith next collects information on the alternative managers and uses both the Sharpe ratio and the information ratio for his initial analysis. As part of this analysis, he

determines what factors would impact both of these ratios. Smith then collects select data about the three portfolio managers and summarizes his
findings in Exhibit 2. 
 
Exhibit 2 
  Active 
Return 
Active 
Risk 
Sharpe 
Ratio 
Information 
Coefficient 
Transfer 
Coefficient 
| Active | Return | Active | Risk | Sharpe | Ratio | Information Coefficient Transfer Coefficient |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Manager** | 1 | 9.40% | 17.30% | 0.63 | 0.57 | 0.70 |
| **Manager** | 2 | 8.80% | 15.40% | 0.51 | 0.68 | 0.20 |
| **Manager** | 3 | 8.20% | 14.90% | 0.70 | 0.28 | 1.00 |

questions.question_text: The current manager's value added from asset allocation is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the value added from asset allocation is equal to the sum of the differences in the weights between the strategic allocation and the actual portfolio allocation (Δw) multiplied by the respective benchmark return (RB). RA = (ΔwequityRB,equity + Δwfixed incomeRB,fixed income) RA = (50% – 60%)(0.14) + (50% – 40%)(0.06) = –0.014 + 0.006 = –0.008= –0.8%.

questions.question_text: Which of the following factors impacts both of the ratios that Smith uses in his initial analysis of the alternative managers?
options: A. The choice of benchmark portfolio
B. The volatility of the portfolio's returns
C. The degree of leverage in the portfolio
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because both the Sharpe ratio and the information ratio would be impacted by the volatility of returns of the portfolio. Sharpe ratio - The Sharpe ratio is used to compare the portfolio return in excess of a riskless rate with the volatility of the portfolio return. SRP = (RP – RF)/σP, where RP = Portfolio return, R F = the risk free rate, and σP = the standard deviation of the portfolio, Information ratio - The information ratio of an actively managed portfolio is calculated by dividing the active return by active risk, where active risk is the standard deviation (volatility) of the excess return of the portfolio over the return of the benchmark.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 2, which manager has the highest information ratio?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the information ratio (IR) is the active return / the active risk. The IR for each manager is as follows: Manager 1 = 9.4/17.3 = 0.5434 Manager 2 = 8.8/15.4 = 0.5714 Manager 3 = 8.2/14.9 = 0.5503. Thus, Manager 2 has the highest information ratio.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 2, which manager most likely has the fewest investment constraints?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a low TC results from the formal or informal constraints imposed on the structure of the portfolio. In fact, at TC = 0.00, there would be no correspondence between the active return forecasts and active weights taken and thus no expectation of value added from active management. In contrast, TC = 1.00 (no binding constraints) represents a perfect correspondence between active weights taken and forecasted active returns. Since Manager 3 has the highest transfer coefficient, they most likely have the fewest portfolio constraints.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 34-37
vignettes.case_study_text:
Anna Bauer runs a small family office investment firm. She is reviewing several active 
portfolio managers for potential inclusion into her portfolio. 
 
Bauer identifies three unconstrained, diversified fixed -income portfolios with three 
different managers as potential investments. Each manager has the same benchmark. To 
evaluate the managers, Bauer reviews the annual performance data in Exhibit 1 and 
| Characteristic | Value 1 |
| :--- | :--- |
| **assumes a risk-free rate of** | 0.50%. |
| **Return 8.75% 8.80% 9.40%** | 10.50% |
| **Standard deviation of returns 17.50% 17.60% 19.20%** | 24.20% |
| **Active risk 0.00% 3.06% 3.32%** | 9.46% |

Exhibit 1 Benchmark Portfolio A Portfolio B Portfolio C Bauer also evaluates Portfolio D. The portfolio has no investment constraints and is invested in an equity fund and a fixed -income fund. Exhibit 2 shows Portfolio D's strategic asset allocation and its current allocation to these funds as well as the return of the funds and their two benchmarks. Exhibit 2 Portfolio D
Allocation 
Strategic Asset 
Allocation Fund Return Benchmark 
Return 
Fixed-income fund 70% 50% 10.00% 8.00% 
Equity fund 30% 50% 12.00% 10.50% 
 
Bauer next examines the annual performance data in Exhibit 3 in order to determine the 
level of active risk that would maximize the Sharpe ratio of Portfolio D. 
 
| level of active risk that would | maximize the Sharpe ratio of Portfolio D. |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Number of securities 60** | 50 |
| **Standard deviation of returns 12.30%** | 13.80% |
| **Sharpe ratio 0.71** | 0.73 |

Exhibit 3 Benchmark Portfolio D Information ratio --- 0.23 Bauer meets with the manager of Portfolio D. She informs the manager that the family office will require ESG considerations to be incorporated into all of their investments going forward. The manager considers how the imposition of ESG constraints will affect their performance as measured by the fundamental law of active management.

questions.question_text: Which portfolio in Exhibit 1 has the highest information ratio?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the information ratio tells the investor how much active return has been earned, or is expected to be earned, for incurring the level of active risk. Active return, R A, is the difference between the managed portfolio return, R P, and the benchmark portfolio return, R B. The information ratio of an actively managed portfolio, IR, is calculated by dividing the active return, RA, by active risk σA: IR = (RP – RB)/σ(RP – RB) = RA/σA. Using the figures presented in Exhibit 1: For Portfolio A, IR = (8.80% – 8.75%)/3.06% = 0.05%/3.06% = 0.016 For Portfolio B, IR = (9.40% – 8.75%)/3.32% = 0.65%/3.32% = 0.196 For Portfolio C, IR = (10.50% – 8.75%)/9.46% = 1.75%/9.46% = 0.185 Therefore, Portfolio B has the highest information ratio.

questions.question_text: The value added to Portfolio D that is attributable to security selection is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the value added due to security selection is the product of the active return R A = R P – RB and the allocated weight to the fixed -income and equity funds, respectively; 70%(10.00% – 8.00%) + 30%(12.00% – 10.50%) = 1.40% + 0.45% = 1.85% ≈ 1.9%.

questions.question_text: The level of active risk that would maximize the Sharpe ratio of Portfolio D is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because for unconstrained portfolios, the level of active risk that leads to the highest Sharpe ratio is σA = IR/SRB × σB, where σB is the standard deviation of the benchmark's return, IR is the portfolio's information ratio and SRB is the benchmark's Sharpe ratio. Using the information presented in Exhibit 2, σA = 0.23/0.71 × 12.30% = 0.0398 ≈ 4.0%.

questions.question_text: In terms of the fundamental law, the family office's investment requirements will most likely lower Portfolio D's:
options: C. active risk and transfer coefficient.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Portfolio D is currently unconstrained and therefore its transfer coefficient equals 1; the planned addition of ESG constraint would reduce this. Like the information coefficient, the transfer coefficient, TC, is a simple multiplicative factor in the fundamental law. It measures the extent to which constraints reduce the expected value added of the investor’s forecasting ability. In the absence of constraints, the transfer coefficient is approximately 1.00, resulting in the basic form of the fundam ental law. However, in practice, investors often work under constraints that result in TC values between 0.20 and 0.80.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 38-41
vignettes.case_study_text:
Paula Mackie is a portfolio manager with a family office. She is reviewing the 
performance of three managers of active portfolios over the past year. 
 
First, Mackie considers Manager 1's portfolio, which is invested in an equity fund and a 
fixed-income fund. Exhibit 1 shows Manager 1's strategic asset allocation and current 
allocation to these funds as well as the return on the two funds and their benchmarks. 
 
Exhibit 1 
| Item | Value 1 | Value 2 | Value 3 | Value 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Equity fund | 10.00% | 12.00% | 50% | 60% |
| Fixed-income fund | 3.00% | 1.00% | 50% | 40% |
 
Mackie meets with Manager 2, who runs an unconstrained global equities portfolio. 
Mackie informs Manager 2 that the family office will require ESG considerations to be 
incorporated into all of their investments going forward. The manager considers how the 
imposition of ESG constraints will affect portfolio performance as measured by the 
fundamental law of active management. 
 
After her discussion with Manager 2, Mackie does further research into the strengths 
and limitations of the fundamental law of active management and makes the following 
observations: 
 
• Observation 1: If returns are not independent, the number of assets in a portfolio 
is not an adequate measure of breadth. 
• Observation 2: The fundamental law requires the empirical validity of the CAPM. 
 
Lastly, Mackie gathers information about Manager 3's portfolio in Exhibit 2. She uses 
the fundamental law of active management to evaluate the portfolio's performance.

questions.question_text: The value added to Manager 1's portfolio attributable to security selection is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the value added by security selection is the product of the active return (RA), which is the return on each underlying fund (RP) less the return on its respective benchmark (RB), and the allocated weight given to each fund, respectively. In this case: 0.6(0.10 – 0.12) + 0.4(0.03 – 0.01) = –1.2% + 0.8% = –0.4%.

questions.question_text: In terms of the fundamental law of active management, the family office's investment requirements will most likely lower Manager 2's:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because Manager 2's portfolio is currently unconstrained and therefore its transfer coefficient equals 1, the planned addition of ESG constraint would reduce this. Like the information coefficient, the transfer coefficient, TC, is a simple multiplicative factor in the fundamental law. It measures the extent to which constraints reduce the expected value added of the investor’s forecasting ability. In the absence of constraints, the transfer coefficient is approximately 1.00, resulting in the basic form of the fundamental law. However, in practice, investors often work under constraints that result in TC values between 0.20 and 0.80.

questions.question_text: Are both of Mackie's observations about the fundamental law of active management correct?
options: B. No, only Observation 1 is correct
C. No, only Observation 2 is correct
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the number of individual assets, N, is not an adequate measure of strategy breadth, BR, when the active returns between individual assets are correlated, as defined by the risk model, and forecasts are not independent from period to period. Another example of the limitation of the fundamental law due to the lack of decision independence is the active management of fixed -income portfolios. Statement 2 is incorrect because the fundamental law does not require the empirical validity of the CAPM, the multi-factor APT (arbitrage pricing theory), or any other equilibrium theory of required returns.

questions.question_text: Based on the fundamental law of active management, Manager 3's information ratio is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the fundamental law states that E(RA) = (IC)(TC)(BR)0.5σA, where E(RA) is the expected active return, IC is the information coefficient, TC is the transfer coefficient, BR is the breadth, and σA is the active portfolio risk. Since the Information ratio is E(RA)/σA, from the above equation it can be shown that the IR = (IC)(TC)(BR)0.5 = (0.09)(0.7)(9)0.5 = 0.189 ≈ 0.19.

vignettes.topic: Analysis of Active Portfolio Management - Vignette Questions 42-47
vignettes.case_study_text:
Matthew Spencer is a senior consultant in the institutional consulting division of Reed & 
Associates (Reed). Reed advises its institutional clients on asset manager selection and 
provides ongoing evaluation and performance analysis of all the fund managers  in its 
database. Spencer was recently engaged by the retirement fund of a large municipality 
that has ten managers, each specializing in a particular asset class or style. Spencer 
notes that the members of the fund's investment committee vary greatly in t heir level 
of understanding of financial markets and active portfolio management. He hopes to 
increase their comprehension during a meeting scheduled for the following day. 
 
At the start of the meeting, Spencer provides the investment committee members with 
a report outlining the three-year historical performance and value-added statistics for 
the fund's ten managers. He highlights the fund’s best- and worst-performing managers 
for the same period. David Moore, a new committee member, asks Spencer how to 
interpret value added statistics. Spencer responds with the following statements. 
 
• **Statement 1:**
The measurement is derived from the various sources that make up 
the contribution from active management decisions, including asset allocation and 
security selection. 
• **Statement 2:**
It appraises active management decisions and further compares the 
portfolio’s return in excess of the benchmark with the risk-free rate and relative to 
the standard deviation of returns. 
• **Statement 3:**
The statistic is a useful tool for comparing relative returns, but it is 
most applicable in comparing active management decisions of equity portfolios. 
 
Spencer wants to ensure the committee understands the amount of active management 
risk its equity growth fund managers are taking in the portfolios. He mentions he is 
particularly concerned about the risk statistics for Atlanta Asset Management. The 
statistics have him questioning whether the manager is actually adding value by following 
an active management strategy. He provides the committee with data presented in 
Exhibit 1. 
 
Exhibit 1 Selected Data for Atlanta Asset Management 
Benchmark return –9.0% 
Portfolio return –11.0%

questions.question_text: Which of Spencer’s statements responding to Moore’s question is most likely correct?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Value added is determined by deviations from benchmark performance and arises from deviation from portfolio benchmark weights. This calculation can be further broken down into the various sources that make up the contribution from active management decision, including asset allocation and security selection.
