Avignon Corporation Selected Financial Information as of Year-End 2016 Shares outstanding 200 million

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, Avignon’s current dividend policy is best described as a:
options: C. constant dividend payout ratio policy.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Avignon’s current dividend policy would be classified as stable because it has paid the same amount for the past three years regardless of earnings volatility.

questions.question_text: If the analysts’ beliefs about the increase in sales are correct, the change in dividend policy that Avignon would most likely make would be to:
options: B. increase the quarterly dividend amount.
C. cut the quarterly dividend in anticipation of next year’s sales forecast.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. If analysts are correct that the change in sales is temporary, the company is most likely to declare a special dividend. Companies, particularly in cyclical industries, may choose to use special dividends to distribute more earnings during strong earning years.

questions.question_text: Which of Lee’s statements to Carlyle about share repurchases is least accurate?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Statement 1 is least accurate. Although the amount of cash distributed is the same, only those shareholders who have their shares purchased by the company will receive any cash.

questions.question_text: If SpeedyPro had used all of its surplus cash to repurchase its shares, based on Exhibit 2, the percentage increase in EPS would have been closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The increase in EPS was 25%, calculated as follows: Steps Calculation EPS EPS before the repurchase from Exhibit 2 --- $1.24 Surplus cash available for repurchase --- $836 million --- # share outstanding before repurchase --- 100 million --- Share price at time of repurchase --- $38.00 --- Price premium for repurchase --- 10% --- Repurchase price per share $38.00 × 110% $41.80 --- # shares repurchased $836 million ÷ $41.80 per share 20 million --- # shares outstanding after repurchase 100 million – 20 million 80 million --- EPS after the repurchase $124 million ÷ 80 million shares --- $1.55 % increase in EPS ($1.55 – $1.24) ÷ $1.24 --- 25%

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 2, SpeedyPro most likely repurchased shares using:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. SpeedyPro offered a 10% premium over the current price. This is most consistent with a fixed -price tender offer, which normally requires a premium. Negotiated purchase agreements are almost as likely to take place at prices lower than market because they are at prices above market, particularly when shareholders are trying to meet liquidity needs. Open market purchases are market based and can be timed to avoid price impact.

questions.question_text: The best answer to Carlyle’s question about the potential violation of the debt covenants is that the covenant:
options: A. is not violated if Avignon repurchases shares.
B. will be violated if Avignon uses debt to finance the repurchase.
C. will be violated if Avignon uses the surplus cash to finance the repurchase.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The debt-financed repurchase increases the debt-to-equity ratio above the 35% threshold and thus violates the debt covenant. Book value of equity (millions) Exhibit 3 C$3,600 Cash available for purchase (millions) Exhibit 3 C$155 Debt-to-equity ratio (D/E) before Exhibit 3 30.00% Book value of debt (millions) 30% × C$3,600 C$1,080 D/E with cash repurchase C$1,080 ÷ (C$3,600 – C$155) 31.30% D/E with debt-financed repurchase (C$1,080 + C$155) ÷ (C$3,600 – C$155) 35.80%

vignettes.module: Analysis of Dividends and Share Repurchases
vignettes.topic: Analysis of Dividends and Share Repurchases - Vignette Questions 36-39
vignettes.case_study_text:
Michael Thornhill is CFO at American Automotive Parts & Service Corporation (AAPS), 
an automotive manufacturer that specializes in engine components in a number of 
established vehicle production lines. He is preparing for a meeting with members of the 
executive committee to discuss and prepare recommendations for the board to 
determine if AAPS should make any changes to its dividend policy and structure over the 
next fiscal year. 
In preparation for this meeting, Thornhill summarizes AAPS's  historical dividends and 
related information in Exhibit 1. 
 
| Characteristic | Value 1 |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Dividend amount, current** | $0.50/share |
| **Number of shares outstanding** | 1,00,00,000 |
| **Target payout ratio** | 20% |
| **Corporate tax rate** | 35% |
| **Share price – 52-week low-high $7.84 –** | $10.62 |
| **Share price – closing** | $9.89 |
| **potential investments options** | and | suggests | that | the | company | consider | a | 4% | stock |

parts division. The sale generated $29 million in cash proceeds. Thornhill recommends that the company issue a one-time dividend of $0.95/share to existing shareholders.

Exhibit 1 Dividend frequency Quarterly In the meeting, the first topic discussed is the recently completed sale of AAPS's brake The Chief Operating Officer (COO) highlights that the company has a number of dividend instead. Thornhill agrees to prepare a summary of the effect the stock dividend would have on the company's results assuming a purchase price per share based on the current market price. The following statements about the stock dividend are discussed:
 
• **Statement 1:**
The stock dividend would be negatively received by shareholders 
because it would increase their tax liability.   
• **Statement 2:**
The stock dividend would have a more adverse effect on the company's 
financial leverage ratios which are being closely monitored by the bank. 
• **Statement 3:**
The flotation costs of the stock dividend would be lower than a 
traditional issuance and should be considered in their analysis. 
 
As a result of the extended discussion and different perspectives on dividend payout 
structures, Thornhill suggests an alternative approach could be a share buyback 
transaction. The following statements are made: 
 
• **Statement 1:**
AAPS should negotiate a greenmail transaction directly with a major 
activist shareholder in order to secure a discount to the current market price. 
• **Statement 2:**
If there is a concern  about the perception of excluding other 
shareholders with the greenmail transaction, a  fixed price tender offer could be 
accomplished quickly and allow them to purchase a pro rata portion of shares from all 
interested shareholders.   
• **Statement 3:**
AAPS should announce an open market repurchase program as it would 
provide flexibility to cancel the share purchases if the company is able to identify 
another project that would be a more effective use of funds.

questions.question_text: The one-time dividend Thornhill recommends is best described as a:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a liquidating dividend arises when a company sells a portion of its business for cash and the proceeds are distributed to shareholders. In this case, AAPS sold a division and distributed a portion of the proceeds back to shareholders and the one-time $0.95/share dividend is a liquidating dividend.

questions.question_text: If AAPS follows the COO's suggestion, the expected share price after the stock dividend is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. For a stock dividend, the total market value of the company is unaffected by the stock dividend because the decrease in the share price is exactly offset by the increase in the number of shares outstanding. The total cost basis of the shares remains the same; however, the number of shares owned increases. Assuming a shareholder owns 100 shares. Total value = $989.00 [$9.89 x 100] With a 4.0% stock dividend, the number of shares would increase to 104. The new share price = Total value / new number of shares = $989.00 / 104 = $9.51

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about the effect of the stock dividend is most accurate?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Flotation costs are the costs associated with selling shares to the public that would include underwriters' fees, legal costs, registration expenses, and possible negative price effects and would be lower with a direct stock dividend to existing owners. The company did not have to spend any actual money issuing a dividend.

questions.question_text: In the share buyback discussion, the statement that is the least accurate is:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because in a greenmail transaction, the company buys back the would-be suitor's shares at a premium to the market price, not at a discount. Unsuccessful takeover attempts have ended with the company buying back the would -be suitor’s shares at a premium to the market price, referred to as a greenmail transaction, often to the detriment of remaining shareholders.

vignettes.module: Analysis of Dividends and Share Repurchases
vignettes.topic: Analysis of Dividends and Share Repurchases - Vignette Questions 40-43
vignettes.case_study_text:
Andrea Larsen is the CFO of Fjord Marine Industries (FMI), a Norwegian pleasure boat 
manufacturer. Larsen is reviewing FMI's dividend policy in advance of recommending a 
course of action to the Board of Directors regarding the current year dividend payment.  
FMI's business is influenced by many factors, including commodity prices, interest rates 
and general economic 
 
Exhibit 1 
  Current Year Prior Year Two Years Ago 
| Current | Year | Prior | Year Two Years Ago |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pre-tax income (in NOK millions)** | 600 | 450 | 400 |
| **After-tax net income (in NOK millions)** | 420 | 315 | 300 |
| **Dividends paid (in NOK millions)** | 120 115 |  |  |
| **Number of shares outstanding (in millions)** | 6 | 6 | 6 |

FMI's shares are currently trading at NOK 460. Exhibit 1 shows that FMI had a very strong year in the current year, generating an additional NOK 105 million net income over the previous year. Larsen considers four possible options in addition to the regular dividend to return a portion of this additional capital to shareholders in the current year:
 
• Option 1: Issue a stock dividend of 20%. 
• Option 2: Increase the annual per share dividend by 20% more than the prior year. 
• Option 3: Maintain the current annual dividend and pay a special dividend of NOK 10 
per share. 
• Option 4: Repurchase NOK 200 million worth of shares at a NOK 40 premium to the 
current share price.

questions.question_text: For FMI, Option 1 would most likely:
options: B. reduce the volatility of its share price.
C. increase the value of each shareholder's ownership position in the company.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the payment of a stock dividend also helps increase the stock’s float, which improves the liquidity of the shares and dampens share price volatility.

questions.question_text: Compared to option 2, option 3 results in a total current year dividend payment per share that is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because under option 2, the current year dividend payment would be (NOK 120 million × 1.2) / 6 million shares = NOK 24 per share. Under option 3 the total dividend paid would be NOK 120 million / 6 million + NOK 10 = NOK 30. The per share dividend increase is therefore NOK 30 – NOK 24 = NOK 6. An extra dividend or special dividend (also known as an irregular dividend) is either a dividend paid by a company that does not pay dividends on a regular schedule or a dividend that supplements regular cash dividends with an extra payment.

questions.question_text: Based on Exhibit 1, the safety of FMI's dividend in the prior year compared to two years ago:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the dividend coverage ratio (after -tax net income / dividends paid) increased from 300/115 ≈ 2.609 from two years ago to 315/120 = 2.625 in the prior year, which means the probability of receiving a dividend increased making the dividend more safe.

questions.question_text: If option 4 is implemented using surplus cash, which of the following is most likely to decrease? FMI's:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because share repurchases involve a reduction in shareholder equity and therefore a reduction in the total book value reported on the balance sheet. Whether the book value per share increases or decreases depends on whether the market price per share is above or below the book value per share.

vignettes.module: Analysis of Dividends and Share Repurchases
vignettes.topic: Analysis of Dividends and Share Repurchases - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: The payment of a 10% stock dividend by a company will result in an increase in that company’s:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. A stock dividend is accounted for as a transfer of retained earnings to contributed capital.

questions.question_text: If a company’s common shares trade at very low prices, that company would be most likely to consider the use of a:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. A reverse stock split would increase the price per share of the stock to a higher, more marketable range that could possibly increase the number of investors who would consider buying the stock.

questions.question_text: In a recent presentation, Doug Pearce made two statements about dividends: Statement 1: “A stock dividend will increase share price on the ex -dividend date, all other things being equal.” Statement 2: “One practical concern with a stock split is that it will reduce the company’s price - to-earnings ratio.” Are Pearce’s two statements about the effects of the stock dividend and stock split correct?
options: B. Yes for Statement 1, and no for Statement 2.
C. No for Statement 1, and yes for Statement 2.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Both statements are incorrect. A stock dividend will decrease the price per share, all other things being equal. A stock split will reduce the price and earnings per share proportionately, leaving the price-to-earnings ratio the same.

questions.question_text: All other things being equal, the payment of an internally financed cash dividend is most likely to result in:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. By reducing corporate cash, a cash dividend reduces the current ratio, whereas a stock dividend (whatever the size) has no effect on the current ratio.

questions.question_text: Which of the following assumptions is not required for Miller and Modigliani’s (MM) dividend theory?
options: A. Shareholders have no transaction costs when buying and selling shares.
C. Investors prefer dividends over uncertain capital gains.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The MM dividend theory assumes no taxes or transaction costs, but it does not assume investors have a preference for dividends over capital gains.

questions.question_text: Sophie Chan owns 100,000 shares of PAT Company. PAT is selling for €40 per share, so Chan’s investment is worth €4,000,000. Chan reinvests the gross amount of all dividends received to purchase additional shares. Assume that the clientele for PAT shares consists of tax -exempt investors. If PAT pays a €1.50 dividend, Chan’s new share ownership after reinvesting dividends at the ex-dividend price is most likely to be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Because the clientele for PAT investors has the same tax rate (zero) for dividends and capital gains, the ex-dividend stock price of PAT should decline by the amount of the dividend to €40 − €1.50 = €38.50. Chan will purchase €150,000/€38.50 = 3,896 additional shares. This increases her total shares owned to 103,896. Chan’s new share ownership is closest to 103,900.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is most likely to signal negative information concerning a company?
options: B. Decrease in the quarterly dividend rate.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. A decrease in the quarterly dividend rate is likely to signal negative information. A decrease is typically understood as signaling poor future business prospects.

questions.question_text: WL Corporation is located in a jurisdiction that has a 40% corporate tax rate on pretax income and a 30% personal tax rate on dividends. WL distributes all its after- tax income to shareholders. What is the effective tax rate on WL pretax income distributed in dividends?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The effective tax rate can be computed as 1 minus the fraction of 1 unit of earnings that investors retain after all taxes, or 1 − (1 − 0.40)(1 − 0.30) = 0.58 or 58% effective tax rate. Another way to obtain the solution: Corporate taxes = 1.00 × 0.40 = 0.40 and Personal taxes = 0.60 in dividends × 0.30 = 0.18, so Total tax = 0.40 + 0.18 = 0.58, or 58% effective rate.

questions.question_text: Which of the following factors is least likely to be associated with a company having a low dividend payout ratio?
options: A. High flotation costs on new equity issues.
B. High tax rates on dividends.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. With low growth prospects, a company would typically have a high payout ratio, returning funds to its shareholders rather than retaining funds.

questions.question_text: The dividend policy of Berkshire Gardens Inc. can be represented by a gradual adjustment to a target dividend payout ratio. Last year Berkshire had earnings per share of US$3.00 and paid a dividend of US$0.60 a share. This year it estimates earnings per share will be US$4.00. Find its dividend per share for this year if it has a 25% target payout ratio and uses a five-year period to adjust its dividend.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The estimated dividend per share is US$0.68. Previous DPS = US$0.60 Expected EPS = US$4 Target payout ratio = 0.25 Five-year adjustment factor = 1/5 = 0.2 Expected dividend = Previous dividend + (Expected earnings × Target payout ratio − Previous dividend) × Adjustment factor = US$0.60 + [(US$4.00 × 0.25 − US$0.60) × 0.2] = US$0.60 + US$0.08 = US$0.68

questions.question_text: Beta Corporation is a manufacturer of inflatable furniture. Which of the following scenarios best reflects a stable dividend policy for Beta?
options: A. Maintaining a constant dividend payout ratio of 40–50%.
B. Maintaining the dividend at US$1.00 a share for several years given no change in
C. Increasing the dividend 5% a year over several years to reflect the two years in
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Choice A is consistent with a constant dividend target payout ratio policy. Choice C is not correct because the earnings increases described are not sustainable long term.

questions.question_text: A company has 1 million shares outstanding and earnings are £2 million. The company decides to use £10 million in surplus cash to repurchase shares in the open market. The company’s shares are trading at £50 per share. If the company uses the entire £10 million of surplus cash to repurchase shares at the market price, the company’s earnings per share will be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. At the current market price, the company can repurchase 200,000 shares (£10 million/£50 = 200,000 shares). The company would have 800,000 shares outstanding after the repurchase (1 million shares − 200,000 shares = 800,000 shares). EPS before the buyback is £2.00 (£2 million/1 million shares = £2.00). Total earnings after the buyback are the same because the company uses surplus (nonearning) cash to purchase the shares, but the number of shares outstanding is reduced to 800,000. EPS increases to £2.50 (£2 million/ 800,000 shares = £2.50).

questions.question_text: Devon Ltd. common shares sell at US$40 a share, and their estimated price -to- earnings ratio (P/E) is 32. If Devon borrows funds to repurchase shares at its after- tax cost of debt of 5%, its EPS is most likely to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. If the P/E is 32, the earnings -to-price ratio (earnings yield or E/P) is 1/32 = 3.125%. When the cost of capital is greater than the earnings yield, earnings dilution will result from the buyback.

questions.question_text: A company can borrow funds at an after-tax cost of 4.5%. The company’s stock price is US$40 per share, earnings per share is US$2.00, and the company has 15 million shares outstanding. If the company borrows just enough to repurchase 2 million shares of st ock at the prevailing market price, that company’s earnings per share is most likely to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The company’s earnings yield (E/P) is US$2/US$40 = 0.05. When the earnings yield is greater than the after-tax cost of borrowed funds, EPS will increase if shares are repurchased using borrowed funds.

questions.question_text: Crozet Corporation plans to borrow just enough money to repurchase 100,000 shares. The following information relates to the share repurchase: Shares outstanding before buyback 3.1 million Earnings per share before buyback US$4.00 Share price at time of buyback US$50 After-tax cost of borrowing 6% Crozet’s earnings per share after the buyback will be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Total earnings before buyback: US$4.00 × 3,100,000 shares = US$12,400,000 Total amount of borrowing: US$50 × 100,000 shares = US$5,000,000 After-tax cost of borrowing the amount of funds needed: US$5,000,000 × 0.06 = US$300,000 Number of shares outstanding after buyback: 3,100,000 − 100,000 = 3,000,000 EPS after buyback: (US$12,400,000 − US$300,000)/3,000,000 shares = US$4.03 The P/E before the buyback is US$50/US$4 = 12.5; thus, the E/P is 8%. The after- tax cost of debt is 6%; therefore, EPS will increase.

questions.question_text: A company with 20 million shares outstanding decides to repurchase 2 million shares at the prevailing market price of €30 per share. At the time of the buyback, the company reports total assets of €850 million and total liabilities of €250 million. As a result of the buyback, that company’s book value per share will most likely:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The company’s book value before the buyback is €850 million in assets − €250 million in liabilities = €600 million. Book value per share is €600 million/20 million = €30 per share. The buyback will reduce equity by 2 million shares at the prevailing market price of €30 per share. The book value of equity will be reduced to €600 million − €60 million = €540 million, and the number of shares will be reduced to 18 million; €540 million/18 million = €30 book value per share. If the prevailing mark et price is equal to the book value per share at the time of the buyback, book value per share is unchanged.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathered the following information about a company: Number of shares outstanding 10 million Earnings per share US$2.00 P/E 20 Book value per share US$30 If the company repurchases 1 million shares at the prevailing market price, the resulting book value per share will be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The prevailing market price is US$2.00(20) = US$40.00 per share; thus, the buyback would reduce equity by US$40 million. Book value of equity before the buyback is US$300 million. Book value of equity after the buyback would be US$300 million − US$40 million = US$260 million. The number of shares outstanding after the buyback would be 9 million. Thus, book value per share after the buyback would be US$260 million/9 million = US$28.89 ≈ US$29.

questions.question_text: If a company’s objective is to support its stock price in the event of a market downturn, it would be advised to authorize:
options: A. an open market share repurchase plan to be executed over the next five years.
B. a tender offer share repurchase at a fixed price effective in 30 days.
C. a Dutch auction tender offer effective in 30 days.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Of the three methods, only an authorized open market share repurchase plan allows the company the flexibility to time share repurchases to coincide with share price declines.

questions.question_text: A company has positive free cash flow and is considering whether to use the entire amount of that free cash flow to pay a special cash dividend or to repurchase shares at the prevailing market price. Shareholders’ wealth under the two options will be equivalent unless the:
options: A. company’s book value per share is less than the prevailing market price.
B. company’s book value per share is greater than the prevailing market price.
C. tax consequences and/or information content for each alternative is different.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. For the two options to be equivalent with respect to shareholders’ wealth, the amount of cash distributed, the taxation, and the information content must be the same for both options.

questions.question_text: Assume that a company is based in a country that has no taxes on dividends or capital gains. The company is considering either paying a special dividend or repurchasing its own shares. Shareholders of the company would have:
options: A. greater wealth if the company paid a special cash dividend.
B. greater wealth if the company repurchased its shares.
C. the same wealth under either a cash dividend or share repurchase program.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. When there are no taxes, there are no tax differences between dividends and capital gains. All other things being equal, the effect on shareholder wealth of a dividend and a share repurchase should be the same.

questions.question_text: Investors may prefer companies that repurchase their shares instead of paying a cash dividend when:
options: A. capital gains are taxed at lower rates than dividends.
B. capital gains are taxed at the same rate as dividends.
C. the company needs more equity to finance capital expenditures.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. When capital gains are taxed at lower rates than dividends, investors may prefer companies that return cash to shareholders through share repurchases rather than dividends.

questions.question_text: A highly profitable company with excess cash has slowing sales growth due to a lack of investment opportunities. The best way for management to reduce agency costs is to:
options: C. maintain the dividend at its current level.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because increasing the dividend would reduce agency costs by returning excess cash flows to shareholders rather than being reinvested poorly. The potential overinvestment agency problem might be alleviated by the payment of dividends. In particular, by paying out all free cash flow to equity in dividends, managers would be constrained in their ability to overinvest by taking on negative NPV projects.

questions.question_text: All else being equal, a dividend increase would be most appropriate for which of the following companies?
options: B. A profitable company with only a few positive NPV projects
C. A company with volatile earnings that has posted strong profits in the current
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because all else equal, a company with many profitable investment opportunities will tend to pay out less in dividends than a company with fewer opportunities because the former company will have more uses for internally generated cash flows. Internally g enerated cash flow is generally a cheaper source of equity funding than new equity issuance.

questions.question_text: Compared to a stable dividend policy, a constant dividend payout ratio policy would most likely result in:
options: A. gradual adjustment towards a target payout ratio.
B. dividends fluctuating with earnings in the short term.
C. less uncertainty for shareholders about future dividends.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because with this type of dividend policy, dividends fluctuate with earnings in the short term. Constant dividend payout ratio policies are infrequently adopted in practice. In contrast, under a stable dividend policy, dividends over the period were eithe r stable or rising —even while earnings experienced considerable variability.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements regarding trends in payout policies is most accurate?
options: A. There is evidence of a positive relationship between dividend initiations and
B. Globally, aggregate dividend amounts have generally increased over time while
C. Dividend payers are, on average, larger and more profitable companies that spend
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the dividend payers are, on average, larger, more profitable, have fewer growth opportunities, and spend less on R&D compared to the non - dividend payers.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following financial statement data of three companies financials for the most recent fiscal year (amounts are in $ millions): Company A Company B Company C Net income 550 725 254 Cash flow from operations 745 810 295 Capital expenditures 315 432 75 Net borrowing –100 –250 –85 Dividends paid 135 40 45 Stock repurchases 65 70 50 Based on the FCFE coverage ratio, the company with the greatest dividend safety is:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because under the FCFE method Company A has the greatest dividend safety. FCFE coverage ratio = FCFE/(Dividends + Share Repurchases) FCFE = Cash flow from operations – Capital expenditures + Net borrowing FCFE Company A = 745 – 315 + (100) = 330 FCFE Company B = 810 – 432 + (250) = 128 FCFE Company C = 295 – 75 + (85) = 135 FCFE Coverage Ratio Company A = 330/(135 + 65) = 1.65 FCFE Coverage Ratio Company B = 128/(40 + 70) = 1.16 FCFE Coverage Ratio Company C = 135/(45 + 50) = 1.42

questions.question_text: Which of the following is most likely an early signal that a company might not be able to sustain its cash dividend?
options: A. Dividends being financed by new debt
B. Dividend yield at the lowest historical level
C. Dividend coverage ratio consistently increasing
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because analysts should be particularly alert to companies that support their dividends and stock repurchases by reducing productive capital spending or by adding net debt or by some combination of the two because these neither are sustainable policies.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about income tax rates in a jurisdiction with a split-rate tax system: Corporate tax rate on retained earnings 30% Corporate tax rate on earnings allocated to dividends 15% Marginal shareholder tax rate on dividends 40% Assuming a payout ratio of 100%, the effective tax rate on pretax income distributed in dividends is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because under this system, corporate earnings that are distributed as dividends are taxed at a lower rate at the corporate level than earnings that are retained. At the level of the individual investor, dividends are taxed as ordinary income. Earnings distributed as dividends are still taxed twice, but the relatively low corporate tax rate on earnings mitigates that penalty. Effective tax rate on dividends = Corporate tax rate on earnings allocated to dividends + (1 – Corporate tax rate on earnings allocated to dividends) × Shareholder tax rate on dividends = 15% + (1 – 15%) × 40% = 49%

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company with a stable dividend policy: Previous year's EPS $2.00 Previous year's payout ratio 30% Current year's expected EPS $2.50 Target payout ratio 36% If the company uses a 3 -year period to adjust its dividends, the expected increase in the current year dividend per share should be:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because Expected increase in dividends = (Expected earnings × Target payout ratio − Previous dividend) × Adjustment factor, where the adjustment factor is equal to 1 / Number of years to adjust dividends. In this case: Adjustment factor = 1/3 Expected increase in dividends = (2.50 × 36% − 2.00 × 30%) × 1/3 = 0.10 = $0.10

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company that plans to issue new debt to repurchase shares: EPS before share repurchase $3 Market price per share at the time of repurchase $40 After-tax cost of new debt 7% As a result of the share repurchase, the company's EPS will:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because in the case of external financing, the effect on EPS is positive if the earnings yield exceeds the after -tax cost of financing the repurchase. The earnings yield, the ratio of earnings per share to share price, is 3/ 40 = 7.5%. Since this value is greater than 7%, the after-tax cost of new debt, the share repurchase will have a positive effect on EPS.

questions.question_text: Which payout methods are best suited for distributing extraordinary increases in a company's cash flow that are not expected to continue in future years?
options: A. Regular and special cash dividends
B. Special cash dividends and share repurchases
C. Regular cash dividends and share repurchases
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because a company can use both special cash dividends and share repurchases as a supplement to regular cash dividends. These means of distributing cash are often used in years when there are large and extraordinary increases in cash flow that are not expe cted to continue in future years. In making these types of payments, the company essentially communicates that the distribution, like the increase in cash flow, should not be expected to continue in the future.

questions.question_text: Based on global trends in corporate payout policies, which of the following has generally decreased over time?
options: C. Percentage of companies paying dividends
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because research on dividend behavior globally shows that aggregate dividend amounts as well as payout ratios have generally increased over time, although the fraction of dividend payers has decreased.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information (in $ millions) about a company: FCFE 492 Dividends paid 215 Stock repurchases 173 The company's FCFE coverage ratio is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because FCFE coverage ratio = FCFE/[Dividends + Share repurchases]. Based on the provided inputs: FCFE coverage ratio = 492 / (215 + 173) = 1.268041 ≈ 1.27x

questions.question_text: For which of the following companies would a dividend increase most likely be viewed as a positive signal by its shareholders?
options: A. A retailer facing financial pressure
C. A fast growing tech company planning to raise equity
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because some researchers have argued that a company’s dividend initiation or increase tends to be associated with share price increases because it attracts more attention to the company. Managers have an incentive to increase the company’s dividend if the y believe the company to be undervalued because the increased scrutiny will lead to a positive price adjustment. Therefore, increasing the dividend is a positive signal for shareholders of an undervalued company.

questions.question_text: Which of the following would best constrain management's ability to overinvest by taking on negative NPV projects?
options: B. Increase internally financed cash dividends
C. Set minimum levels of EBITDA coverage of interest charges as a condition for
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because one managerial incentive of particular concern is the potential private benefit managers may obtain from investment in negative net present value (NPV) projects. The potential overinvestment agency problem might be alleviated by the payment of dividends. In particular, by paying out all free cash flow to equity in dividends, managers would be constrained in their ability to overinvest by taking on negative NPV projects.

questions.question_text: Which of the following share repurchase methods provides a company with the most flexibility?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the open market share repurchase method gives the company maximum flexibility. Open market repurchases are the most flexible option for a company because there is no legal obligation to undertake or complete the repurchase program; a company may n ot follow through with an announced program for various reasons, such as unexpected cash needs for liquidity, acquisitions, or capital expenditures.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: • Current share price: $100 • Latest reported annual earnings after taxes: $45 million • Shares outstanding: 4.5 million • After-tax cost of debt: 7% If the company plans to raise $25 million in debt to repurchase 250,000 shares, its EPS after the share repurchase will be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because EPS after buyback = (After -tax Earnings – After-tax cost of funds)/Shares outstanding after buyback After-tax Earnings minus after-tax cost of funds = $45,000,000 – ($25,000,000 × 7%) = $43,250,000 EPS after buyback = $43,250,000/(4,500,000 – 250,000) = $10.17647 ≈ $10.2.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is most likely a motivation for a company using share repurchases rather than cash dividends to distribute earnings to shareholders?
options: A. To decrease the company's financial leverage
B. To signal that the company's prospects are positive
C. To create the expectation of future share repurchases
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the announcement of a share repurchase program is often understood as a positive signal about the company’s prospects and attractiveness as an investment.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information of three companies in the same industry: Company A Company B Company C Dividend coverage ratio (x) 3.13 2.5 1.12 Debt ratio 37% 22% 26% If the earnings of all three companies are expected to decline by 20% due to a business downturn, which company has the highest risk of a dividend cut?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because higher dividend payout ratios (or lower dividend coverage ratios) often constitute a risk factor that a dividend may be cut if earnings decline. High dividend payout ratios in relation to those of peer group companies may also point to dividend sa fety concerns. Company C has the lowest dividend coverage ratio. An earnings decline will likely reduce this ratio so that a dividend cut would be the most likely among the three companies.

questions.question_text: A company operating under perfect capital market assumptions has a dividend yield of 10%. Compared to the company's change in share price predicted by the Miller and Modigliani model, the change in share price predicted by the model of Graham, Dodd, et al. is:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the model of Graham, Dodd, et al, or the "bird in the hand" argument, predicts a higher share price than the Miller and Modigliani model predicts, since the Miller and Modigliani model predicts that the dividend yield does not matter, while the Gr aham, Dodd, et al model predicts that the dividend yield positively influences the stock price. Financial theorists have argued that, even under perfect capital markets assumptions, investors prefer a dollar of dividends to a dollar of potential capital gains from reinvesting earnings because they view dividends as less risky. A related viewpoint is that 'the typical dollar of reinvestment has less economic value to the shareholder than a dollar paid in dividends' (Graham, Dodd, Cottle, and Tatham 1962). These arguments are similar and have sometimes been called the 'bird in the hand' argument, a reference to the proverb 'a bird in the hand is worth two in the bush.' By assuming that a given amount of dividends is less risky than the same amount of capital gains, the argument is that a company that pays dividends will have a lower cost of equity capital than an otherwise similar company that does not pay dividends; the lower cost of equity should result in a higher share price. In a 1961 paper, Miller and Modigliani (MM) argued that in a world without taxes, transaction costs, and equal ('symmetric') in formation among all investors —that is, under perfect capital market assumptions—a company’s dividend policy should have no impact on its cost of capital or on shareholder wealth.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Retained earnings €12 million Contributed capital €28 million Total shareholders’ equity €40 million All else being equal, a €2 million stock dividend would result in contributed capital of:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a stock dividend is accounted for as a transfer of retained earnings to contributed capital. In this case: Retained earnings after the stock dividend = Retained earnings before the stock dividend – Stock dividend = €12 million – €2 million = €10 million Contributed capital after the stock dividend = Contributed capital before the stock dividend + Stock dividend = €28 million + €2 million = €30 million As an aside, total shareholders’ equity after the stock dividend would remain the same at €40 million.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Pretax income $1,000,000 Corporate tax rate 30% Dividend payout 100% The shareholder's marginal tax rate is 22%. Under the dividend imputation tax system, the effective tax rate on dividends is:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because under the dividend imputation tax system, a corporation’s earnings are first taxed at the corporate level. When those earnings are distributed to shareholders in the form of dividends, shareholders receive a tax credit, known as a franking credit, for the taxes that the corporation paid on those distributed earnings (i.e., corporate taxes paid are imputed to the individual shareholder). A shareholder with a lower marginal tax bracket than the company’s actually receives a tax credit for the differe nce between the corporate rate and his own rate. This system effectively applies the shareholder tax rate to corporate pretax income by using tax credits or additional taxes to account for any differences between the corporate and shareholder tax rates. In this case: Taxes at 30% corporate tax rate = Pretax income × 30% = 1,000,000 × 30% = 300,000 Net income after tax = Pretax income – Taxes at 30% corporate tax rate = 1,000,000 – 300,000 = 700,000 Dividends paid = Net income after tax × Dividend payout = 700,000 × 100% = 700,000 Shareholder tax on pretax income = Pretax income × Shareholder’s marginal tax rate = 1,000,000 × 22% = 220,000 Tax credit for corporate payment = Taxes at 30% corporate tax rate = 300,000 Tax credit to the shareholder = Shareholder tax on pretax income – Tax credit for corporate payment = 220,000 – 300,000 = –80,000 Amounts received by shareholder = Dividends + Tax credit = 700,000 + 80,000= 780,000. Effective tax rate on dividend = Shareholder tax on pretax income/Pretax income = 220,000/1,000,000= 22% or (Pretax income – Amounts received by shareholder)/Pretax income = (1,000,000 – 780,000)/1,000,000 = 220,000/1,000,000 = 22%.

questions.question_text: Which of the following corporate actions reduces a company's outstanding shares?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a reverse stock split reduces the number of shares outstanding, with no effect on the market value of a company’s equity or on shareholders’ total cost basis.

questions.question_text: A company seeking to repurchase shares prioritizes speed over other factors such as cost and flexibility. Which of the following is the most appropriate method of share repurchase for the company?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because a fixed price tender offer can be accomplished quickly, but the company usually has to offer a premium.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company undertaking a share buyback: Book value $100 million Share price $5.00 Shares outstanding 10 million If the company buys back 200,000 shares, the book value per share after the buyback will be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because if the company buys back 200,000 shares, this equates to 200,000 × $5.00 = $1,000,000 buyback amount. The book value of the company after the buyback is therefore: $100,000,000 – $1,000,000 = $99,000,000 Book value per share after the buyback is $99,000,000/(10,000,000 – 200,000) = $99,000,000/9,800,000 = $10.1020 ≈ $10.10.

questions.question_text: In which of the following tax systems would earnings distributed as dividends most likely face a lower corporate tax rate than earnings that are retained?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because under the split -rate tax] system, corporate earnings that are distributed as dividends are taxed at a lower rate at the corporate level than earnings that are retained. At the level of the individual investor, dividends are taxed as ordinary incom e. Earnings distributed as dividends are still taxed twice, but the relatively low corporate tax rate on earnings mitigates that penalty.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Market price per share $25 Book value per share $20 Number of shares outstanding 10,00,000 If the company executes a $5 million buyback at the current market price, the company's book value per share is expected to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because at the market price, the company will buy back 200,000 shares (= $5 million/$25 per share), thus the number of shares after the buyback will be 800,000. The company's current book value is $20 million (= $20 book value per share × 1,000,000). After the buyback, the company's book value will fall to $15 million (= $20 million – $5 million spent on buyback). The new book value per share will be $15 million/800,000 = $18.75, thus decreasing by $1.25 per share from the value before the buyback.

vignettes.topic: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) - Vignette Questions 1-5
vignettes.case_study_text:
Theresa Blass manages the Toptier  Balanced Fund (the Fund) and recently hired John 
Yorkton, a junior analyst, to help her research investment opportunities. Blass plans to 
integrate environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors into her analysis. She is 
researching an equity investm ent in Titian International, a global steel producer. She 
asks Yorkton to identify ESG factors impacting Titian and estimate the equity valuation 
for the company.Yorkton uses proprietary methods to identify the ESG factors. 
Yorkton points out that Titian’s steel production is energy intensive and relies on coal in 
producing its main product, stainless steel. The firm’s major customers are oil and gas 
firms using stainless steel in their drilling operations. Most of Titian’s s teel capacity is 
located in developing economies, where it currently faces few environmental regulations. 
Titian has a 10 -member board with a chairperson and 5 independent members. The 
chairperson is not the CEO, and the board is diverse, with 6 women. The company has an 
excellent record on employee health and safety. In a discussion with Blass about ESG 
factors in investment analysis, Yorkton makes the following statements: 
 
**Statement 1:**
Material ESG information used in investment analysis is best obtained from the individual 
companies. 
**Statement 2:**
The level of disclosure varies among companies because these disclosures are voluntary. 
**Statement 3:**
The time horizon has little effect on the materiality of the underlying ESGfactors. 
 
Yorkton integrates ESG factors into the equity valuation of Titian. He believes the 
company faces significant long-term risk due to regulatory changes regarding greenhouse 
gas emissions in the developing economies. These changes will have a negative impact on 
Titian’s steel capacity and its production costs. Based on long -term forecasts from the 
International Energy Agency (IEA), Yorkton expects oil and natural gas demand to 
decline over the next decade, reducing oil company capital expenditures on explorat ion 
and drilling. He uses a discounted cash flow model to value Titian stock.

questions.question_text: The potential problem with Yorkton’s approach to identifying ESG factors is the:
options: A. promotion of uniform accounting standards.
B. subjective assessment of ESG scores and rankings.
C. inconsistent reporting of ESG information and metrics among firms.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Yorkton uses the proprietary method to identify company and industry ESG factors. This approach relies on using company-specific ESG data that is publicly available from annual reports, proxy reports, corporate sustainability reports, and regulatory filings such as the 10 -K. The problem is inconsistent reporting of ESG information and metrics among firms. The level of disclosure also varies considerably among companies because ESG -related disclosures are voluntary. This creates comparability issues for analysts.

questions.question_text: The most relevant industry risk factors affecting Titian are:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. In identifying a company’s ESG risks and opportunities, an analyst must determine which ESG factors are relevant to its industry. Industries such as energy and steel are typically more impacted by environmental factors. This is clearly the case with Titian. Titian’s steel production is energy intensive and relies on coal in producing its main product, stainless steel. Its major customers are oil and natural gas companies, and most of its steel capacity is located in developing economies, where it currently faces few environmental regulations. Changes in such regulations and projected declining demand for its main product are major risk factors for the firm.

questions.question_text: Which of the statements made by Yorkton on ESG factors in investment analysis is correct?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Statement 2 is correct because the level of disclosure varies considerably among companies since ESG -related disclosures are voluntary. This creates a comparability issue for analysts. This is a problem associated with the proprietary methods used to identify company and industry ESG factors.

questions.question_text: Yorkton’s ESG integration approach is likely to impact equity valuation by:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Titian faces significant long -term environmental risk factors. The imposition of stricter regulation on greenhouse gas emissions in the developing countries will result in stranded assets, as much of Titian steel capacity becomes obsolete and not economically viable. Shifting away from low-cost coal usage will likely result in higher operating costs, and declining oil and natural gas demand will result in lower revenues for stainless steel. Thus Yorkton should raise the discount rate for Titian to account for the higher environmental risk.

questions.question_text: After integrating the ESG factors into the discounted cash flow model, the equity value of Titian is likely to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The stock price for Titian is likely to decline. Titian faces significant long-term environmental risk as a result of more stringent future regulation on greenhouse gas emissions in the developing economies and a future decline in demand for its main product, stainless steel. Thus in the discount cash flow model, Titian should increase the cost of equity and most likely lower the growth rate in cash flow. Both factors will cause the price to fall.

vignettes.topic: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) - Vignette Questions 6-9
vignettes.case_study_text:
Emily Marker, CFA, is a fixed-income analyst for the Namsan Funds. Her supervisor asks 
her to identify ESG factors and value the corporate bonds of BR Hotels, a publicly traded 
boutique hotel company. Marker notes that BR Hotels is a “green hotel” company that 
prioritizes sustainability and has successfully reduce d water and energy usage at its 
hotels. The founding family owns 55% of the outstanding shares. Each ownership share 
has equivalent voting rights.The board of directors of BR Hotels consists of 15 members, 
with independent CEO and chairperson roles. The bo ard includes one independent 
member and two women, and 20% of the board members have experience in the hotel 
industry. 
BR Hotels has historically had a high labor turnover rate. Most of its workforce are paid 
at or near the minimum wage, and the company offers no health benefits. Marker and her 
supervisor discuss how BR Hotels will be affected by the expected passage of legislation 
raising the minimum wage and growing pressure to offer benefits. Marker integrates ESG 
factors in the investment valuation of BR Hotels’ corporate bonds.

questions.question_text: The potential conflict between or among shareholders and managers of BR Hotels can best be described as:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. BR Hotels has concentrated ownership, given that the family owns 55% of the shares. It also has concentrated voting power, since each ownership share has equal voting rights. In this ownership structure, the controlling shareholders have power over both management and minority shareholders. The controlling shareholders are referred to as strong shareholders and the managers as weak managers. The ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis conflict in this structure exists between the controlling shareholders and the minority shareholders. The controlling shareholders can potentially divert resources for their own benefit at the expense of the minority shareholders. This conflict is referred to as a principal-principal problem.

questions.question_text: BR Hotels’ corporate governance risk is increased by:
options: C. the low percentage of independent board members.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The corporate governance risk for BR Hotels is high due to a low percentage of independent board members. Of the 15 members on the board, only one is independent. Many OECD countries have introduced a recommendation for the minimum ratio of independent directors serving on the board. They typically set the minimum ratio of independent directors in a range of 20%–50% or greater. BR Hotels falls below this range.

questions.question_text: The security analysis of BR Hotels is most likely focused on:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The implementation of ESG factors in security analysis differs for equity analysis and fixed-income analysis. For BK Hotels’ corporate bonds, the focus of ESG integration is on mitigating downside risk. In contrast, in equity analysis, ESG integration is used to both identify potential opportunities and mitigate downside risk.

questions.question_text: After integrating the ESG factors, the credit spread on BR Hotels’ bonds is most likely to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. BR Hotels faces significant corporate governance and social risk. Corporate governance risk is high due to a low number of independent board members (1 out of 15 members), lack of gender diversity (2 women out of 15 members), and low percentage of board members with hotel industry experience (20%). These factors are likely to increase investors’ perception of the corporation’s risk. The social risk for BR Hotels is also high. BR Hotels has a high labor turnover rate, pays most of its workforce at or near the minimum wage, and offers no health benefits. Legislation raising the minimum wage and the growing pressure on BR Hotels to offer benefits would increase operating costs. This could have a negative impact on future cash flows, which would be detrimental to the bond holders. The valuation of BR Hotels’ bonds could be adversely affected by the higher ESG risk. To account for the higher ESG risk, the credit spread on BR Hotels’ bonds is likely to increase.

vignettes.topic: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) - Vignette Questions 10-13
vignettes.case_study_text:
Sugi Tyler is a chemical industry analyst for a global investment firm. Tyler is researching the impact of environment, social, and governance (ESG) issues as they relate to ChemiKa LLC (ChemiKa) and PlastiKa Corp (PlastiKa). She summarizes the companies' ownership and governance structures in Exhibit 1.

### Exhibit 1: Corporate Ownership and Governance Structures

| Characteristic | ChemiKa | PlastiKa |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total number of directors** | 19 | 8 |
| **Number of independent directors** | 12 | 4 |
| **Average board tenure** | 8 years | 14 years |
| **Independent committees:** | | |

Ownership Concentrated Dispersed Voting power Concentrated Dispersed Two-tiered board No Yes CEO duality Yes No     *Audit* Yes No     *Compensation* Yes Yes     *Director selection* No Yes

Tyler shares this data with her co-worker Kim Myer and asks Myer to determine the risk of ChemiKa’s governance structure. Myer states: "ChemiKa’s ownership and voting structure will likely diminish the ownership rights of minority shareholders which indicates principal-agent problem."

To evaluate the corporate governance policies and procedures of the two companies, Tyler considers the rankings provided by an independent ESG data analysis firm specializing in board and governance structure. The contributions of each board member are only considered after at least one year of service.

The ESG data analysis firm uses a scoring system awarding one point for favorable characteristics among the following:
* **CEO duality** is absent.
* **Average board tenure** is below ten years.
* **Board independence**: A two-tiered board is considered favorable. A simple majority of independent directors is generally favorable. However, in a one-tiered board (which is considered unfavorable), a 60% majority of independent directors is the favorable threshold.
* **Independent committees**: Presence of independent committees for audit, compensation, and director selection (1 point for the presence of each independent committee).

In recent years, both companies have faced increasing concern about the environmental impact of their products. In response to actions by shareholders, regulators, and other constituencies, industry producers have made substantial capital investments toward production of more biodegradable products. ChemiKa has built a large bio-plastics plant and financed the project with green bonds which go alongside of the company’s conventional “plain vanilla” corporate bonds. Tyler notices that ChemiKa’s green bonds trade with tighter spreads than its conventional bonds.

PlastiKa announces the addition of a chemical engineer as a new independent member of its board. Although not yet familiar with the company's core operations, it is expected that he will lead the effort to meet increasing regulatory requirements and aid in new product research and development.

questions.question_text: Myer’s statement about ChemiKa's governance risk is:
options: B. incorrect with respect to minority shareholders.
C. incorrect with respect to principal-agent problem.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the conflict described for ChemiKa is a principal -principal problem, not a principal -agent problem. The combination of concentrated ownership and concentrated voting power often results in controlling shareholders maintaining a position of power over both managers and minority shareholders and this conflict is known as a principal–principal problem. Managers are considered weak whereas in a principal-agent conflict managers are strong.

questions.question_text: Based on the ESG data analysis firm's scoring system and Exhibit 1, compared to PlastiKa, ChemiKa most likely ranks:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because based on the ESG data analysis firm's scoring system and Exhibit 1, ChemiKa and PlastiKa each has 4 points and they rank the same: Exhibit 1 ChemiKa PlastiKa SCORE ChemiKa; PlastiKa Ownership Concentrated Dispersed not scored Voting power Concentrated Dispersed not scored ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis Total number of directors 19 8 not scored Number of independent directors 12 4 1 (63% majority), 0 (50% is not a majority) Two-tiered board No Yes 0; 1 CEO duality Yes No 0; 1 Average board tenure 8 years 14 years 1; 0 Independent committees: Audit Yes No 1; 0 Compensation Yes Yes 1; 1 Director selection No Yes 0; 1 Total 4; 4

questions.question_text: The market pricing of ChemiKa's bonds that Tyler notices is most likely due to:
options: C. greater bondholder recourse with the green bonds.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because green bonds typically resemble an issuer’s conventional bonds, with the exception that the bond proceeds are earmarked for green projects. Green bonds normally have the same credit ratings and bondholder recourse as conventional bonds of the same issuer (all else being equal). Some green bonds, however, may command a premium, or tighter credit spread, versus comparable conventional bonds because of market demand.

questions.question_text: Which of the following board policies and practices will be most improved by PlastiKa's new board member?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the new board member has expertise in chemical engineering to apply to environmental regulatory issues as well as product research and development. In certain sectors/industries that rely on natural resources or face potentially large ESG risks, board members typically have expertise in environmental, climate, or social issues.

vignettes.topic: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) - Vignette Questions 14-17
vignettes.case_study_text:
The board of directors of QC Technology, Inc. (QCT),  a publicly-traded manufacturer 
of semiconductors and electronics, hires an advisory team to complete an external review 
of the board's performance. 
Alex Quinn,  an analyst on the external advisory team, summarizes QCT's current 
structure in Exhibit 1. 
 
| Characteristic | Value 1 |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Number of directors [including 5 independent*, 4 female]** | 16 |

Exhibit 1 Ownership Info Equity type Publicly traded Voting type Straight voting Percentage of shares owned by largest shareholder 40 Board Information Board structure One-Tier CEO duality Yes Average Tenure [years] 8 Percentage of directors with industry experience 40
* Minimum independent directors required is 3.   
 
Quinn notes that two directors are executives of significant  QCT suppliers and each 
holds 10% of QCT's shares. QCT maintains a similar ownership stake in  each supplier. 
QCT's founder, who is the largest QCT shareholder, also sits on the board of directors 
for each supplier.  
In a discussion with Quinn's colleagues on  the risk premium associated with 
QCT's shares, several recommendations are made.   
 
Recommendation 1: Create a dual-class share structure. 
Recommendation 2: Appoint a lead independent director. 
Recommendation 3: Target four independent directors at all times (minimum number 
required plus one). 
 
After the discussion, Quinn considers a fourth recommendation to reduce the risk 
premium by  assessing the potential benefits of completing a public disclosure that 
confirms that all independent directors will be approved by the entire shareholder base 
and the non-controlling shareholders going forward.  
In discussions with QCT's management team, Quinn learns that 35% of QCT's term debt 
is scheduled to be redeemed in two years and that the bondholder is not planning to 
renew. This bondholder provides QCT and Quinn with a copy of an ESG assessment report 
that they had completed within the last two months.   Also, the management team 
indicates that QCT plans to implement initiatives to change their business model as part 
of their 5-year strategic planning including efforts to improve on-going sustainability.  
 
Initiative 1: Expand into the manufacturing of graphics processors the following year. 

ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis 
 
 
Initiative 2: Begin to  convert existing manufacturing plants to renewable energy 
sources after three years. 
Initiative 3: Adopt new and more stringent supply chain and sourcing standards for raw 
materials the following year.  
 
As Quinn prepares a proprietary  5-year ESG assessment on QCT, he validates that 
once the new plants outlined in Initiative 2 are fully operational, QCT anticipates an 1% 
to 2% increase in their manufacturing energy costs which is expected to  be offset by 
an improved competitive position resulting from the emissions reduction versus their 
non-renewable energy competitors.

questions.question_text: QCT's current company ownership structure is best described as:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because horizontal ownership involves companies with mutual business interests (e.g., key customers or suppliers) that have cross -holding share arrangements with each other. The relationship with the two significant QCT suppliers that each maintain 10% ownership and board representation and QCT's ownership in the suppliers reflect a horizontal ownership structure.

questions.question_text: Based on the recommendations provided by Quinn's colleagues and the information outlined in Exhibit 1, the recommendation that will have the most impact on decreasing the risk premium on QCT's shares is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because as outlined in Exhibit 1, QCT's company structure includes CEO duality whereby the chief executive officer (CEO) also serves as chairperson on the board. CEO duality may raise concerns that the monitoring and oversight role of the board may be compromised relative to independent chairperson and CEO roles. This would increase risk premium on QCT shares. When the chairperson is not independent or the role is combined, a company may appoint a lead independent director to help protect investor interests. This would likely improve corporate governance and thus result in a decrease of the risk premium. ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis

questions.question_text: The public disclosure that Quinn considers for the fourth recommendation is best described as:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a special voting arrangements is a requirement designed to improve the position of minority shareholders. In this example, the pro-active disclosure is related to a special voting arrangement to protect minority shareholders in confirming independent directors on the board.

questions.question_text: The initiative that would have the highest level of materiality in the ESG assessment provided to QCT and Quinn would be:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because for the ESG assessment, the adoption of more stringent sourcing standards for raw materials would be considered an Environmental category topic, have sufficient material impact on QCT's supply chain, and be within the investment time horizon for the term debt lender.

vignettes.topic: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) - Vignette Questions 18-21
vignettes.case_study_text:
Ouse Inc., based in England, is a private company that produces and retails skin care 
products, primarily soaps and lotions. Catherine Ferguson and her sister co-founded the 
company 10 years ago because of their shared interest in developing plant -based 
products that are not tested on animals. Initially the two sisters owned all the shares, 
but two years ago they implemented performance -based compensation for the top five 
senior managers, and as part of this process, the managers now own a combined 10% of 
the equity. 
 

ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis 
 
 
The company is looking to expand its equity base to help fund a new production facility 
and support growth plans. Ferguson, who is responsible for the company’s financial 
management, is meeting with Haji Malik, a financial consultant, to explore Ouse’s equ ity 
financing options. Ferguson asks if they were to go public, could they have a share 
structure similar to a company like Facebook, where the co-founders could retain voting 
control of the company through the issuance of multi-voting ordinary (common) shares. 
 
Malik informs her that the dual class shares she has described are not permitted in the 
United Kingdom. He states that before Ouse considers going public, there are other 
options available. He suggests they look for private equity investment. He mentions being 
familiar with a private equity fund that runs a socially responsible investment (SRI) pool 
that could potentially be interested in Ouse. He says in order to qualify to be included in 
the SRI pool, a company needs to demonstrate positive attributes in all areas of ESG 
(environmental, social, and governance) considerations. 
 
Ferguson is very interested in being associated with an SRI fund and asks how Ouse could 
qualify for the investment. Malik explains that the private equity fund he is thinking of 
uses data provided by the company and looks for other information from indust ry 
organizations, news reports, and environmental groups. 
 
Ferguson explains to Malik that Ouse is implementing a new initiative to reduce the 
packaging associated with their products. The company will stock the majority of their 
products in bulk containers in their retail outlets. Customers will purchase refillab le 
bottles, available in three different sizes, to be used for future purchases. This change 
will attract customers interested in reducing their plastic footprint. The company also 
expects the change to reduce shipping, packaging, and handling costs, both at the 
distribution centers and in the retail stores. The numerous individual bottles that would 
have been packaged for shipping and then unpacked and shelved at the stores will be 
replaced with larger bulk containers. Malik notes that when announced, analysts will use 
this information in their valuation of Ouse.

questions.question_text: The change in ownership structure that occurred two years ago most likely addressed which of the following issues associated with family-owned businesses?
options: C. Ability to attract quality management
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Ouse was initially a family-owned business, owned by Catherine Ferguson and her sister, which can make it difficult to attract quality talent for management positions. However, the implementation of a performance -based compensation plan two years ago would improve the motivation and rewards available to management and make it easier to attract quality talent.

questions.question_text: If Ouse were to go public with the share structure similar to Facebook that Ferguson asked about, which governance issue would most likely arise?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Ferguson asked about the possibility of a dual class share structure, which creates concentrated ownership and concentrated voting power. The controlling shareholders may be able to allocate resources to their own benefit at the expense of the minority sha reholders. This situation is known as the principal – principal problem.

questions.question_text: Which of the following approaches to identifying a company’s ESG factors best describes the one used by the private equity fund that Malik mentions? ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The private equity fund is using proprietary methods to identify and assess ESG investments. Proprietary methods include analysts using their own judgement based on information available from corporate reports, industry organizations, news reports, and environmental groups.

questions.question_text: Analysts interested in incorporating ESG factors into their analysis will most likely adjust for the announcement of the changes arising from Ouse’s new packaging initiative by:
options: B. increasing the company’s fair value.
C. modifying only the qualitative ESG analysis.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The new packaging initiative is expected to reduce costs associated with shipping, packaging, and handling. These savings should increase Ouse’s operating margins and operating cash flows. The higher earnings should result in an increase in the fair value estimate of Ouse.

vignettes.topic: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: An analyst observes the following governance characteristics of a company: • Characteristic 1: The number of executive directors is greater than the number of independent directors. • Characteristic 2: The key board committees have a majority of independent members. • Characteristic 3: The chief executive officer (CEO) also serves as chairperson of the board. Which characteristic best reflects effective corporate governance?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because investors assess whether there are sufficiently independent committees that focus on key governance concerns, such as audit, compensation, and the selection of directors. The presence of non-independent committee members or executive directors may prompt the consideration of potential conflicts of interest or biases. Therefore, this is an example of effective corporate governance.

questions.question_text: With respect to corporate ownership structures, the principal–agent conflict caused by the combination of dispersed ownership and dispersed voting power is best mitigated through:
options: C. insiders with large ownership positions.
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the combination of dispersed ownership and dispersed voting power is generally associated with shareholders who lack the power to exercise control over managers. These shareholders are referred to as weak shareholders, and such managers are referr ed to as strong managers. Under this combination, conflict between the shareholders and managers of a corporation may be significant. Shareholders are interested in maximizing shareholder value. There is a risk, however, that managers will seek to use a co mpany’s resources to pursue their own ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis interests. In corporate governance, this conflict is known as a principal –agent problem. This problem can be mitigated if controlling shareholders are present because they may be able to control the board of directors (and, in turn, the appointment of managers) and have the incentive to monitor management.

questions.question_text: Shareholder control over a company's management is least likely if the company has:
options: A. dispersed ownership and dispersed voting power.
B. dispersed ownership and concentrated voting power.
C. concentrated ownership and concentrated voting power.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the combination of dispersed ownership and dispersed voting power is generally associated with shareholders who lack the power to exercise control over managers. These shareholders are referred to as weak shareholders, and such managers are referr ed to as strong managers. Under this combination, conflict between the shareholders and managers of a corporation may be significant.

questions.question_text: Which of the following most likely has the primary decision for labeling a security as a green bond?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because issuers have the primary decision for labeling their bonds green. This decision is made in close cooperation with the lead underwriter. At a minimum level, issuers provide detail to investors about the green eligibility criteria for the use of proceeds. Issuers are responsible for providing investors with details on the criteria used to classify the bonds as green and how the bond’s proceeds are used.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about three companies: Company 1 Company 2 Company 3 ESG Considerations in Investment Analysis Percentage of independent directors Low Low High CEO serves as chairperson No Yes Yes All else being equal, which of the companies most likely has the weakest corporate governance?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because neither a high percentage of independent directors nor the CEO serving as chairperson (CEO duality) supports good corporate governance. The absence or presence of a minority of independent directors is a negative aspect of corporate governance. Without independent directors, the potential exists for management to act in a self -serving manner. Consequently, a lack of independent directors on a board may increase investors' perception of the corporation's risk. Also, separation of the roles of CEO and chairperson is an example of a strong corporate governance practice that reduces potential risks for shareholders. In case of CEO duality, the concern may be that the monitoring and oversight role of the board may be compromised relative to independent chairperson and CEO roles.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is most likely to be adjusted when integrating ESG considerations into an equity valuation?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because for equities, valuation adjustments often include adjusting a company’s cost of capital using the discount rate or a multiple of price or terminal value.

vignettes.topic: Cost of Capital: Advanced Topics - Vignette Questions 1-5
vignettes.case_study_text:
An equity index is established in Year 1 for a country that has recently moved to a market 
economy. The index vendor constructed returns for the four years prior to Year 1 based 
on the initial group of companies constituting the index in Year 1. From Year 12 to Year 
16, a series of military confrontations concerning a disputed border disrupted the 
economy and financial markets. The dispute is conclusively arbitrated at the end of Year 
16. In total, 20 years of equity market return history is available. Othe r selected data 
are in the following tables. 
 
Selected Data 
Geometric mean return relative to 10-year government bond 
returns (over a 20-year period) 2% per year 
Arithmetic mean return relative to 10-year government bond 
returns (over a 20-year period) 2.3% per year 
Index forward dividend yield 1% 
Forecasted public company earnings growth 5% per year 
Forecasted market P/E growth 1% per year 
Forecasted real GDP growth rate (by Year 19) 4% 
Current vs. long-term inflation forecast 6% vs. 4% per year 
Current yield curve (inversion) Short maturities: 9% 
10-year maturities: 7%

questions.question_text: The inclusion of index returns prior to Year 1 would be expected to:
options: A. bias the historical ERP estimate upward.
B. bias the historical ERP estimate downward.
C. have no effect on the historical ERP estimate.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The backfilling of index returns using companies that have survived to the index construction date is expected to introduce a positive survivorship bias into returns.
