in its sales, collection, purchases or inventory policies. 
 
Bruno realizes that most of Libra’s competitors are US companies, which report under 
US GAAP.  When reviewing Libra’s cash flow statement, he notices that the only 
difference in cash flow classification relates to interest paid. To improve comparability 
with those competitors, Bruno reclassifies interest paid in Libra’s cash flow statement. 
 
Bruno believes that Libra's management significantly underestimates the environmental 
risks of its business. To investigate the issue, Bruno refers to different 
information sources.

questions.question_text: The trend in the receivables turnover ratio most likely indicates that, in the most recent year, Libra engaged in:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the receivables turnover ratio shows a significant jump in the most recent year. This means that receivables as a percentage of revenues decreased significantly. Since Libra had no significant change in its sales or collection policies, the trend in the receivables turnover ratio could be an indication that, in the most recent year, Libra engaged in the practice of selling receivables to third parties. According to the reading, a company may focus on accounts receivable because it wants to hide liquidity or revenue collection issues. Choices include removing the accounts receivable from the balance sheet by selling them externally.

questions.question_text: The trend in the inventory turnover ratio most likely indicates that, in the most recent year, Libra has aggressively:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the inventory turnover ratio shows a significant decrease in the most recent year. This means that inventory as a percentage of cost of goods sold increased significantly. Since Libra had no significant change in its purchase or inventory policies, the trend in the inventory turnover ratio could be an indication that, in the most recent year, Libra may have aggressively reduced its obsolescence allowance. Management may use items such as reserves and allowances to manage or smooth earnings.

questions.question_text: Bruno’s adjustment to Libra’s cash flow statement will result in a higher cash flow from:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because Bruno’s adjustment to Libra’s cash flow statement will result in a reclassification of interest paid from financing activities to operating activities. IFRS permits companies to classify interest paid either as operating or as financing. In contrast, US GAAP requires that interest paid, be classified as operating cash flows. Since the only classification difference between Libra’s cash flow statement and its US competitors’ is the classification of interest paid, Bruno will have to reclassify the i nterest paid from cash flow from financing activities to cash flow from operating activities. This adjustment will make cash flow from financing activities higher, cash flow from operating activities lower, and cash flow from investing activities the same.

questions.question_text: Which of the following information sources is least useful for Bruno’s investigation?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because to investigate risks, Bruno can turn to several sources. However, an auditor’s opinion is unlikely to be an analyst’s first source of information about a company’s risk. The auditor’s report indicates if the financial statements fairly reflect the financial position and results of the company’s business activities, but it does not list nor completely explain the company’s environmental risks.

vignettes.topic: Evaluating Quality of Financial Reports - Vignette Questions 27-30
vignettes.case_study_text:
Hannah Treadway is an analyst at Knight Investment Management (Knight). Knight holds 
Cooper Creek Cable Limited (CCCL) as part of its AFE (Australian and Far East) 
investment portfolio. CCCL is a diversified cable and communications company operating 
in Western Australia. The company consists of three divisions: 
 
• Cable: Provides subscription television services and high speed internet to residential 
customers. 
• Media: Owns and operates a group of radio stations and publishes several magazines. 
• Wireless: Is engaged in wireless voice and data communications services. 
 
Treadway is just starting her annual review of the company based on its most recent 
financial statements, excerpts of which are in Exhibits 1 and 2. The financial statements 
for CCCL are prepared in accordance with Australian Accounting Standards (AASB), 
which comply with IFRS. All figures are in Australian dollars ($). 
 
**Exhibit 1:**
Cooper Creek Cable Limited Statement of Earnings for Years Ending 
December 31 (all figures in $ thousands) 
  2016 2015 
Revenue 7,11,200 6,74,600 
Programming and communication expenses 3,12,900 3,17,000 
Gross margin 3,98,300 3,57,600 
Depreciation expense 98,750 78,650 
Amortization of intangibles 7,250 8,150 
Reversal of impairment loss –12,500 --- 
Gain on sale of assets held for sale –14,400 --- 
Operating costs 1,85,900 1,73,000 
Interest expense 64,100 65,900 
Income from investments in associates 1,200 850 
Profit before tax 70,400 32,750 
Tax benefit (expense) 17,600 –8,187 
Net profit for the year 88,000 24,563 
**Exhibit 2:**
Cooper Creek Cable Limited Balance Sheet as of December 31 (all figures 
in $ thousands) 
  2016 2015 
Cash 95,600 74,400 
Accounts receivable, net 35,700 33,500 
Assets held for sale --- 23,500 
Total current assets 1,31,300 1,31,400 
Investments in associates 42,700 42,300 
Capital assets, net 2,21,800 2,41,200 
Intangible assets, net 43,250 24,500 
Goodwill 11,000 6,500 
Deferred tax assets 1,85,500 1,69,900 
Total assets 6,35,550 6,15,800 
  
Trade payables 92,100 1,04,200 
Interest bearing loans 49,700   
Short-term unearned revenue 12,500 21,250 
Other liabilities 23,800 23,000 
Total current Liabilities 1,78,100 1,48,450 
Interest bearing debt 7,03,800 8,14,300 
Long-term unearned revenue 6,500 13,500 
Total liabilities 8,88,400 9,76,250 
  
Issued capital 5,56,400 5,36,800 
Accumulated losses –809,250 –897,250 
Total equity –252,850 –360,450 
Total liabilities and equity 6,35,550 6,15,800 
 
CCCL sustained substantial losses in its start -up period (2001 –2005) (from which it is 
still benefiting for tax purposes) but has been profitable since 2005, reporting a record 
profit after tax in 2016. However, Treadway is wondering if CCCL’s revenues in general 
are supported by cash flows and if the company might be trying to increase the 
appearance of profitability in order to increase the share price, which remains low. 
 
The Wireless division was acquired by CCCL in a share purchase in late 2015. Treadway 
wants to review the accounting policies CCCL has adopted for both revenue and expenses 
incurred on long-term wireless contracts (Exhibit 3). 
 
**Exhibit 3:**
Excerpts of Accounting Policy Notes (all figures in thousands) 
Note 1 d) Long-Term Wireless Contracts 
Customers who enter into long-term service contracts for wireless services 
can obtain their mobile devices for a nominal amount. Commencing in 2016, 
the discount offered on the devices, relative to the regular price, is 
capitalized as a customer acquisitio n cost and amortized straight -line over 
the life of the contract, or a minimum of three years. Previously this amount 
was recognized in income immediately. 
Note 1 g) Unearned Revenue 
Unearned revenue for subscriptions, or for services paid in advance, was 
historically recognized on a straight-line basis over the term of the contract 
or subscription, which was typically three years. After reviewing the 
historical pattern of usage and ca ncellations for service contracts in 2016, 
the pattern of recognition was changed to recognize the majority of the 
revenues in the first 12 months after the service contract is signed with the 
remainder recognized in the following year. 
Note 12) Broadcast Licenses 
During 2016, the company successfully disposed of broadcast licenses that 
were held for sale for $37,900 (net book value of $23,500). Based on the 
successful completion of that sale the impairment losses taken in 2014 on 
other licenses have been reversed, restoring those intangible assets to their 
amortized historical cost. Broadcast licenses are amortized over a period of 
15 to 25 years. 
 
On reading the note about the rapid reversal of the impairment loss related to the 
Broadcast licenses (Exhibit 3, Note 12) Treadway strongly believes that it arose as an 
attempt by management to manage earnings. She realizes that both her 2014 and 2015 
analyses were impacted by these actions and now need to be reconsidered. 
 
As part of her annual review, Treadway determines the Altman’s Z-scores for CCCL. The 
results for the current and past years are reported in Exhibit 4. 
 
**Exhibit 4:**
Cooper Creek Cable Limited Altman Z-Scores 
  2016 2015 
Altman Z-score* 2.14 1.82 
* Critical values: 1.81 and 3.0 
 
Finally, Treadway noted that during 2016 CCCL acquired 100% of MusicMusic  (MM), a 
specialty cable music channel in an all -stock deal. At the time of the acquisition MM 
reported intangible assets for broadcast licenses at a value of $2,500. CCCL estimated 
the fair value of those licenses to be $5,500 at that date and estimated the value of the 
MusicMusic brand name to be $2,000, all figures in thousands. The acquisition did not 
give rise to any goodwill.

questions.question_text: The change in which of the following items most likely indicates that CCCL might be recognizing revenue early?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. In the past, revenue from service contracts had been recognized on a straight-line basis over the (typical) three -year period of the contract (Note 1 g in Exhibit 3). Under the new policy, most of the revenue is recognized in the first year and all of it within the two-year time frame. This represents a much more aggressive revenue recognition policy. An increase in DSO could be an indicator of early revenue recognition but for CCCL the ratio did not change significantly (18.1 days in 2015 to 18.3 days in 2016 using year-end receivables). Deferred tax assets (Exhibit 2) can arise from differences in revenue recognition for taxes and financial statement purposes (they would rise with increases in unearned revenue), but there is no indication here that revenue is the reason for the increase i n deferred tax assets DTA (in fact, unearned revenue decreased). The deferred tax assets most likely arise from the loss carry forwards generated from earlier losses.

questions.question_text: The new accounting policy adopted in 2016 for the customer acquisition cost related to long-term wireless contracts (Exhibit 3, Note 1 d) most likely increases CCCL’s:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. In 2016, CCCL started capitalizing the discount offered (from selling the mobile devices at a lower price) instead of recording it in the period it is incurred. This change in the policy would increase net income (by lowering expenses) and cash from operations. The amounts capitalized would be recorded as cash outflows for investing activities, compared to cash from operations if they were expensed.

questions.question_text: If Treadway’s belief about management’s motivation behind the 2014 treatment of the broadcast licenses is correct, compared to the actual economic results in 2015, her original 2015 analysis would most likely have:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The broadcast licenses were written down in 2014, but the write -down was reversed in 2016. Therefore, during 2015 the intangible assets were understated, which would have understated amortization expense for the year and increased profit. Thus in 2015, net profit margin was overstated.

questions.question_text: From her Altman Score results, Treadway is most likely to conclude that the probability of bankruptcy for CCCL has:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Altman scores in excess of 3.0 indicate low probability of bankruptcy; those below 1.81 indicate a high probability of bankruptcy. Scores within the 1.81 to 3.0 range do not provide a clear indication of bankruptcy.

vignettes.topic: Evaluating Quality of Financial Reports - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: An analyst is evaluating the financial reports of three GAAP-compliant companies and makes the following observations: • Company 1's earnings are affected by biased accounting choices. • Company 2's earnings do not provide an adequate level of return. • Company 3's earnings appear to be managed to result in lower earnings volatility. Which company should be considered to offer the highest financial reporting quality?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the hierarchy of financial reporting quality is: 1) GAAP-compliant, decision useful, sustainable and adequate returns 2) GAAP, decision useful, but unsustainable or inadequate earnings (Company 2) 3) Within GAAP, but biased accounting choices (Company 1) 4) Within GAAP, but 'earnings management' (Company 3) 5) Non-compliant accounting 6) Fictitious transactions

questions.question_text: Decisions in financial reporting that increase earnings in the current period and are likely to decrease earnings in subsequent periods are best described as:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because choices are deemed to be 'aggressive' if they increase the company’s reported performance and financial position in the current period. Aggressive choices may decrease the company’s reported performance and financial position in later periods.

questions.question_text: The only factor expected to give rise to normal accruals in a company’s earnings is the growth in credit sales. If there is no residual when total accruals are regressed on the growth in credit sales, total accruals are most likely equal to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a company’s normal accruals are modeled as a function of economic factors, such as growth in credit sales and the amount of depreciable assets. Growth in credit sales would be expected to result in accounts receivable growth, and depreciable assets would be associated with the amount of depreciation. To apply this approach, total accruals are regressed on the factors expected to give rise to normal accruals, and the residual of the regression would be considered a proxy for abnormal accruals. If th e residual of the regression is zero, the total accruals would equal the normal accruals (also called 'non-discretionary' accruals). An important distinction is between accruals that arise from normal transactions in the period (called 'non -discretionary') and accruals that result from transactions or accounting choices outside the normal, which are possibly made with the intent to distort reported earnings (called 'discretionary accruals').

questions.question_text: An analyst assesses the earnings of three companies using a regression model, Earningst+1 = α + β1 × Earningst + ε. The analyst determines the following parameters: α β1 Company 1 0.4 0.8 Company 2 0.5 0.6 Company 3 0.3 0.4 The company showing the highest earnings persistence is most likely:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because persistence can be expressed as the coefficient on current earnings in a simple model: Earnings t+1 = α + β 1Earningst + ε A higher coefficient (β 1) represents more persistent earnings. Company 1 has the highest β1 (0.8) among the 3 companies thus showing the highest earnings persistence.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers information about two companies to assess the quality of their cash flow: Company 1 Reports operating cash flow with relatively low volatility relative to industry participants Company 2 Reports large differences between earnings and operating cash flow with the gap constantly increasing Which company shows a potentially poor reporting quality?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Company 2 shows evidence of potential earnings manipulation which is a misrepresentation of reality and thus an example of poor reporting quality. Cash flow can be described as low quality either because the reported information correctly represents bad economic performance (poor results quality) or because the reported information misrepresents economic reality (poor reporting quality). Large differences between earnings and operating cash flow or increases in such differences can be an indication of earnings manipulation.

questions.question_text: A company understating the valuation allowance for deferred tax assets:
options: A. overstates both tax expenses and the value of the deferred tax assets.
B. overstates tax expenses and understates the value of the deferred tax assets.
C. understates tax expenses and overstates the value of the deferred tax assets.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the understatement of valuation allowance for deferred tax assets would understate tax expenses and overstate the value of the assets on the balance sheet.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about three companies: Company 1 Contributes a substantial portion to the auditing firm’s revenue Company 2 Has management that is particularly close to the auditor's management Company 3 Changes auditors only if required by mandatory rules Possible reporting problems are least likely signaled in relation to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because as long as mandatory rotation rules are complied with, maintaining the same auditor is not a red flag by itself but the other way around a change in the auditor (especially multiple changes in the auditor) can signal possible reporting problems.

questions.question_text: An analyst makes the following statements about disadvantages of using the audit opinion as a source of information about reporting risk: • Statement 1: The audit opinion is unlikely to provide timely information • Statement 2: Internal controls over financial reporting are out of scope of audit opinions Which of the above statements is most accurate?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because an audit opinion(s) covering financial statements (and internal controls over financial reporting, where required) can provide some information about reporting risk. However, the content of an audit opinion is unlikely to be a timely source of information about risk.

questions.question_text: A company has a substantial portion of its assets valued using non-observable inputs. Which of the following aspects of financial reporting quality is most likely to be adversely impacted?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because an important aspect of financial reporting quality for the balance sheet is unbiased measurement . The balance sheet of a company with a substantial portion of its assets valued using non -observable inputs likely warrants closer scrutiny.

questions.question_text: Which of the following bankruptcy prediction models best addresses the issue of the going-concern assumption in using historical financial statements?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because another shortcoming of the Altman model, and other accounting- based bankruptcy prediction models, is that financial statements measure past performance and incorporate the going -concern assumption. The reported values on a company’s balance sheet assume that the company is a going concern rather than one that might be failing. An alternative is to use market-based bankruptcy prediction models.

questions.question_text: Based on the Altman model Z-scores below, which company is most likely to have the highest probability of bankruptcy? Company Most Recent Year Prior Year Change in Z-Score A 1.6 2.0 –0.4 B 2.4 1.7 0.7 C 3.2 3.0 0.2
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the interpretation of the score is that a higher Z-score is better. In Altman’s application of the model, scores of less than 1.81 indicated a high probability of bankruptcy, scores greater than 3.00 indicated a low probability of bankruptcy, and scores between 1.81 and 3.00 were not clear indicators. The company with the lowest Z -score in the current year is Company A, with a Z-score of 1.6. Company C has the highest Z-score at 3.2, and Company B also has a higher Z-score at 2.4. The Z-score for Company A is below the relevant cutoff of 1.81 which indicates a high probability of bankruptcy.

questions.question_text: Which of the following reclassifications is most likely to increase the operating cash flows of a company reporting under IFRS? • Reclassification 1: Reclassifying interest received from operating to investing • Reclassification 2: Reclassifying interest paid from operating to financing
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because if an IFRS-reporting company that changed its classification of interest paid from operating to financing, the company’s operating cash flow would appear higher than the prior period even if no other activities occurred in the period.

questions.question_text: Which of the following companies is best described as having high quality cash flows? Company A Company B Company C Sustainability of operating cash flow Low High High Volatility of operating cash flow Low Low High
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Company B's OCF has high sustainability and low volatility, which is preferred over Company A's OCF with low sustainability and Company C's OCF with high volatility. In general, for established companies, high -quality cash flow would typically have most or all of the following characteristics: • Positive OCF • OCF derived from sustainable sources • OCF adequate to cover capital expenditures, dividends, and debt repayments • OCF with relatively low volatility (relative to industry participants).

questions.question_text: Which of the following practices involve inappropriate transfers of resources between public companies and private entities owned by their managers?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because both propping and tunnelling are practices involving inappropriate transfer of resources between public companies and private entities owned by their managers. The manager-owned entity could transfer resources to the public company to ensure its economic viability and thus preserve the option to misappropriate or to participate in profits in the future. These practices are often referred to as propping. Dealings between a public company and the manager -owned entity might take place at prices that are unfavorable for the public company in order to transfer wealth from the public company to the manager -owned entity. These practices are often referred to as tunneling.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company's software sales in the beginning of the most recent year: Software license component $3.6 million Support service component during the contract period $2.4 million Contract period 3 years What is the most appropriate revenue to be recognized in the most recent year?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because for multiple -deliverable arrangements, product revenue is usually recognized immediately whereas service revenue is recognized as the services are provided. Following the principle, the calculation for the year of sale is: Software license component + Support service component / Contract period = $3.6 million + $2.4 million / 3 years = $3.6 million + $0.8 million = $4.4 million For multiple-deliverable arrangements, recognition of revenue on a software delivery is only permitted if the software sale could be separated from the service portion of the contract.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered as an after -the-fact confirmation of poor earnings quality?
options: A. Restatements related to earnings management only
B. Enforcement actions related to earnings management only
C. Both restatements and enforcement actions related to earnings management
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because enforcement actions and restatements are after -the-fact confirmations of poor-quality earnings. From an analyst’s perspective, after-the-fact evidence of earnings management is not particularly useful for anticipating issues with earnings quality.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Profit before tax $2.0 billion Non-recurring expenses Acquisition and divesture expenses $0.6 billion Restructuring expenses $0.4 billion Recurring expenses Total operating expenses $2.5 billion Total non-operating expenses $0.5 billion The company’s recurring or core pretax earnings is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because recurring or core pre-tax earnings would be $3.0 billion, which is calculated as follows: Profit before tax + non-recurring expenses = $2.0 billion + acquisitions and divestiture expenses + restructuring expenses = $2.0 billion + $0.6 billion + $0.4 billion = $3.0 billion

questions.question_text: Which of the following best explains why the predictive power of the Beneish model is declining over time?
options: A. The model using accounting numbers is a partial representation of economic
B. Earnings manipulators are aware of the power of the model to screen possible
C. Financial models based on accounting numbers are only capable of establishing
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because earnings manipulators are just as aware as analysts of the power of quantitative models to screen for possible cases of earnings manipulation. The predictive power of the Beneish model is declining over time. Undoubtedly, many managers have learned to test the detectability of earnings manipulation tactics by using the model to anticipate analysts’ perceptions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is most likely to be a warning sign on the quality of financial reports?
options: C. Cash from operations is greater than net income
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because declining receivables turnover could suggest that some revenues are fictitious or recorded prematurely or that the allowance for doubtful accounts is insufficient.

questions.question_text: Earnings most likely show the fastest reversion to the mean if:
options: A. earnings have a significant accruals component and the accrual elements are
B. earnings have a significant accruals component and the accrual elements are
C. earnings are dominated by the cash flow component and the accruals component
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because if earnings have a significant accruals component, it may hasten the earnings’ reversion to the mean, even more so when the accrual elements are outliers relative to the normal amount of accruals in a company’s earnings.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a take-or-pay contract disclosed by a company in the notes to its financial statements: Future purchase obligation payments € 1,00,00,000 Present value of future purchase obligation payments € 80,00,000
options: A. To properly reflect the company's leverage, using constructive capitalization, the
B. adding €8,000,000 to the assets and adding €8,000,000 to the liabilities.
C. adding €10,000,000 to the assets and adding €10,000,000 to the liabilities.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because one common source of off-balance-sheet obligation is purchase contracts, which may be structured as take -or-pay contracts. Analysts typically adjust reported financial statement information by constructively capitalizing, where material, purchase obligations. Constructive capitalization means that the analyst estimates the amount of the obligation as the present value of future purchase obligation payments and then adds the amount of the obligation to the company’s reported assets and liabilities. In this case, the analyst should add €8,000,000 to the company’s reported assets and liabilities.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the Beneish model M-scores below and uses a −1.78 M-score cutoff. Current Year Prior Year Change in M-score Company 1 −2.00 −2.25 0.25 Company 2 −1.50 −1.25 −0.25 Company 3 −2.25 −2.00 −0.25 Based on this information, which company has the highest probability of earnings manipulation?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because Company 2's M-score of −1.50 is higher than −1.78. Higher M - scores (i.e., less negative numbers) indicate an increased probability of earnings manipulation. Therefore, using a −1.78 M-score cutoff, Company 2 shows an increased probability of manipulation. Compared to Company 1 and 3, which have M-scores below the cutoff, Company 2 has the highest probability of earnings manipulation.

questions.question_text: An analyst reviews a company's restructuring charge taken in the current period. The analyst determines similar events occur regularly enough that they should be factored into estimates of permanent earnings. The analyst should attempt to 'normalize' earnings by spreading the restructuring charge over:
options: C. both past periods and the current period.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because when faced with a restructuring charge, an analyst should consider whether similar events occur regularly enough such that they should be factored into estimates of permanent earnings, or whether they should be regarded as one-off items that provi de little information about the future earnings of the remaining activities of the company. If it is the former, then the analyst should attempt to 'normalize' earnings by essentially spreading the current restructuring charge(s) over past periods as well as the current period.

questions.question_text: All else being equal, deferring payments on payables:
options: A. decreases cash flow from operations.
B. does not impact cash flow from operations.
C. increases cash flow from operations.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because cash flow from operations may be increased by deferring payments on payables, accelerating payments from customers, deferring purchases of inventory, and deferring other expenditures related to operations, such as maintenance and research.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about mean reversion in earnings is most accurate? Earnings are more sustainable when the:
options: A. cash flow component equals the accruals component.
B. accruals component dominates the cash flow component.
C. cash flow component dominates the accruals component.
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because earnings are the sum of cash flows and accruals, and they will be more sustainable and persistent when the cash flow component dominates earnings. If earnings have a significant accruals component, it may hasten the earnings’ reversion to the mean, even more so when the accrual elements are outliers relative to the normal amount of accruals in a company’s earnings.

vignettes.module: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques
vignettes.topic: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques - Vignette Questions 1-7
vignettes.case_study_text:
Quentin Abay, CFA, is an analyst for a private equity firm interested in purchasing 
Bickchip Enterprises, a conglomerate. His first task is to determine the trends in ROE 
and the main drivers of the trends using DuPont analysis. To do so he gathers the dat a 
in Exhibit 1. 
 
**Exhibit 1:**
Selected Financial Data for Bickchip Enterprises (€ Thousands) 
  2020 2019 2018 
Revenue 72,448 66,487 55,781 
Earnings before interest and tax 6,270 4,710 3,609 
Earnings before tax 5,101 4,114 3,168 
Net income 4,038 3,345 2,576 
Asset turnover 0.79 0.76 0.68 
Assets/Equity 3.09 3.38 3.43 
 
After conducting the DuPont analysis, Abay believes that his firm could increase the 
ROE without operational changes. Further, Abay thinks that ROE could improve if the 
company divested segments that were generating the lowest returns on capital employed 
(total assets less non-interest-bearing liabilities). Segment EBIT margins in 2020 were 
11 percent for Automation Equipment, 5 percent for Power and Industrial, and 8 percent 
for Medical Equipment. Other relevant segment information is presented in Exhibit 2. 
 
**Exhibit 2:**
Segment Data for Bickchip Enterprises (€ Thousands) 
  
Capital Employed   
Capital Expenditures 
(Excluding Acquisitions) 
Operating Segments 2020 2019 2018   2020 2019 2018 
Automation Equipment 10,705 6,384 5,647   700 743 616 
Power and Industrial 15,805 13,195 12,100   900 849 634 
Medical Equipment 22,870 22,985 22,587   908 824 749 
  49,380 42,564 40,334   2,508 2,416 1,999 
 
Abay is also concerned with earnings quality, so he intends to calculate Bickchip’s cash -
flow-based accruals ratio and the ratio of operating cash flow before interest and taxes 
to operating income. To do so, he prepares the information in Exhibit 3. 
 
**Exhibit 3:**
Earnings Quality Data for Bickchip Enterprises (€ Thousands) 
  2020 2019 2018 
Net income 4,038 3,345 2,576 
Net cash flow provided by (used in) operating activitya 9,822 5,003 3,198 
Net cash flow provided by (used in) investing activity (10,068) (4,315) (5,052) 
Net cash flow provided by (used in) financing activityb (5,792) 1,540 (2,241) 
Average net operating assets 43,192 45,373 40,421 
a includes cash paid for taxes of: (1,930) (1,191) (1,093) 
b includes cash paid for interest of: (1,169) (596) (441)

questions.question_text: Over the three -year period presented in Exhibit 1, Bickchip’s return on equity is best described as:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. The ROE has been trending higher. ROE can be calculated by multiplying (net profit margin) × (asset turnover) × (financial leverage). Net profit margin is net income/sales. In 2018 the net profit margin was 2,576/55,781 = 4.6% and the ROE = 4.6% × 0.68 × 3.43 = 10.8%. Using the same method, ROE was 12.9 percent in 2019 and 13.6 percent in 2020.

questions.question_text: Based on the DuPont analysis, Abay’s belief regarding ROE is most likely based on:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The DuPont analysis shows that profit margins and asset turnover have both increased over the last three years, but leverage has declined. The reduction in leverage offsets a portion of the improvement in profitability and turnover. Thus, ROE would have been higher if leverage had not decreased.

questions.question_text: Based on Abay’s criteria, the business segment best suited for divestiture is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. The Power and Industrial segment has the lowest EBIT margins but uses about 31 percent of the capital employed. Further, Power and Industrial’s proportion of the capital expenditures has increased from 32 percent to 36 percent over the three years. Its capital intensity only looked to get worse, as the segment’s percentage of total capital expenditures was higher than its percentage of total capital in each of the three years. If Abay is considering divesting segments that do not earn sufficient returns on capital employed, this segment is most suitable.

questions.question_text: Bickchip’s cash-flow-based accruals ratio in 2020 is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The cash-flow-based accruals ratio = [NI − (CFO + CFI)]/(Average NOA) = [4,038 − (9,822 − 10,068)]/43,192 = 9.9%.

questions.question_text: The cash-flow-based accruals ratios from 2018 to 2020 indicate:
options: C. no change in earnings quality.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. The cash -flow-based accruals ratio falls from 11.0 percent in 2018 to 5.9 percent in 2019, and then rises to 9.9 percent in 2020. However, the change over the three-year period is a net modest decline, indicating a slight improvement in earnings quality.

questions.question_text: The ratio of operating cash flow before interest and taxes to operating income for Bickchip for 2020 is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Net cash flow provided by (used in) operating activity has to be adjusted for interest and taxes, as necessary, in order to be comparable to operating income (EBIT). Bickchip, reporting under IFRS, chose to classify interest expense as a financing cash flow so the only necessary adjustment is for taxes. The operating cash flow before interest and taxes = 9,822 + 1,930 = 11,752. Dividing this by EBIT of 6,270 yields 1.9.

questions.question_text: Based on the ratios for operating cash flow before interest and taxes to operating income, Abay should conclude that:
options: A. Bickchip’s earnings are backed by cash flow.
B. Bickchip’s earnings are not backed by cash flow.
C. Abay can draw no conclusion due to the changes in the ratios over time.
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Operating cash flow before interest and taxes to operating income rises steadily (not erratically) from 1.2 to 1.3 to 1.9. The ratios over 1.0 and the trend indicate that earnings are supported by cash flow.

vignettes.module: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques
vignettes.topic: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques - Vignette Questions 8-11
vignettes.case_study_text:
Ione Bailey is a financial analyst who is examining the most recent annual report of 
Eastview Inc., a US -based company that complies with US  GAAP. Bailey wants to 
determine Eastview's ROE as well as  the trend in its ROE components by using DuPont 
analysis. Data used for this purpose is presented in Exhibit 1. 
 
Exhibit 1 
  Most Recent Year Previous Year Two Years Ago 
Revenue (USD million)  750 680 600 
Net income (USD million) 60 55 50 
Total liabilities (USD million) 230 220 200 
Total asset turnover ratio 1.30 1.25 1.20 
 
Bailey also wants to evaluate Eastview's three geographic operating segments to assess 
their ROI and growth during the most recent year. For this purpose, she prepares the 
data presented in Exhibit 2 for the most recent year. 
 
Exhibit 2 
  Europe Asia Americas 
Segment EBIT margin % 12.50 13.10 12.80 
Segment % of total assets 26.50 31.20 42.30 
Segment % of total capital expenditures 32.20 29.10 38.70 
 
Next, while reading the notes to the financial statements in the annual report, Bailey 
observes that Eastview's property, plant and equipment have depreciable lives that are 
20% longer than its industry competitors' average. For comparison purposes, 
Bailey adjusts the financial statements to  determine the effect of the difference  on 
the company's financial ratios and information. 
Finally, in order to assess Eastview's accruals and earnings quality, Bailey calculates the 
accruals ratios that are presented in Exhibit 3. 
 
Exhibit 3 
  Most Recent 
Year 
Previous 
Year 
Two Years 
Ago 
Balance-sheet-based accruals ratio (%) –7.90 –3.80 3.10 
Cash-flow-based accruals ratio (%) –4.70 4.40 2.60

questions.question_text: Based on the 3 years of data provided for DuPont analysis, Eastview's financial leverage has been:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because financial leverage can be determined from the information provided as follows: Formula Most recent year Previous year Two years ago Total assets Revenues/Total asset turnover 750/1.30 = 576.92 680/1.25 = 544 600/1.2 = 500 Total shareholders' equity Total assets – Total liabilities 576.92 – 230 = 346.92 544 – 220 = 324 500 – 200 = 300 Financial leverage Total assets/Total shareholders' equity 576.92/346.92 = 1.663 544/324=1.679 500/300 = 1.667 Accordingly, the 3-year trend in financial leverage has been stable at 1.7.

questions.question_text: Based on the segment information, Eastview is growing its business the most in:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the ratio of the total capital expenditures % to the total assets % measures segment business growth. A ratio of less than 1 indicates that the segment is being allocated a lesser proportion of capital expenditures than its proportion of total assets; if a trend develops, the segment will become less significant over time. A ratio of greater than 1 indicates the company is growing the segment; the segment is receiving a 'growth allocation' of capital spending. Accordingly, the segment growth evaluation is as follows: Europe: 32.2% ÷ 26.5% = 1.215 Asia: 29.1% ÷ 31.2% = 0.933 Americas: 38.7% ÷ 42.3% = 0.915 Accordingly, Europe is the geographic segment that is growing fastest.

questions.question_text: After Bailey makes the adjustment for Eastview's treatment of its property, plant and equipment, which of the following will increase?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because the debt -to-equity ratio (Total debt ÷ Total shareholders' equity) will increase. Net income and therefore shareholders' equity will decrease after Bailey makes the adjustment for a greater amount of depreciation expense. There is no effect on total debt.

questions.question_text: Based on the Bailey's calculations, Eastview's balance-sheet-based accruals ratio is:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the percentage trend of the balance-sheet-based accruals ratio is increasing over the 3 -year period. This trend indicates that accruals are an increasing factor in the financial results.

vignettes.module: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques
vignettes.topic: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques - Vignette Questions 12-15
vignettes.case_study_text:
Natalyn Winslow is an analyst working for High Impact Investments (HII), which 
manages a variety of investment funds. She analyzes the recent performance of Dorado 
Beverages SA (DB), a multinational brewer of carbonated beverages that recently 
entered the energy drinks market. As a core holding, DB's prominence in Europe provides 
geographic diversification for HII which tends to be North-America-centric.  
Winslow refers to DB's most recent year and previous year balance sheets  and 
comments: "DB is continuing to  follow a growth -by-acquisition strategy." In addition, 
Winslow adjusts DB's most recent year income statement to determine the company's 
net income under the direct control of management by using information (in EUR millions) 
in Exhibit 1. 
 
Exhibit 1 
  Most Recent Year 
Income from operations 1,765 
Income from investments in associates 191 
Interest expense (648) 
Taxes (238) 
Net income 1,070 
Of which: 
Net income attributable to non-controlling interests 86 
Net income attributable to shareholders 984 
 
Winslow and her team assess the quality of DB's overall performance.  The consensus is 
positive, but Winslow disagrees with her team for the following reasons: 
 
• **Reason 1:**
The rate of fluctuation in the cash-flow-based accruals ratio is low. 
• **Reason 2:**
Balance-sheet-based accruals are trending down relative to total assets. 
• **Reason 3:**
Income from operations is consistently higher than cash generated from 
operations. 
Winslow and her team notice from DB's annual report that the company has four 
segments. They analyze DB's segments by using information (in EUR millions) in Exhibit 
2. 
 
Exhibit 2 
  Most Recent Year Previous Year 
  Sales EBIT Assets Sales 
Carbonated drinks - Europe 2,845 955 12,416 2,758 
Carbonated drinks - North America 1,913 558 5,876 1,721 
Carbonated drinks - Asia Pacific 535 172 2,661 503 
Energy drinks 284 16 1,219 202 
Total 5,577 1,701 22,172 5,184 
 
After performing the segment analysis, Winslow and her team conclude on the following 
statements: 
 
• **Statement 1:**
The sales growth rate in carbonated drinks - North America  is 
stronger than that of energy drinks. 
• **Statement 2:**
Carbonated drinks - Europe's EBIT margin is superior to that 
of carbonated drinks - North America.  
• **Statement 3:**
Asset turnover in carbonated drinks - Europe is better than that of 
DB overall.

questions.question_text: Which of the following balance sheet line items is most appropriate to justify Winslow's comment about DB's growth strategy?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because goodwill arises when a company acquires another business. Goodwill and intangible assets are hallmarks of a growth-by-acquisition strategy.

questions.question_text: After Winslow's adjustment, DB's net income (in EUR millions) under management's direct control is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because it excludes the income from investments in associates which is not under DB's direct control (in EUR millions): Net income 1,070 Less: Income from investments in associates (191) 879 Including the net investments and returns of associates with the full reported value of Nestlé’s own assets and income would introduce noise into the analytical signals. The returns earned by associates are not under the direct control of Nestlé’s management.

questions.question_text: Which of the following reasons best supports Winslow's disagreement with her team about DB's overall performance?
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because the quality of income from operations is considered better when it is backed by cash flow. In this case, cash generated from operations is not fully backing income from operations, thereby calling the quality of income from operations into question. If the cash flow generated by operations was significantly or consistently less than operating profit, one would have reason to be suspicious about the quality of the operating profit.

questions.question_text: With respect to the segment analysis, which of the following statements is correct?
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because per the table below (in EUR millions except where indicated), carbonated drinks - Europe's EBIT margin is higher than, and thus superior to that of carbonated drinks - North America. Carbonated drinks - Europe Carbonated drinks - North America Analysis EBIT (A) 955 558 --- Sales (B) 2,845 1,913 --- EBIT margin % (A/B) 33.60% 29.20% Carbonated drinks - Europe higher

vignettes.module: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques
vignettes.topic: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques - Vignette Questions 16-19
vignettes.case_study_text:
Claus Petersen, a pension fund equity analyst, is preparing an analysis of Rhine AG for 
the upcoming quarterly fund meeting. Rhine is a Germany -based manufacturer that 
operates three distinct divisions: children’s products (infant car seats, strollers, cri bs, 
etc.), recreational products (bicycles, bicycle trailers, etc.), and home furnishings 
(contemporary furniture). All three divisions sell through retail outlets around the world. 
 
The company has been pursuing an aggressive growth strategy, achieved through both 
foreign acquisitions and organic growth. Petersen is interested in determining how well 
Rhine is allocating its resources between the three divisions and the effects of the 
foreign acquisitions on overall performance. Exhibit 1 summarizes selected divisional and 
corporate data for the current year and the prior year. 
 
**Exhibit 1:**
Rhine AG Selected Divisional and Corporate Data (€ millions) 
  Total for Three 
Divisions 
Children's 
Products 
Recreational 
Products 
Home 
Furnishings 
  Current 
Year 
Prior 
Year 
Current 
Year 
Prior 
Year 
Current 
Year 
Prior 
Year 
Current 
Year 
Prior 
Year 
Revenues 2,837.1 2,775.5 1,176.2 1,236.2 1,034.1 930.0 626.8 609.3 
Gross profit 621.4 640.8 296.6 337.6 246.0 220.3 78.8 82.9 
Operating 
profit 172.7 219.4 64.7 115.7 72.9 62.2 35.1 41.5 
Earnings 
before taxes

Total assets 2,498.0 2,479.5 1,270.9 1,249.6 961.5 948.5 265.6 281.4 
Capital 
expenditures 32.7 42.3 22.1 30.0 6.7 8.6 3.9 3.7

of capital

Proportion 
of total 
assets 
100% 100% 50.9% 50.4% 38.5% 38.3% 10.6% 11.3% 
 
Petersen’s preferred method to determine which division is becoming less significant 
over time is to review the relationship between capital expenditures and total assets by 
operating division. He plans to base his conclusion on the assumption that the current 
year's investment behavior is representative of future investment patterns. 
 
Petersen knows that revenues in the children’s products division have suffered because 
of declining birth rates in Europe and North America, but he believes that if Rhine can 
maintain the operating margin for this division then overall company profitability should 
not be affected. 
 
Corinna Berg, another analyst with the fund, reminds Petersen that during the current 
year, the US dollar weakened against the euro by 4% and that 50% of the sales in the 
recreational products division are sold in the United States. 
 
Petersen recalls that some of the recent global expansion was aimed at establishing 
operations in Ireland because its statutory corporate tax rate is lower than the German 
rate of 29.8%. If Petersen assumes that other tax credits were the same in the current 
year as the prior year, he can analyze changes in Rhine’s effective tax rate to determine 
whether the geographic mix of the company’s profits has changed in the current year. 
 
Petersen finally examines the company’s liquidity ratios, which are shown in Exhibit 2. 
Even though the company’s current and quick ratio have improved, his interpretation of 
the changes in the company’s cash conversion cycle is that the company’s liquidity position 
has deteriorated. 
 
**Exhibit 2:**
Rhine AG Selected Ratios 
Ratio Current Year Prior Year 2 Years Prior 
Current ratio 2.31 2.17 1.16 
Quick ratio 1.06 0.89 0.53 
Accounts receivable turnover 5.82 6.08 6.11 
Inventory turnover 3.78 3.91 4.09 
Accounts payable turnover 5.71 5.78 5.60 
Cash conversion cycle 95 days 90 days 84 days 
 
Worried that the balance sheet–based and cash flow–based accruals ratios (not shown) 
raise some concerns about the possible use of accruals to manage earnings, Petersen asks 
Berg for advice on what further type of analysis he should do as a follow-up on this issue.

questions.question_text: Using Petersen’s preferred method and current year divisional data, the best conclusion Peterson can make about which division will potentially become less significant in the future is that it will be:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. Petersen prefers to use the relationship between capital expenditures and total assets by operating division and thus would use the ratio of capital expenditure proportion to total asset proportion for each division. This ratio for the recreational produ cts division is less than 1 (see table), indicating that Rhine is allocating a lower proportion of capital expenditures to that division relative to asset proportions. If this trend continues, the recreational products division will become less significant over time. Children’s Products Recreational Products Home Furnishings Total for the Three Divisions Proportion of capital expenditures (Exhibit 1) 67.60% 20.50% 11.90% 100.00% Proportion of total assets (Exhibit 1) 50.90% 38.50% 10.60% 100.00% Ratio: Proportion of capital expenditures to proportion of total assets 1.3 0.5 1.1 1.0

questions.question_text: If the children’s products division had been able to maintain its prior year operating margin in the current year, the company’s overall operating margin in current year, compared to the prior year, would have been:
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Apply the prior year operating margin for the children’s products division to the current year revenues for the division to determine what the current year overall operating profit margin would have been if the margin had been maintained, and then compare it with the prior year overall operating profit margin. Operating margin in children’s products in prior year = Operating profit/revenues 115.7/1,236.2 9.40% Apply the 9.4% margin to current year revenues for division 0.094 × 1,176.2 110.6 Increase in current year operating profit for division, had the margin been maintained 110.6 – 64.7 45.9 Revised current year company operating profit with increase in children’s products operating profit 172.7 + 45.9 218.6 Revised current year company operating margin 218.6/2,837.1 7.70% Prior year company operating margin 219.4/2,775.5 7.90% Even if the children’s products division had maintained its operating margin in the current year, the overall company operating margin would still have decreased slightly (7.7% versus 7.9%).

questions.question_text: Compared with 2 years prior, the change in which working capital account most likely had the largest effect on Petersen’s observed deterioration in liquidity?
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. Petersen interprets that the changes in the cash conversion cycle (CCC) indicate a deterioration in liquidity. The CCC has increased since 2 years prior, from 84 days to 95 days (see the following table). The working capital account that had the largest effect on the increase was inventory because the holding period has increased 6.4 days. Current Year Prior Year Working Capital Account Turnover Days (365/Turnover) Turnover Days Accounts receivable 5.82x 62.70 6.11x 59.80 Inventory 3.78x 96.60 4.09x 89.20 Accounts payable 5.71x 63.90 5.6x 65.20 Cash conversion cycle* 95.40 83.80 * CCC = Days in sales + Days in inventory – Days in payables.

questions.question_text: Berg’s best answer to Petersen’s question about further analysis is that he should conduct a:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. Concerns about earnings manipulation are best addressed by cash flow ratios, such as operating cash flow before interest and taxes to operating income.

vignettes.module: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques
vignettes.topic: Integration of Financial Statement Analysis Techniques - Standalone Practice Questions
vignettes.case_study_text: The following are standalone practice questions for this learning module.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company (in € millions): One-off income from associates and joint ventures 10 One-off revaluation loss 4 Recurring yearly provisions for restructuring activities 3 Profit before tax 30 The adjusted profit before tax that best reflects the company's core performance (in € millions) is:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because the analyst is interested in the ongoing operations of the company unaffected by non-repeating types of gains or losses (such as One - off income from associates and joint ventures and One-off revaluation loss). Accordingly, the Adjusted profit before tax of core operations = Profit before tax – One-off income from associates and joint ventures + One-off revaluation loss = 30 – 10 + 4 = 24. Recurring yearly provisions for restructuring activities are already reflected in Profit before tax and no adjustment is needed. Provisions involve decisions by management, they recur regularly, and they affect the returns to shareholders. In assessing the company’s prospects, these charges are important variables that should not to be ignored.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information (in € millions) about a company: EBIT 80 Cash paid for taxes 17 Cash paid for interest 26 Net operating cash flows 97 The company classifies its interest expenses as financing cash flows. The ratio of operating cash flow before interest and taxes to EBIT is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because net operating cash flows has to be adjusted for interest and taxes in order to be comparable to EBIT. As the company classifies interest expenses as financing cash flows, the only necessary adjustment is for taxes. The operating cash flow before interest and taxes = 97 + 17 = 114. Dividing this by EBIT of 80 yields 1.425 ≈ 1.4.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company: Most Recent Year Prior Year Balance sheet accruals ratio 9.80% 1.30% Cash flow accruals ratio –1.40% –6.70% Which of the company's accruals ratios have improved?
options: A. Cash flow accruals ratio only
B. Balance sheet accruals ratio only
C. Both the cash flow accruals ratio and the balance sheet accruals ratio
questions.official_explanation: A is correct. because when examining accruals ratios, it would be a concern if the absolute levels of the accruals ratio were high and if they were consistently trending higher. The cash flow accruals ratio improved from –6.7% to –1.4% with a lower absolute value, while the balance sheet accruals ratio deteriorated from 1.3% to 9.8% with a higher absolute value.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information about a company's financial reporting quality: Current Year Prior Year Cash-flow-based accruals ratio –1% –3% Balance-sheet-based accruals ratio 1% 3% Which of the above ratios shows an improvement?
options: C. Both cash-flow-based accruals and balance-sheet-based accruals ratios
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because both balance -sheet-based and cash-flow-based accruals are insignificant and they are improving in absolute levels from 3% in the prior year to 1% in the current year. The cash-flow-based accruals are negative and increasing but it is their absolute level that is relevant in this case. It would be a concern if the absolute levels of the accruals ratio were high; even more worrisome would have been if they were consistently trending higher which is not the case here.

questions.question_text: Which of the following situations most likely requires a company to restate its prior year financial statements?
options: A. The cash-flow-based accruals ratio is exceptionally high
B. A significant amount of income is sourced from investments in associates
C. Changed accounting standards alter the calculation of benefit plan obligations
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because a company is required to restate its prior year financial statements for changed accounting standards that alter the calculation of benefit obligations. The analyst identifies the beginning of year adjustments and revises the end of last year year -end balances accordingly. Therefore, when there is an accounting standards change, prior year financial statements have to be restated by the company to improve comparability across years, and the analyst should take this into account.

questions.question_text: Top Tech Company (TTC) reports its only investment in associates, an interest in First Computer Company (FCC), in its financial statements (in $ millions) as follows: Market Value Earnings TTC (including investment in associate) 2,50,000 15,000 FCC 35,000 1,200 The P/E ratio of TTC excluding the investment in associates is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because remove the earnings of associates from the earnings of the combined entity to make a price-to-earnings comparison for parent company earnings alone. The market value of TTC excluding the investment in associates = 250,000 – 35,000 = 215,000, while earnings of TTC excluding the investment in associates = 15,000 – 1,200 = 13,800. Therefore, the P/E ratio of TTC excluding the investment in associates = Market value of TTC (exclude the investment in associates)/Earnings of TTC (exclude the investment in associates) = 215,000/13,800 ≈ 15.58.

questions.question_text: An analyst gathers the following information (in $ millions) about a company: Total sales 95,000 Income from associates 1,050 Operating profit 9,550 Impairment of goodwill 1,860 Total provisions for non-recurring items 850 The operating profit margin excluding associates and all unusual charges is closest to:
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because profit adjusted for income from associates and all unusual charges = 9,550 – 1,050 + 1,860 + 850 = 11,210. Therefore, the net profit margin excluding associates and all unusual charges = 11,210/Total sales = 11,210/95,000 = 11.8%.

questions.question_text: A financial analyst is preparing an investment recommendation report on a public company. To prepare the report, she analyzes the company’s financial statements following a framework comprising the following phases: (1) Define purpose of analysis, (2) Collect input data, (3) Process input data, (4) Analyze/Interpret data, (5) Develop and communicate recommendations, (6) Follow -up. Which of the following is most likely an output of phase 3?
options: B. List of questions to answer
questions.official_explanation: C is correct. because adjusted financial statements is the result of processing financial data. After collecting the financial data (phase 2), the analyst processes the data (phase (3), making the adjustments necessary to leave the data ready for analysis.

questions.question_text: A one-time restructuring charge for a pretax amount of €120 million is included in a company's financial statements. An analyst considers this restructuring charge to be unusual. She compiles the following information (in € millions) for the most recent year in order to exclude the one-time charge from an ROE calculation: Shareholders’ equity at beginning of the year 2,500.0 Shareholders’ equity at end of the year 2,646.0 Average shareholders’ equity 2,573.0 Net income 257.3 If the income tax rate is 25%, adjusted ROE would be closest to:
questions.official_explanation: B is correct. because net income (numerator of ROE) and average shareholders’ equity (denominator of ROE) should be adjusted as follows: Initial figures Charge Adjust. Tax Adjust. Adjusted figures Shareholders’ equity at beginning of the year (€ millions) (A) 2,500.00 - - 2,500.00 Shareholders’ equity at end of the year (€ millions) (B) 2,646.00 +120 -30 2,736.00 Average Shareholders’ equity (€ millions) (C)=[(A)+(B)]/2 2,573.00 2,618.00 Net income (€ millions) (D) 257.30 +120 -30 347.30 ROE (E)=(D)/(C) 10.00% 13.30%
